channel.title: Alpha Funnel — Investing
channel.description: Daily Investing briefings from Alpha Funnel.

item.title: The broad tape was weak and Alphabet is carving a bear flag into Wednesday, but the neocloud names exploded higher on a wall of new contracts, and oil round-tripped from over $90 to under $80 in a single session
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: IREN jumped 16 percent on $2.8 billion of fresh AI-cloud deals while the sector printed one signing after another. Oil crashed below $80 on a reported Iran ceasefire proposal, gold is stuck in a two-sided fight, and the whole market is holding its breath for one earnings print in 48 hours.
item.content:encoded:
IREN jumped 16 percent on $2.8 billion of fresh AI-cloud deals while the sector printed one signing after another. Oil crashed below $80 on a reported Iran ceasefire proposal, gold is stuck in a two-sided fight, and the whole market is holding its breath for one earnings print in 48 hours.

A Monday of crosscurrents. The index went nowhere, the compute-infrastructure names went vertical, and the war that pushed oil over $90 this morning pushed it back under $80 by the afternoon. Here is the session recap, then five stories.

Session recap. The broad tape was heavy but the AI-infrastructure trade was on fire underneath it. Per the Market list, the neoclouds bounced hard on a run of new deals while Alphabet carved a weekly bear flag into Wednesday's earnings and Oracle sat in its largest drawdown in more than 25 years. The war whipsawed the whole session, with oil crashing below $80 on a reported Iranian ceasefire proposal after trading over $90 in the morning, gasoline back over $4 at the pump, and SpaceX printing a new all-time low, per the Polymarket list. Volatility sat calm near 17.65, Jim Cramer declared tech simply too hard, and everything is now queued behind the Alphabet print.

1. The neocloud names exploded on a wall of new contracts. The trade of the day was compute infrastructure. Per the Market list, IREN jumped as much as 16 percent after signing $2.8 billion of new multi-year AI-cloud contracts and raising its year-end run-rate target above $4 billion, with 85 percent of that already under contract, customers prepaying roughly 45 percent of the GPU cost, and $7.6 billion of cash worth about 60 percent of its market value.

It was not one name, it was the whole sector. Per the same tape, Hut 8 landed a fifteen-year $9.8 billion deal, CleanSpark signed its first high-performance-computing deal at $6.6 billion, Nebius booked $1 billion with Reflection AI on top of 684 percent revenue growth, and TeraWulf inked a roughly $19 billion agreement with Anthropic, while BlackRock led $12 billion of financing for Meta's Texas data centers. The read-through, per the Stocks list, is that the capex-collapse fear that drove last week's selloff keeps running into signed contracts, and the customers are paying up front.

2. Oil round-tripped in a day and gold is fighting itself. The macro whipsaw was in commodities. Per the Polymarket list, oil crashed below $80 after Iran reportedly proposed a ten-day ceasefire to revive an interim deal, a violent reversal from the morning's move above $90, even as CENTCOM completed its ninth straight night of strikes and the Houthis declared a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia. A war being fought and de-escalated in the same day is producing a fuel price that cannot decide what it is.

Gold is running its own split-screen. Per VirtualBacon, gold has broken its 40-week average at 4,500 and entered a technical bear market with downside eventually below 3,000, pressured by the same commodity margin-call selling. Yet per Lark Davis, China's central bank bought 480,000 ounces in June, its biggest purchase since 2023 and its twentieth straight month of buying, stepping into an 11.6 percent price drop. The chartists see a breakdown and the largest strategic buyer sees a discount, and only one of them is trading for the next quarter.

3. Alphabet on Wednesday is still the referee, and it just showed its hand on chips. The print the whole week hinges on is 48 hours out. Per the Market list, Alphabet is developing a new in-house chip called Frozen V2 that reportedly runs its Gemini models up to ten times more efficiently than its latest processors, a bullish signal for its own compute even as the stock carves a bear flag into the report.

The number hanging over it is the bill. Per the Polymarket list, Goldman Sachs warned that the $5.3 trillion of AI spending planned by Meta, Microsoft, Amazon, and Alphabet through 2030 could start to pressure the bond market, the macro version of the same question the neocloud deals keep answering with a yes. Alphabet's capital-spending guide on Wednesday is where the market finds out whether that $5.3 trillion is a promise or a warning.

4. The memory bull got a huge buyback number, and the bottleneck may be power. The single-name memory story got sharper. Per the Polymarket list, UBS expects Micron to generate more than $400 billion of free cash flow through 2028 and to repurchase over 40 percent of its shares once its buyback restriction expires in December 2026, though the desks still flag SK Hynix as the cleaner way to own the same cycle, per the People list.

The constraint underneath the chips is electricity. Per this breakdown, Navitas makes the gallium-nitride and silicon-carbide power chips that feed Nvidia's move to 800-volt, one-megawatt racks, with about $450 million of design wins as data-center power demand heads past 1,000 terawatt-hours by the end of the decade. It is unprofitable and a design win is not an order, but the theme is real, that the AI trade is running into a physical wall of power and heat, and the same New Mexico permit fight that just hit Bloom Energy is the proof.

5. Crypto is stuck in a box while the contrarians circle Ethereum. The majors are going nowhere. Per the Crypto list, Mike Novogratz says Bitcoin is stuck between $65,000 and $85,000 and needs both the CLARITY Act to pass and the Fed to start cutting to break higher, with one of those two legs currently broken, while stablecoin supply fell another $1.24 billion and public companies added just 101.5 Bitcoin last week.

The bolder call is on the most hated major. Per Lark Davis, Ethereum is down about 65 percent with its weekly momentum reading at the third-lowest level ever recorded, a setup he compares to the June 2022 low rather than the final bottom, and the Ether-to-Bitcoin ratio just printed a bullish crossover that last preceded a 132 percent stretch of outperformance. The catalyst he points to is Robinhood Chain, where bridged Ether jumped seventyfold in a week, so the thesis is that the rotation into Ethereum simply has not happened yet. Those are one promoter's indicators, so weigh them as a setup and not a certainty, but universal hatred of an asset is at least the right precondition.

Single names to track.

  • $IREN. Jumped 16 percent on $2.8 billion of new AI-cloud contracts, run-rate target above $4 billion with 85 percent under contract and customers prepaying 45 percent of GPU cost, per the Market list.
  • $GOOGL (Alphabet). Reports Wednesday as the referee for the AI-capex debate, carving a bear flag but developing a Frozen V2 chip said to run Gemini ten times more efficiently, per the Market list.
  • $MU (Micron). UBS sees more than $400 billion of free cash flow through 2028 and a buyback of over 40 percent of shares once the restriction lifts, per the Polymarket list.
  • $NVTS (Navitas). The power-semi bottleneck play feeding Nvidia's 800-volt racks, roughly $450 million of design wins but still unprofitable, per this breakdown.
  • ETH (Ethereum). Down 65 percent with the third-lowest weekly momentum reading ever and a bullish ratio crossover, the contrarian setup, per Lark Davis.

Psychology. Watch how fast the crowd flips. This morning the story was oil over $90 breaking the disinflation trade, and by the afternoon oil was under $80 on a ceasefire headline and the neoclouds were up double digits, the same market swinging between panic and greed inside a single session. When the tape moves that violently on headlines, the temptation is to trade every swing, and the cost of doing that is usually your patience and your capital both.

The tell of the moment is the irony nobody is naming out loud. Two weeks ago the market sold every AI stock because a cheap Chinese model supposedly proved the buildout was overbuilt, and this weekend that same model, Kimi K3, had to pause its subscriptions because it ran out of compute, per the Market list. The bear case and the bull case were the same fact read in opposite directions, and the neocloud deals signed today are the market quietly admitting which reading was right. Michael Burry's line that 95 percent of investors have no idea what they own fits a tape where IREN and its peers just proved the demand while their stocks were still being sold as broken.

So the discipline into Alphabet on Wednesday is to let the print settle the argument instead of front-running it with leverage. The neocloud contracts and the Micron cash-flow math say the demand is real and paid for, but Jim Cramer calling tech too hard and oil whipping $10 in a day say the price is going to keep lurching regardless. Own the names whose customers are prepaying, respect that a single earnings report can override every chart on your screen, and keep enough cash that the next violent swing is your entry rather than your undoing.

item.title: The whole argument about whether AI spending is about to collapse gets settled on Wednesday when Alphabet reports, and everything trading this week is really a bet on that one number
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Alphabet's capital-spending guide is the referee for a memory complex down 20 percent and a market 2 percent from its record. Korea's stock crisis got worse overnight, oil pushed back over $90, and SpaceX just got a $900 price target the same week its share count starts to explode.
item.content:encoded:
Alphabet's capital-spending guide is the referee for a memory complex down 20 percent and a market 2 percent from its record. Korea's stock crisis got worse overnight, oil pushed back over $90, and SpaceX just got a $900 price target the same week its share count starts to explode.

A Monday that is really a countdown to Wednesday. The market spent last week deciding the AI trade might be broken, and the company most able to prove or disprove that reports in 48 hours. Here is the pre-open picture, then five stories.

Pre-open preview. Futures are flattish into a heavy week. The S&P sits about 2 percent below its early-June record while the Philadelphia semiconductor index is more than 20 percent off its late-June high yet still up 60 percent on the year, per the Market list. More than 80 S&P companies report this week with Alphabet on Wednesday the clear catalyst, Tesla and Intel later in the stretch, and the Fed meeting at month-end. Overnight the trouble was abroad, with Korea's market down another 4 percent, oil back over $90, and gold struggling near $4,000 after its worst week in six, per the Financial Markets list. SpaceX flies its rescheduled Starship test Thursday, not today.

1. Alphabet on Wednesday is the referee for the entire AI-capex fear. The single print that matters is a capital-spending number. Per the Market list, Alphabet reports Wednesday as the first hyperscaler read of the season, and a pullback in its AI capex guidance would ripple straight through the memory and neocloud names that just sold off, while a confident guide would tell the market the buildout is intact.

The fundamentals give the bulls a strong hand going in. Per the Stocks list, Alphabet trades near 22 times forward earnings as the cheapest of the Magnificent Seven, its cloud revenue grew 63 percent to $20 billion with the backlog doubled to $462 billion, and its own chips now sell to Anthropic and Meta. The whole week is positioned around this one report, so the risk is not the number itself but how crowded the bet on it has become.

2. The memory bull case has a cleaner name than Micron, and it is SK Hynix. The trade beneath the AI trade is high-bandwidth memory, and the leader is Korean. Per the People list, Nvidia cannot walk away from SK Hynix because it supplies 70 percent of the memory going into the Vera Rubin platform on co-designed chips, holding 56 percent of the market to Micron's 23, and on Shay Boloor's growth-adjusted screen SK Hynix is the cheapest name in the group at a fraction of Micron's multiple.

The structural case underneath is a cash-flow inversion. Per the Crypto list, Gavin Baker notes that Nvidia, Micron, Broadcom, and Applied Materials are on pace for roughly $430 billion of combined free cash flow over the next year, more than triple two years ago, even as the five hyperscalers' free cash flow falls from around $250 billion in 2024 toward $100 billion by the end of 2026 under an estimated $1.8 trillion of AI spending. The money is visibly moving from the spenders to the suppliers, and Micron itself sits down 30 percent with its memory sold out through 2027, per the Market list.

3. SpaceX got a $900 target the same week its float starts to flood. The most-owned new stock got a bullish number with a catch. Per Lark Davis, Citi set a $900 price target on SpaceX, but the timing matters more than the target, because the public float jumps from about 5 percent now to 60 percent by November as pre-IPO lockups expire, and toward 100 percent by mid-2027.

That is a tsunami of supply regardless of fair value. His framing is the right one, that you do not want to be the exit liquidity for the venture investors who are about to be freed to sell, so the discipline is to let the lockup selling exhaust itself before reaching for Citi's target. The stock is already down about 45 percent from its highs, per the Market list, and the wall of new shares is the reason a great company can still be a bad stock for a few more months.

4. Oil is back over $90 and it is the quiet threat to the whole tape. The move that could break the calm is in crude. Per the People list, Brent jumped back over $90 a barrel as the Iran war entered its tenth day with the Strait of Hormuz still blocked, and gold sold off hard into the strength, posting its biggest weekly drop in six.

The read-through is that the disinflation trade the Fed leaned on is getting more expensive by the week. One rotation call making the rounds is to be long oil from the seventies, move out of semis and software into cyclical financials and healthcare, and stay defensive through the illiquid summer, per the Crypto list. Whether or not you make that exact trade, a crude benchmark over $90 into a Fed meeting is the variable that can override even a good Alphabet print.

5. Crypto is holding its levels while the structural bears sharpen their case. The majors are steady into the week. Per the Crypto list, Bitcoin is back over $65,000 and Ether is pinned to the $2,000 pivot that decides its next move, a close above pointing higher and a loss of it opening a sweep of the lows.

The bearish argument getting louder is Chamath Palihapitiya's. Per the People list, he lays out two structural headwinds for Bitcoin, that speculative liquidity now prefers prediction markets and equities, and that the energy going into Bitcoin mining is worth ten to twenty times more if it is reallocated to running AI models. That is a real long-term question about whether the marginal buyer and the marginal watt both have somewhere better to go, and it is the kind of thesis that matters more the longer the price stays stuck.

Single names to track.

  • $GOOGL (Alphabet). Reports Wednesday as the referee for the AI-capex debate, cheapest Magnificent Seven name near 22 times forward with cloud up 63 percent and a $462 billion backlog, per the Stocks list.
  • SK Hynix. The cleanest memory name, supplying 70 percent of Vera Rubin's high-bandwidth memory at 56 percent market share and the lowest growth-adjusted multiple in the group, per the People list.
  • $MU (Micron). Down 30 percent with memory sold out through 2027, bouncing off its falling-wedge lows on Friday with levels stacked from 806 to 906, per the Market list and Stocks list.
  • $SPCX (SpaceX). Carries a fresh Citi $900 target but faces a float going from 5 to 60 percent by November, down 45 percent from its highs, per Lark Davis.
  • BTC (Bitcoin). Back over $65,000 against Chamath's two structural headwinds of liquidity and energy competition, per the Crypto list.

Psychology. Look east before you look at your own screen. Korea is running the same AI-chip mania the US is, and its market volatility just hit the highest level in its history, worse than the financial crisis, with the index down 30 percent from a record it set only last month, per the Financial Markets list. That is what a crowded, levered version of this exact trade looks like when it unwinds, and it is happening in real time to people running the same thesis you are.

The tell of the moment is how eager the crowd is to call the bottom anyway. The dip-buyers are naming Micron's floor to the dollar and Bitcoin's low to the level, the promoters are quoting Citi's $900 on SpaceX while ignoring the 60 percent float unlock behind it, and everyone is treating Friday's bounce as the all-clear into a week that hinges on a single earnings print. Confidence is cheap the weekend after a scare, and it is usually most abundant right before the scare's second leg.

So the discipline into Alphabet on Wednesday is to hold your size and your patience. The memory demand is real and sold out through 2027, but the cleanest way to own it is the supplier with the lowest multiple, not the leveraged expression that round-trips on a headline. Let Alphabet print before you decide the AI trade is fixed, respect that Korea is showing you the downside of crowding, and keep enough cash that oil over $90 or a soft capex guide is your opportunity rather than your problem. The market wants you certain this morning. The move is to stay liquid and let Wednesday do the talking.

item.title: Micron was worth more than Berkshire Hathaway on Wednesday and had shed nearly half a trillion dollars by Friday, and the crash needed no thesis to break, just a rate hike in Seoul and a cheap model out of China
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The memory trade round-tripped an entire euphoria in a single week, and the round-trip is the lesson. The demand behind Micron is still measured in gigawatts and sold out through 2027, but the leverage stacked on top of it is what showed you what it does on a bad headline.
item.content:encoded:
The memory trade round-tripped an entire euphoria in a single week, and the round-trip is the lesson. The demand behind Micron is still measured in gigawatts and sold out through 2027, but the leverage stacked on top of it is what showed you what it does on a bad headline.

This was the week the biggest trade in the market got a real test, and it was not a test of the story. It was a test of how much leverage the crowd had piled onto a story that was mostly right. Here is the arc, the bull and bear cases, and what to do with it.

The arc of the week. On Tuesday the memory blowoff was still going, with Micron briefly worth more than Berkshire Hathaway and the crowd calling for $2,000, while SanDisk printed the highest weekly relative-strength reading ever recorded on any stock. By Wednesday it had round-tripped, with Micron breaking below $900 in a single session and the money that left the chips going straight into Apple.

Then the cause arrived, and it was not the AI thesis cracking. Per Jose Najarro, the Bank of Korea raised its base rate 25 basis points to 2.75 percent, its first hike in three and a half years, which lifted the borrowing cost on the Korean-financed fabs that SK Hynix and Samsung are building, sent the KOSPI down 7 percent, and dragged the whole memory complex with it.

The key detail is that the hike does not touch the demand curve for high-bandwidth memory or the fabs Micron builds with US money, so the sharpest fall was in the names with the local rate exposure, not the ones with the best long-run position. By Friday the momentum unwind had turned historic, with Micron having lost $459 billion of market value in fourteen trading days, per the Polymarket list.

The final shove came from an unrelated direction. A cheap Chinese model, Kimi K3, topped the coding benchmarks and set off a fear that AI spending would slow, and the memory names took the hit even though, as the AI memo this week lays out, the model is not actually the cost bomb the tape treated it as. Two exogenous shocks, a Seoul rate decision and a Beijing model release, took apart a trade that had nothing to do with either.

The bull case, stated cleanly. The demand is real and it is contracted. Per Morgan Stanley by way of Shay Boloor, Micron's gross margins could reach 90 percent in 2027, supported by sixteen supply agreements with floor pricing and high-bandwidth memory that is sold out through 2027, with the HBM4 for Nvidia's Vera Rubin platform already shipping since March at roughly twice the ramp pace of the prior generation. The scale behind it is not a slogan, with data-center capacity set to add more than 100 gigawatts by 2030 against over $5 trillion of total spending.

The confirming tell is the calmest company in the chain. Per Najarro, ASML raised its four-year guidance and said its lithography capacity grows 30 percent next year and another 30 percent after, with 2027 already sold out, the bottleneck that stretches the cycle rather than ending it. When the pick-and-shovel supplier at the very top of the chain is booked three years forward while the crowd panics over a quarter-point hike in Korea, the panic is the opportunity and not the signal.

The bear case, taken seriously. The round-trip itself is the warning. A stock that is worth more than Berkshire on Tuesday and below $900 on Wednesday is not being valued, it is being traded on leverage, and the leverage is the problem. The selloff was a momentum-factor unwind from the 100th percentile of positioning, the worst such stretch in 27 years, which means the buyers at the top were mechanical and forced, and mechanical buyers become mechanical sellers the instant volatility turns.

The sharper worry is who is short and what they know. Michael Burry turned bearish on Micron in early July and the stock fell 18 percent, per Stocks with Josh, and the professionals are already shorting the next Chinese memory IPO before it even lists. Per threadguy, CXMT, described as China's SK Hynix, goes public July 27 and is already trading as a pre-IPO perpetual at an implied $470 billion against an $80 billion offering, and the two largest positions are both professional shorts. When the smart money is short the shiny new memory name before its first trade, the supply side of this cycle is not the sure thing the bulls make it sound.

What to do with it. The level is the referee, not your conviction. Per Stocks with Josh, Micron needs to reclaim and hold $861 to confirm the bottom and $805 is the line that breaks it, so let the price prove the thesis rather than catching the exact low on a Monday. If you want the memory cycle, the cleaner expressions are the ones the leverage cannot round-trip, ASML booked solid through 2027, or SK Hynix at 7.6 times forward earnings against Micron's 11.7 if you want the direct exposure a third cheaper.

The discipline is to own the bottleneck and avoid the leverage. The 2x memory funds and the CXMT pre-IPO perps are the instruments that just showed you what they do on a bad headline, and the semiconductor earnings landing this week are the referee for the whole argument. Keep the theme, which stays intact, and refuse the leverage, because the two are not the same thing.

Psychology. Read the week as one emotional round-trip. On Tuesday the crowd owned Micron above Berkshire and was calling for $2,000, and that is euphoria wearing the costume of conviction, and by Wednesday the same crowd was liquidated below $900 on a Korean rate hike it could not have named the day before. Nothing about the AI story changed in those 48 hours. What changed is that the leverage got a reason to unwind, and once it started, the 2x ETFs and the pre-IPO perps did the rest, carrying out the very people who felt most certain at the top.

The tell of the week is who stayed calm and who got carried out. ASML, the company that actually controls the supply, raised its guidance and said 2027 is sold out, while the leveraged holders got liquidated on 25 basis points and the smart money was already short the next Chinese memory IPO before it lists. The people closest to the real economics were the least rattled, and the people furthest out on borrowed exposure were the ones getting stopped out at the lows.

So the discipline check into the new week is to ask yourself which side of that you were on. If the round-trip in Micron made you feel like the AI trade is broken, you were positioned in the leverage, not the theme, because the theme did not move. Separate the two before Monday. Let Micron prove it can hold $861 and let the chip earnings print before you decide the panic was the top or the gift, and own the sold-out bottleneck rather than the 2x expression that round-trips on a headline out of Seoul. The story was right all week. The only question the market actually asked was how much you had borrowed to believe it.

item.title: IBM just had the worst day in its hundred-year history by warning that its customers are moving money out of software and into AI hardware, and that is the bull case for the exact chips that got crushed this week
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: IBM pre-announced a big revenue miss and lost a quarter of its value, and the reason is not that AI spending is slowing, it is that it is shifting. Alphabet, Intel, and AMD all report or present this week into a tape where the smart money is buying the neocloud dip and calling the selloff a speed crash, not a top.
item.content:encoded:
IBM pre-announced a big revenue miss and lost a quarter of its value, and the reason is not that AI spending is slowing, it is that it is shifting. Alphabet, Intel, and AMD all report or present this week into a tape where the smart money is buying the neocloud dip and calling the selloff a speed crash, not a top.

A quiet Sunday with a loud read-through. The single most useful data point of the weekend is a legacy tech giant blowing up, because of what it says about where the money is going. Here is the week ahead, then five stories.

Week ahead. This is the week the AI-capex debate gets refereed by earnings. Per Jose Najarro, Alphabet reports Wednesday as the first hyperscaler read on capex and cloud backlog, Intel reports Thursday after the close, and AMD holds an AI event Thursday morning, all landing while the memory and neocloud names sit deep in a selloff, with CoreWeave down 15 percent and Nebius down 16 over five days. The technical backdrop is fragile, since the S&P fund opened and closed below 745 on Friday after needing to hold 755 for a new high, a breakdown by the chart, per Stocks with Josh. Korea reopens Monday after a leverage blowup and SpaceX flies its rescheduled Starship the same day.

1. IBM's crash is the clearest tell of the week, and it points at hardware. The number that reframes everything is a warning. Per Joseph Hogue, IBM pre-announced quarterly revenue of $17.2 billion, well below the Street, and the stock fell more than 25 percent for the largest single-day drop in its hundred-year history, wiping out roughly $70 billion, because customers told it they are shifting spend away from traditional software and toward AI infrastructure like chips, memory, and servers.

The read is that the money is moving, not disappearing, and that is bullish for the hardware that just sold off. Hogue's basket splits into the near-term hardware bounce, naming Broadcom, Micron, Marvell, and Arm, and the longer-term software winners that impose AI cost discipline, naming Cloudflare, ServiceNow, Datadog, and Palantir. It is a promotional channel with an options-course upsell, so weigh the picks accordingly, but the underlying signal is real and it comes straight from an enterprise buyer's own guidance.

2. The neocloud dip is the trade the informed money is actually buying. The most contrarian move of the weekend is buying the compute landlords into the fear. Per a Nebius breakdown, Nebius sold off as much as 17 percent in a session on reports Meta will rent out its own excess compute, yet Meta is Nebius's largest customer with a five-year deal worth up to $27 billion, and every new data center it builds is sold out before completion against a US compute demand Goldman sees more than doubling to 66 gigawatts next year.

The structural case is that the cost of building has exploded. Per a neocloud panel, the price of a one-gigawatt buildout jumped from roughly $50 billion to $100 billion in three months, which means only the largest neoclouds and hyperscalers can afford new capacity, and Nvidia is now backstopping the financing by guaranteeing minimum revenue so lenders will fund the clusters. That makes Nvidia both the landlord and the bank, a powerful moat and also the concentration risk the bears will point at when the custom-silicon projects from OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google mature.

3. The best macro read on the selloff calls it a speed crash rather than a top. The framing worth internalizing came from a serious voice. Per Jordi Visser, the Morgan Stanley tech-momentum factor fell about 40 percent in 18 days, the worst on record back to 1998, but he reads it as a momentum unwind driven by AI agents and volatility-control selling rather than a business-cycle break, the same forced mechanics that gave gold its biggest three-day move in 35 years and knocked Bitcoin down 40 percent.

The reason he stays bullish is the earnings underneath it. Of the first 43 S&P names to report, sales beat by 4 percent and earnings by 16, with JPMorgan surprising by 34 percent and both big banks at all-time highs while credit spreads sit near record tights, and that is not what a real top looks like. Visser bought back Micron on the thesis that AI equals memory, and rotated some proceeds into Bitcoin, Ether, and silver, and the distinction he draws from 2007 is the one that matters, that spreads are not widening and the economy is not rolling over, so this is rotation rather than systemic risk.

4. Crypto is building a bottom in the low fifties while the institutional wave forms behind it. Three independent reads converge on the same floor. Per an altcoin breakdown, the downside target for Bitcoin is roughly $53,000 with a bull flip near $82,600, and per the Milk Road weekend note, the on-chain confluence of realized price and long-term-holder cost sits at $50,000 to $53,000, the level where buying has historically paid.

The bigger story is who arrives next. Per Matt Hougan of Bitwise, the baton of Bitcoin's largest buyer has passed from retail to GBTC to Strategy and is about to reach institutional capital, the final boss, with the tell being Vanguard, an $11 trillion manager that refused crypto for years and just posted a job for a head of digital assets. The parallel bet is Ethereum as the Wall Street rail, where former BlackRock executives argue its real competitor is banks' legacy plumbing, not Solana, per that discussion. These are all promoters talking their book, so treat the timing as hope rather than fact, but the direction of institutional adoption is the one thing the bears cannot easily argue away.

Single names to track.

  • $MU (Micron). Needs to reclaim and hold $861 after closing at $848, the level the technicians and Jordi Visser are both watching as the memory bottom, per Stocks with Josh.
  • $NBIS (Nebius). Sold off 17 percent on the Meta-rents-compute fear despite Meta being its largest customer on a deal worth up to $27 billion, with capacity sold out before completion, per this breakdown.
  • $NVDA (Nvidia). The sovereign-AI angle, running its open Nemotron models inside Palantir for air-gapped government agencies, a moat that diversifies demand beyond the hyperscalers, per this read.
  • $SOUN (SoundHound). The model-independent voice-AI toll booth, revenue growing from $46 million in 2023 to $169 million in 2025 with Casey's now on 2,600 locations, though still unprofitable, per this breakdown.
  • $RDW (Redwire). The in-space manufacturing play at $335 million of 2025 revenue, with its roll-out solar arrays on the ISS and microgravity fiber, dependent on government timing, per this breakdown.

Psychology. Read the weekend against Seoul. The clearest emotional lesson is not on Wall Street, it is in Korea, where retail borrowed to chase the same semiconductor mania and roughly one in every thirty working-age Koreans got liquidated on the pullback, tripping the market's eighth circuit breaker of the year. That is what leverage into a crowded top actually does, and it is the same trade in a more degenerate form than the one the US crowd is running.

The tell of where we are is that the buy-the-dip reflex is already loud. Najarro is rotating from low-beta into high-beta neoclouds, the promoters are calling Bitcoin's bottom to the dollar, and the margin data flagged this weekend shows leverage at a level reached only twice before, ahead of the tech bubble and ahead of 2007. Buying weakness can be right and being early can still get you liquidated, and the Korean tape is the reminder that the second half of that sentence is the one that hurts.

So the discipline into a heavy earnings week is to size for the speed crash Visser describes rather than the recovery you hope for. IBM just told you the money is moving into hardware, and that is a reason to own the bottleneck rather than a reason to lever into it. Let Alphabet and Intel print, let Micron prove it can hold $861, and keep enough dry powder that the next forced-selling wave is your entry instead of your margin call. The crowd is buying the dip with borrowed money again, and Korea just showed you how that ends when the timing is wrong.

item.title: The calendar says the dangerous stretch is just ahead, oil quietly climbed 25 percent while nobody hedged it, and the memory bulls just put a hundred billion dollars of contracts on the table
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Benjamin Cowen lays out why August and September are where midterm years break, and the trigger he names is the exact semiconductor group everyone just bought back. Crude is up sharply into a huge earnings week, and Micron disclosed the numbers that turn its bull case from a forecast into a backlog.
item.content:encoded:
Benjamin Cowen lays out why August and September are where midterm years break, and the trigger he names is the exact semiconductor group everyone just bought back. Crude is up sharply into a huge earnings week, and Micron disclosed the numbers that turn its bull case from a forecast into a backlog.

A quiet Sunday with a loud calendar. The market is coming off a violent week feeling like the worst is behind it, right as the seasonal map and the oil tape both say the opposite. Here is the week ahead, then four stories.

Week ahead. This is the week the mega-caps report into a tape that just had a scare. Alphabet, Tesla, Intel, and ServiceNow all land between Monday and Friday, with the S&P up 8.8 percent on the year, the Nasdaq 100 up 13.4, and the semiconductor index still up roughly 80 percent despite the selloff, per the People list. The two things that can break the calm are the earnings themselves and oil, which climbed 16 to 25 percent over two weeks while the war in the Gulf ground on, per the Market list. Korea reopens Monday after missing the whole memory move, and SpaceX flies its rescheduled Starship test the same day.

1. The seasonal map says the danger is August and September, and the trigger is the chips everyone just bought. The week-ahead warning worth internalizing is a calendar pattern. Per Benjamin Cowen, midterm years tend to see a shallow June dip, a July rally, then a real correction of 10 to 20 percent in August or September, and he has the receipts, with 2022 falling 19 percent from a mid-August top, 2018 falling 20 percent from mid-September, and 2014 falling about 10 percent from a September high.

The uncomfortable part is what he thinks sets it off. Cowen guesses the trigger is the semiconductor group getting elevated again, and that is exactly the trade the crowd rushed back into on Friday's reversal, and he ties the same window to Bitcoin's four-year cycle bottoming alongside a late-summer stock correction. This is a probabilistic pattern and not a prophecy, but it is a reason to treat a strong bounce into August as a chance to raise cash rather than a green light to add leverage.

2. The memory bull case turned from a forecast into a backlog. The single most important upgrade to the Micron story is that the numbers are now contracted, not projected. Per the Crypto list, Morgan Stanley's aggressive call has Micron's gross margin reaching 90 percent in 2027 and revenue nearly doubling toward $286 billion, but the harder detail is that Micron disclosed 16 strategic customer agreements, 14 of them carrying roughly $100 billion of minimum contracted revenue through 2030 with $22 billion of upfront deposits already in hand.

The bear has a specific answer, and it is not that the demand is fake. Per the People list, Dan Niles argues the money is rotating out of Micron because SK Hynix trades 20 to 30 percent cheaper and got its high-bandwidth memory to market first, and his warning is the honest one, that the AI buildout money looks infinite but money is not infinite. So the memory question for the week is no longer whether the orders exist, it is whether you pay up for Micron's backlog or buy the same cycle a third cheaper in Korea.

3. Oil is the tail risk the market keeps ranking third. The move nobody is hedging is in crude. Per the Market list, oil is up 16 to 25 percent in two weeks to the high $80s, US petroleum inventories have drained 184 million barrels since the war began, and traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has fallen to 14 crossings a day from 34 in early July, yet the crowd keeps treating it as background noise.

The reason it stays background is a threshold. The framing making the rounds is that the market ranks its worries as AI first, the Fed second, and Iran third, and that oil only actually breaks equities if it sustains above $100 to $110 a barrel. That is probably right, and it is also exactly the kind of consensus that gets tested, because a benchmark up 25 percent with inventories draining and a chokepoint at 40 percent of normal flow is one headline away from the level that stops being ignorable. Cheap oil is what gave the Fed its cover, and that cover is quietly getting more expensive by the week.

4. Anthropic became the name the whole AI trade leans on, and it is not even public. The most striking claim of the weekend is about a private company. Per the People list, Brad Gerstner says Anthropic's revenue on its Opus 4.8 model went parabolic and lit the fuse under the entire semiconductor rally, calling it the single most important thing in the market, while Raoul Pal describes the ramp as the fastest ever recorded.

Those are promotional, hyperbolic voices and the numbers are unverified, so weigh them as narrative rather than fact. But the structural point survives the hype, that the public chip trade is now underwritten by the revenue of a private model lab, which means the thing holding up Micron and the semis is a company retail cannot own and whose numbers it cannot check. When the load-bearing wall of a trade is invisible, the risk is that you find out how strong it was only after it moves.

Single names to track.

  • $MU (Micron). The bull case is now a backlog, with 16 customer agreements and roughly $100 billion of contracted revenue through 2030 against $22 billion of deposits, though Dan Niles prefers SK Hynix at a 20 to 30 percent discount, per the Crypto list and People list.
  • $GOOGL (Alphabet). Reports this week as the cheapest Magnificent Seven name near 22 times forward earnings, the referee for the whole tape, per the People list.
  • BTC (Bitcoin). Near $64,000 with 30-day volatility priced near the lows and long-term holders at a record 79 percent of supply, the setup for the long-volatility trade if oil breaks, per the Crypto list.

Psychology. Read the weekend mood against the calendar. The crowd is coming off Friday's reversal feeling vindicated, buying back the semis and calling for Alphabet, and doing it in the exact weeks that Cowen shows are where midterm years break, with the exact group he names as the trigger. That is not a coincidence you want to be on the wrong side of, because the emotional signature of a top is not fear, it is the relief that follows a scare, and this weekend is soaked in relief.

The tell of complacency is what the crowd chooses not to look at. Oil is up 25 percent with a chokepoint running at 40 percent of normal and the market has filed it under third place, behind two things it finds more interesting, and that is exactly how a tail risk stays cheap right up until it is not. Meanwhile the whole memory trade is leaning on the unverifiable revenue of a private lab, and the bull case everyone cites is a set of contracts nobody outside Micron has audited.

So the discipline into the new week is to treat the bounce as a gift to get disciplined, not a reason to get greedy. Let Alphabet and the chip names print real numbers before you decide the scare is over, respect that the seasonal window Cowen maps is opening rather than closing, and keep enough cash that an oil headline or a soft hyperscaler guide is an opportunity instead of a margin call. The market handed you back your confidence this weekend. The move is to spend it on caution.

item.title: The chip selloff made it feel like a crash, but outside of tech nearly every index is sitting near a record high, and the smart money spent the week buying the dip into a huge earnings stretch
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The Nasdaq is off about 7 percent from its peak while the equal-weight index barely moved, which reframes the week as a tech unwind rather than a market break. Alphabet, Tesla, and ServiceNow all report in the days ahead, robotics stocks are the new speculation, and a consortium digital dollar is quietly rewiring how US debt gets financed.
item.content:encoded:
The Nasdaq is off about 7 percent from its peak while the equal-weight index barely moved, which reframes the week as a tech unwind rather than a market break. Alphabet, Tesla, and ServiceNow all report in the days ahead, robotics stocks are the new speculation, and a consortium digital dollar is quietly rewiring how US debt gets financed.

The tape that scared everyone on Friday looks different from a Saturday distance. The damage is real but narrow, and the coming week is when the fundamentals get a vote. Here is the week ahead, then five stories.

Week ahead. This is the week the earnings do the talking. Per the Market list, the Nasdaq sits about 7.2 percent off its high while the equal-weight S&P is down barely 1.4 percent and the broad index is only about 2 percent from a record, so the selloff was a tech-momentum unwind, not a market collapse. Alphabet, Tesla, Intel, and ServiceNow all report over the coming days, per the Stocks list, with Alphabet the clear swing factor. Only one of the 23 largest US companies carries an overbought reading, and it is Apple, which tells you where the crowd already ran. Korea's market reopens Monday after missing the whole move, and the memory names spend the week trying to prove Friday was a bottom.

1. The people who manage money bought the dip, and Alphabet is the name they bought. The weekend's clearest signal is accumulation, not panic. Per a Milk Road paid update, their analysts added Alphabet near $372 during the selloff on the case that it is the cheapest of the Magnificent Seven at about 22 times forward earnings while owning the entire AI stack, from the Gemini model to its own chips to distribution.

The fundamentals under that bet are strong on the numbers they cite. Per the Stocks list, Alphabet's cloud revenue grew 63 percent to $20 billion with its backlog doubling to $462 billion, its custom chips now sell to Anthropic and Meta, and Apple is reportedly paying roughly a billion dollars a year to use Gemini in the new Siri. This is a promotional source talking its own book, so treat the entry price as marketing, but Alphabet reporting into a beaten-down tape is the single most important print of the week.

2. Robotics became the new speculation, and one name is the cleanest way to play it. The rotation out of memory is looking for its next story, and this weekend it was humanoids. Per Milk Road, the pitch is that Nvidia and Micron are the safe picks-and-shovels, with a humanoid robot carrying roughly ten times the memory of a self-driving car, while the pure play is Agility Robotics, going public through the Churchill Capital shell to trade as AGLT.

The specifics are what set Agility apart from the vaporware. Its Digit robot has logged more than 65,000 real-world hours across nine customers including Toyota, holds over $300 million in multi-year contracts, and runs a roughly 75 percent US-based supply chain, which the host contrasts with Tesla's still-undeployed Optimus. The watchlist behind it is XPeng, Hyundai as the owner of Boston Dynamics, and the parts makers Schaeffler and Harmonic Drive. This is a heavily promoted pick from a subscription funnel, so the enthusiasm is the product, but a SPAC-listed humanoid with real contracts is a genuine thing to track rather than a meme.

3. The memory bull case got sharper even as the stocks bled. The stock everyone is fighting over is still Micron. Per the People list, Morgan Stanley argues Micron's gross margins could reach 90 percent in 2027, backed by 16 supply agreements with floor pricing and high-bandwidth memory that is sold out through 2027, with the HBM4 for Nvidia's Vera Rubin already shipping since March.

The comparison worth holding is against the Korean leader. Per the Stocks list roundup, SK Hynix owns about 56 percent of the high-bandwidth memory market to Micron's 23 percent and locked a Nvidia contract covering 70 percent of the memory going into Vera Rubin, yet it trades at 7.6 times forward earnings against Micron's 11.7, a third cheaper. Micron's counterweight is US policy, with CHIPS Act grants funding a plan to build 40 percent of American DRAM at home. The whole data-center buildout adds more than 100 gigawatts of capacity and over $5 trillion of spending by 2030, so the demand is not the question, the entry price is.

4. A consortium digital dollar and a crypto law are quietly rewiring the plumbing. The most consequential crypto story is not a token, it is legislation and a stablecoin. Per an a16z op-ed by former Congressman Patrick McHenry, the CLARITY Act moving through Congress is the most important technology law since the 1996 Telecommunications Act, the first non-crisis financial framework since the late 1990s, and a16z has a direct interest in it passing.

The market mechanism underneath it is the part to watch. Per Lark Davis, a consortium of BlackRock, Google, Visa, and many others is launching a digital dollar whose deposits, under the GENIUS Act, must sit in US Treasuries, so every coin issued becomes automatic demand for government debt against a $40 trillion balance and more than a trillion in annual interest. His specific roster and the coin's name are unverified and the framing is promotional, so treat the details loosely, but the direction is real, that stablecoins are becoming a debt-financing tool dressed as a payment upgrade.

5. Bitcoin is range-bound and the Trump memecoin is the weekend's cautionary tale. The crypto majors are stuck. Per the Crypto list, Mike Novogratz sees Bitcoin trapped between $60,000 and $80,000 for the rest of the year absent a real catalyst, long-term holders now sit on a record 79 percent of supply, and the four-year-cycle crowd still points at an October bottom.

The lesson of the week came from a token with a name attached. Per Lark Davis, Trump's memecoin has fallen more than 90 percent from its peak, leaving retail buyers with an estimated $4 billion in losses while Trump reportedly booked over a billion, the same asset producing opposite outcomes depending on who got in when. Those figures are the creator's estimates rather than audited, so hold them at arm's length, but the shape of it is the oldest one in the market, insiders distributing to a crowd that arrived late.

Single names to track.

  • $GOOGL (Alphabet). Bought on the dip near $372 as the cheapest Magnificent Seven name at about 22 times forward earnings, reporting this week with cloud up 63 percent and a $462 billion backlog, per Milk Road and the Stocks list.
  • $MU (Micron). Gross margins seen reaching 90 percent by 2027 on 16 floor-priced supply deals with memory sold out through 2027, up 197 percent on the year, per the People list.
  • AGLT (Agility Robotics). Going public via the Churchill Capital shell, with its Digit robot logging 65,000 hours across nine customers and over $300 million in contracts, per Milk Road.
  • $GLXY (Galaxy Digital). Fell 9 percent on Friday but landed a $500 million credit line and a Russell 1000 spot, the dual crypto and data-center play, per the Milk Road update.
  • BTC (Bitcoin). Range-bound between $60,000 and $80,000 with long-term holders at a record 79 percent of supply and the cycle low modeled for October, per the Crypto list.

Psychology. The weekend is a study in how a narrow move feels like a broad one. The Nasdaq is off 7 percent and it felt like the end of the world, while the equal-weight index barely flinched and Apple is the only mega-cap that is actually overbought, which means the pain was concentrated in exactly the crowded momentum names that had run the furthest. Per the Market list, a lot of the selling was mechanical, vol-targeting funds cutting exposure by formula as volatility spiked, not investors deciding the story was broken.

The tell of a top is not a selloff, it is the froth that survives one. This weekend brought a wave of new leveraged single-theme AI funds filed for launch, a robotics SPAC being pitched as the next Micron, and a presidential memecoin that cost retail an estimated $4 billion, and all of that speculation is still very much alive. When the leveraged product filings and the meme launches keep coming through a 7 percent drawdown, the excess has not been cleared, it has just been repriced.

So the discipline into earnings week is to separate the accumulation from the animal spirits. Buying Alphabet at 22 times forward earnings into a beaten tape is a different act than chasing a humanoid SPAC or a memecoin, even though both are called buying the dip. Let Alphabet and the memory names print real numbers before you decide Friday was the bottom, own the businesses whose demand is measured in gigawatts and supply agreements, and remember that the mechanical selling that felt like conviction can reverse just as mechanically the moment the volatility fades.

item.title: A private company just teamed up with Jack Dorsey to make a run at PayPal, and the real target is not PayPal, it is Visa and Mastercard
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Stripe, Block, and the buyout firm Advent put roughly $53 billion on the table for PayPal, and the argument is that combining 600 million accounts with stablecoin rails could finally build a third payments network. Meanwhile the memory selloff looked for a floor, and the loudest investors are warning that the AI spending itself is the risk.
item.content:encoded:
Stripe, Block, and the buyout firm Advent put roughly $53 billion on the table for PayPal, and the argument is that combining 600 million accounts with stablecoin rails could finally build a third payments network. Meanwhile the memory selloff looked for a floor, and the loudest investors are warning that the AI spending itself is the risk.

A quiet Saturday with one very loud deal and a market still digesting the worst week for chips in over a year. Here is the week ahead, then four stories.

Week ahead. The setup into Monday is a market trying to decide whether Friday's reversal was a bottom or a bounce. The memory complex spent Friday testing its lows and clawing back, big-tech earnings begin to land over the coming week with Alphabet first in line, and Korea's market reopens Monday after its holiday having missed the whole selloff. The through-line from the weekend's smartest voices is that the danger is no longer whether AI demand is real, it is whether the spending to meet it has become a furnace that burns cash faster than it returns it, a theme that runs straight through the PayPal deal and the chip tape alike.

1. Stripe, Block, and Advent are bidding for PayPal, and the prize is the payments duopoly. The weekend's biggest deal is an ambush on an incumbent. Per the All-In Podcast, Stripe, Jack Dorsey's Block, and the private-equity firm Advent are bidding about $53 billion, near $60 a share, for PayPal, with Block contributing $17 billion of equity, and the market expects the final price to land closer to $70.

The bull case, as Chamath Palihapitiya laid it out, is that this is the only combination that could actually challenge Visa and Mastercard. Stitch together PayPal's roughly 400 million accounts plus Venmo and Braintree, Block's Cash App, and Stripe's stablecoin rails from its $1 billion Bridge acquisition, and you get the scale to drive payment costs toward zero across a real third network. The skeptic in the room was David Sacks, who called PayPal a legacy product growing only about 7 percent a year, the same checkout it invented 27 years ago, so the deal only works if an AI-native operator can rebuild a business that has drifted for two decades.

2. The pattern under the deal is AI-native operators buying stale digital businesses. The framing worth keeping is bigger than one target. Per that discussion, David Friedberg called the PayPal bid the second dot on a line after Ryan Cohen's run at eBay, a wave of AI-first operators buying mature, founder-less, digital-native businesses that never captured their own AI potential.

The template he pointed to is the Italian roll-up firm Bending Spoons, which bought AOL, Vimeo, WeTransfer, and Evernote and runs them with young AI-first executives to cut waste and print cash. If that pattern holds, the takeaway for a portfolio is that the stale, cheap, cash-generating names the market wrote off as dead money become acquisition targets, and the operator matters more than the asset. Chamath even teased that the one person who should look hardest at a rival PayPal bid is Elon Musk, whose fingerprints are on the original.

3. The memory selloff hunted for a bottom, and the smart money is split on Micron. The single name at the center of the chip wreck is Micron. Per Stocks with Josh, Micron dipped to its only real support near $800 to $805 on Friday, bounced as high as $905, then faded to close at $848, so the level to reclaim and hold is $861 and the line that breaks the setup is $805.

The reason it matters is that two respected names are on opposite sides. Michael Burry turned bearish on Micron about two weeks ago and the stock fell 18 percent, while Bank of America upgraded the memory group on the argument that AI hardware spending is not slowing. That disagreement is the whole memory trade in one stock, and the tell to watch is whether the buyers who paid up for call options at the ask on Friday are early or trapped. This is the same money-burning-furnace question from a different angle, whether the capex that fills Micron's order book keeps flowing or finally pauses.

4. Ethereum got a roadmap and crypto got a liquidity warning, in the same weekend. The one genuinely new crypto item is a protocol plan. Per Lark Davis, Vitalik Buterin unveiled a roadmap called Lean Ethereum built on three pillars, quantum resistance, privacy, and long-term simplification, with the quantum piece framed as a problem to solve now rather than later. There are no numbers attached and the clip is promotional, so it is a direction rather than a catalyst.

The sober counterpoint is that there is very little money actually moving. Per threadguy, on-chain liquidity has thinned to the point where he estimates only $10 million to $50 million is really rotating, and that thinness is why the memecoin drama produces headlines without producing gains. A roadmap for the next 50 years is worth little in a week when the tape has no fresh buyers, and the honest read is that Ethereum's upgrade is a story for later, not for Monday.

Single names to track.

  • $PYPL (PayPal). The target of a roughly $53 billion bid from Stripe, Block, and Advent near $60 a share, with the market expecting closer to $70, per the All-In Podcast.
  • $MU (Micron). Held its $800 support and bounced to $905 before fading to $848, with Michael Burry short and Bank of America upgrading the group, per Stocks with Josh.
  • $NFLX (Netflix). Bought on the dip near its post-earnings low and bounced, though the structural bear case is that it cannot match YouTube's open creator model, per Stocks with Josh and threadguy.

Psychology. The weekend gave you two versions of the same fear. The PayPal deal is a bet that a 27-year-old business can be revived, and the memory selloff is a bet that a red-hot one can be broken, and both come down to whether you trust the spending behind the story. Per the All-In crew, the warning is that enterprise token spending is climbing so fast that a public-company finance chief is going to miss a quarter on it, and that is the first time the AI trade has been framed as a cost problem rather than a demand one.

The tell of the moment is disagreement among people who are usually right. Michael Burry is short Micron while Bank of America upgrades it, one side of the room calls PayPal obsolete while the other calls it a Visa killer, and Vitalik is planning for 2076 while there is barely $50 million moving on-chain today. When the sharpest investors split this cleanly, the honest move is to admit the outcome is not knowable from here.

So the discipline into a fresh week is to let the levels referee the argument rather than your conviction. Let Micron prove it can hold $861 before you call the memory bottom, let the PayPal price settle before you decide whether $53 billion was a floor or a ceiling, and treat the capex-burn warning as the real variable to track, because the day the spending pauses is the day both the deal math and the chip math change at once. Patience is cheap this weekend, and it is the only edge on offer.

item.title: The worst week for chips in over a year ended with a violent reversal, and with Apple retaking the crown from Nvidia by doing the one thing that now looks smart, refusing to spend
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The Philadelphia semiconductor index fell 12.5 percent on the week and Micron dropped below a trillion dollars before the tape ripped back green into the close. A cheap Chinese model became the excuse for the selloff, Wall Street rotated hard into tokenization, and the crypto crowd is loudly split on whether $57,000 was the bottom.
item.content:encoded:
The Philadelphia semiconductor index fell 12.5 percent on the week and Micron dropped below a trillion dollars before the tape ripped back green into the close. A cheap Chinese model became the excuse for the selloff, Wall Street rotated hard into tokenization, and the crypto crowd is loudly split on whether $57,000 was the bottom.

The morning looked like the top was in. By the close it looked like a shakeout. Both readings drew on the same week, and the gap between them is the whole story tonight. Here is the session recap, then five stories, then the single names.

Session recap. Friday was a rout that reversed. Per the Market list, the Philadelphia semiconductor index fell 12.5 percent on the week for its worst stretch in more than a year, and Micron closed below a trillion dollars for the first time in six weeks after shedding more than $400 billion since late June, more than Qualcomm and Marvell combined, per the Barron's recap.

Then the tape turned. The market opened deep red with the semis down three and a half percent, and by early afternoon the Dow had erased its losses and gone green while the memory names bounced double digits off the lows, per the Financial Markets list. Apple ended the day as the most valuable company in the world again at $4.871 trillion, edging Nvidia's $4.837 trillion by falling half a percent on a day Nvidia fell nearly four, per the People list. Netflix sank 11 percent on its print and SpaceX fell below its IPO price as a Starship test aborted, now rescheduled for Monday.

1. The thing that cracked the market was a cheap Chinese model, and the panic outran the math. The trigger had a name. Per threadguy, Moonshot's new Kimi K3 hit number one on the front-end coding arena and carried a headline price of roughly 50 cents per million tokens against something like $56 for the top American model, and that number is what set off the capex-domino fear, that if US firms lean on cheap Chinese models the spending on advanced chips slows.

That fear is what took the memory complex apart, and it is also what reversed. Per the Market list, hedge funds spent the afternoon calling the bottom and buying the dip, and the DRAM fund that tracks Micron, SK Hynix, and Samsung bounced 12 percent off its lows after taking in $10 billion of new cash during the drawdown. The panic priced a demand collapse that the actual product, as the AI channel lays out tonight, does not yet justify. When the whole tape moves on one leaked price and then takes half of it back by the close, the move was positioning, not proof.

2. Apple won the day by being the company that refuses to play the spending game. The leadership flip is the tell of the whole rotation. Per the People list, Apple retook the title of most valuable company not by rising but by falling the least, and HSBC upgraded it to a buy with a $366 target, the market rewarding the one megacap that is not pouring hundreds of billions into AI infrastructure.

The logic threadguy named is that if intelligence is becoming a cheap commodity, the winner is whoever owns the device it plugs into, per that stream. That is the exact inverse of the trade that led all year, when the capex spenders were the leaders and Apple was the laggard for underinvesting. On a day the market got scared that the spending might not pay off, it ran to the one name that never made the bet.

3. While Wall Street sold AI, the plumbing of a fully digital market quietly went live. The rotation had somewhere to go, and it was tokenization. Per Blockworks, the Depository Trust and Clearing Corporation, which holds more than $114 trillion in securities, ran its first live production trades of tokenized stocks and Treasuries on Wednesday, with JPMorgan, Vanguard, BlackRock, and the NYSE among more than thirty firms tokenizing real assets like the Invesco QQQ, Microsoft shares, and Circle stock.

This is the institutional version of the crypto trade, and it is why the rotation names are the picks and shovels of on-chain finance. The full service does not launch until October and rests on a temporary regulatory letter, so it is a rollout and not a finished system, but the direction is set. Per Arbitrage Andy, the money leaving crowded AI semis is rotating toward Chainlink, Ondo, and Aave, the tokenization rails, and even the retail version of the trade, per a ValueInvesting post, is a list of non-AI compounders like Mercado Libre, Uber, and Booking rather than another chip.

4. Galaxy Digital is the cleanest way to see the AI selloff and the crypto rotation collide. One stock sits on both sides of the trade. Per Mike Novogratz on Prof G Markets, his Galaxy Digital is building a Texas data center campus called Helios with 1.6 gigawatts approved and another gigawatt in the queue, which he frames as roughly $75 billion of contracted capex and potentially over $100 billion fully built, alongside a crypto-trading business he guided to $90 to $100 million of quarterly profit.

The catalyst is close. Per a Milk Road breakdown, Galaxy reports earnings at the end of the month, its 800 megawatts already contracted to CoreWeave imply about a billion dollars of revenue at 90 percent margins, and the analyst pegs fair value near $57 against a stock around $24. That analyst holds it as a fifth of his portfolio and both sources are the same promotional house, so weigh the target accordingly, but the structure is real, a crypto balance sheet stapled to a data-center landlord at the exact moment both trades are in play.

5. The crypto crowd is loudly split on whether the bottom is already in. Two credible framings point in opposite directions. Per the Benjamin Cowen cycle memo, Bitcoin near $65,000 is most likely not at its low yet, with his day-count model pointing at an October bottom, August and September negative in all three prior midterm years, and the bull case confirmed only on two weekly closes above the 50-week average near $86,500.

The other side says the bottom already printed. Per Lark Davis, whales bought a claimed 270,000 Bitcoin near $59,000 in what he calls the largest on-chain accumulation spike ever, long-term-holder metrics sit at 2018 and 2022 bottom levels, and the June wave of record ETF outflows is already flipping back to inflows. That clip is heavy with paid-group promotion, so treat the specific figures as his, but the disagreement itself is the signal. Novogratz splits the difference, calling a $60,000 to $80,000 range for the rest of the year and noting the speculative crowd has left crypto for sports betting, zero-day options, and Korean chip stocks, per that interview.

Single names to track.

  • $AAPL (Apple). Retook the most-valuable-company title at $4.871 trillion by falling half a percent while Nvidia fell four, upgraded to buy at HSBC with a $366 target, per the People list.
  • $MU (Micron). Closed below a trillion dollars for the first time in six weeks, down more than $400 billion since late June, then bounced with the group into the close, per the Barron's recap.
  • GLXY (Galaxy Digital). The dual crypto-and-data-center play, Helios at 1.6 gigawatts approved with 800 megawatts contracted to CoreWeave, earnings at month-end, per Milk Road.
  • $ARM (Arm Holdings). Record royalty revenue near $2.6 billion, up 21 percent, with version-nine chips carrying double the royalty rate of version eight across 350 billion cumulative devices, per this breakdown.
  • $NOW (ServiceNow). Subscription revenue near $3.7 billion, up 22 percent, with its Now Assist agents embedded across the workflow layer, per this breakdown.
  • Solstice. The Honeywell materials spinoff is buying Element Solutions for $10 in cash plus half a share, a roughly $24 billion combination funded with a $4.5 billion bridge that the analysts call a reason for caution, per this review.

Psychology. The market handed you whiplash and called it a verdict. The Philadelphia chip index fell 12.5 percent on the week and Micron lost a trillion-dollar handle, and then hedge funds bought the afternoon and the Dow closed green, which means the same session that felt like the top to a seller at the open felt like a gift to a buyer at two o'clock. The lesson is that a move built on one leaked model price and a wall of forced momentum selling is a move that can reverse in an afternoon, and it did.

The tell of the day is where the crowd ran when it got scared. It ran to Apple, the company that spent the least, and it ran to tokenization, the trade with a catalyst rather than a crash, while it abandoned the memory names it worshipped a month ago. Per Novogratz, the speculative money has already left for sports betting and zero-day options, and equity leverage is at a record high above the 2007 and 1999 peaks, the condition that makes both the crash and the snapback so violent.

So the discipline into next week is to refuse the single-day story in either direction. The Cowen memo says the Bitcoin low is probably still two months out while Lark Davis says whales already bought it at $59,000, and both cannot be right, so let the level prove itself rather than picking a side on conviction. The chips are not worth 12 percent less because of one Chinese model, and they were not worth the June highs either. Own the businesses whose demand is real, let the leverage finish washing out, and remember that the tape that scared you at the open paid the buyer who waited for the close.

item.title: Taiwan Semiconductor told everyone the demand runs to 2030 and the chips sold off anyway, because this is a momentum unwind and momentum does not read earnings
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: TSMC raised its spending and guided growth above 30 percent with visibility into 2030, yet Micron has shed $459 billion in fourteen days and the tech-momentum factor just had its worst stretch in 27 years. Netflix capitulated after the close, SpaceX broke its IPO price as a Starship test scrubbed, and Apple quietly printed a record high.
item.content:encoded:
TSMC raised its spending and guided growth above 30 percent with visibility into 2030, yet Micron has shed $459 billion in fourteen days and the tech-momentum factor just had its worst stretch in 27 years. Netflix capitulated after the close, SpaceX broke its IPO price as a Starship test scrubbed, and Apple quietly printed a record high.

Yesterday the lesson was that beating every number does not save a stock in a momentum unwind. This morning Taiwan Semiconductor made the case even harder to ignore, and the tape still is not listening. Here is the pre-open picture, then five stories.

Pre-open preview. This is a Friday opex, and the setup is capitulation meeting good fundamentals. Per the Stocks list, TSMC raised its 2026 capital budget to a range of $52 to $56 billion and guided revenue growth above 30 percent, and chips fell anyway, with the tech-momentum factor down 35.9 percent over 17 days, its worst in the index's 27-year history, per the Financial Markets list.

Netflix reported after Thursday's close and washed out near $68, Apple printed a new all-time high as the best Magnificent Seven name of the year, and SpaceX closed below its $135 IPO price at $131.11 while a Starship test scrubbed on the pad. Korea's market was closed, Japan fell about 4 percent overnight, oil is firmer on the sixth straight night of US strikes on Iran, and June inflation came in cool.

1. TSMC gave the AI bulls their cleanest talking point in weeks, and the market walked right past it. The reframe is that the most disciplined company in the chain just extended the runway. Per Jose Najarro's read of the call, TSMC lifted its full-year growth guide toward the 40 percent range, raised capex about $10 billion, and said demand visibility now runs clearly into 2028, 2029, and 2030, while deliberately declining to overbuild the way it did in past cycles. The bull case is that rationing supply makes the cycle last longer rather than blowing off. Treat the exact figures as a viewer's recap of the call rather than the filing.

The problem is that none of that is what moved prices. The same session saw TSMC down 2 percent, Nvidia down 2 percent, Broadcom and Intel off more than 5, and Micron down 6, per the Stocks list. Micron alone has now shed $459 billion in market value in fourteen trading days despite an 80 percent operating margin and its entire 2026 high-bandwidth memory output already committed to Nvidia, per the Polymarket list. When the best fundamental news of the month lands on a stock down six percent, you are not in a fundamentals tape.

2. Netflix capitulated into its own print, and that is the setup traders are circling. The most-watched washout of the week has a clean structure. Per the Stocks list, Netflix reported after Thursday's close having already collapsed to around $68 on a multi-month slide, and the read from Mel Mattison is a textbook capitulation, a washed-out name reporting on a Thursday so the next day's options expiration forces volume through a flush.

The fundamental footnote is its own small controversy, with Netflix disclosing that roughly 300 of its shows and films used generative AI in production this year, per the Polymarket list. The trade and the theme are separate here. A capitulation flush is a setup for a bounce, and a studio quietly automating 300 productions is a longer story about margins that the panic is not pricing.

3. The real fear under the tape is that the AI demand is partly financing itself. The bearish thesis getting louder is circularity. Per a widely shared ValueInvesting post, Nvidia has invested in IREN, which buys Nvidia chips, taken a stake in CoreWeave, a major Nvidia buyer, and bought into Intel, so the worry is that vendor capital is doing a lap and coming back booked as demand, against roughly a quarter-trillion dollars of AI bonds issued this year. Those figures are the poster's rather than sourced, but the pattern is the one every bull has to answer.

The cleaner version of the question is Amazon. Per another writeup, Amazon is spending about $200 billion in capex this year against roughly $148 billion of operating cash flow, a $52 billion gap, betting AWS growth of 28 percent and its own Trainium chips at a $20 billion run-rate justify it. The counterweight is the oldest argument in the book, and a retail deep-dive attributes it to Warren Buffett around a claimed $31 billion Alphabet stake, that this is a race where a company that stops to catch its breath gets run over. That stake and its numbers are unconfirmed, so hold them loosely, but the logic is why the capex does not stop.

4. SpaceX broke its IPO price and its rocket scrubbed on the same morning. The most-owned new stock keeps finding new lows. Per the Financial Markets list, SpaceX closed below its $135 IPO price for the first time at $131.11, and as many as 911.5 million locked shares, about 1.4 times the entire IPO float, become eligible to sell after the first earnings report, and that is the supply overhang the bears keep pointing at. Michael Burry put it bluntly, that nothing in the S-1 suggests the company is worth a trillion dollars, let alone two.

The operational headline did not help. Per the People list, the Starship Flight 13 test scrubbed moments after ignition when several Raptor engines failed to start, pushing the retry out by days. The Prof G view frames the whole stock as an infrastructure play plus a non-top-tier AI model, with Starlink the real asset but facing the same brutal economics as terrestrial telecom once rivals add capacity, per that discussion. A falling stock before the lockup even opens is the tell that supply, not story, is in charge.

5. Crypto is quietly building a base while the equity crowd capitulates. The two most credible cycle reads agree on the level. Per Benjamin Cowen, Bitcoin near $64,000 is about 100 days from where the last two cycles bottomed on his day-count model, pointing at a fourth-quarter low, and per David Duong, the former Coinbase research head, the confluence of support sits at $56,000 to $58,000, already touched, with the bottom either in or very close.

The structural story is Robinhood Chain, which launched last week and went straight into the top five decentralized exchanges by volume, with about 28 million customers and $300 million of stablecoins on it, per Duong. Real assets are trading there too, with tokenized versions of SpaceX, gold, and oil changing hands on-chain, per this rundown. The caution is that Bitcoin ETFs just saw record monthly outflows near $4.5 billion, so the base is being built on capitulation rather than demand, per the Crypto list. Duong's framing is the useful one, that this looks closer to 1996 than 1999.

Single names to track.

  • $TSM (Taiwan Semiconductor). Raised 2026 capex to $52 to $56 billion and guided growth above 30 percent with demand visibility into 2030, then fell with the group, per the Stocks list.
  • $MU (Micron). Shed $459 billion in fourteen days on the momentum unwind despite an 80 percent operating margin and 2026 output sold to Nvidia, per the Polymarket list.
  • $SPCX (SpaceX). Broke its $135 IPO price to $131.11 with 911.5 million shares unlockable after first earnings, as a Starship test scrubbed, per the Financial Markets list.
  • $AAPL (Apple). Printed a new all-time high as the best Magnificent Seven name of the year, up about 24 percent, on rotation into cash-heavy defensives, per the Hottest Mag 7 read.
  • $SMCI (Super Micro). A bull writeup pegs it at 8 times forward earnings with margins recovering past 9 percent, though the author holds it and it carries active investigations.
  • BTC (Bitcoin). Near $64,000 with the cycle low modeled for the fourth quarter and support at $56,000 to $58,000, against record $4.5 billion ETF outflows, per Cowen and the Crypto list.

Psychology. The market spent the week teaching one idea from every angle, and today it wrote it in bold. Taiwan Semiconductor told you demand is booked into 2030, and Micron still bled $459 billion in fourteen days, which means the selling has nothing to do with whether the AI buildout is real and everything to do with a momentum factor unwinding from the 100th percentile of positioning. When a stock cannot rally on the best possible news, the crowd is not weighing the company, it is being forced out of a trade.

The tell of a capitulation is that the fundamentals and the price point in opposite directions at once. Netflix is washed out to $68 the same week it disclosed automating 300 productions, SpaceX is breaking its IPO price before a single insider is even allowed to sell, and Bitcoin is printing record ETF outflows right where two independent cycle models say the low forms. Each of those is a story of forced supply, not broken value, and forced supply is the thing that exhausts itself.

So the discipline into a Friday opex is to stop trying to catch the exact bottom and start separating the ones being sold from the ones being abandoned. The circularity fear is real and worth respecting, but Buffett's answer, if the writeup has it right, is that the spenders have no choice, so the demand does not vanish because the stocks fell. Let Netflix print, let the Micron selling find a seller of last resort, and remember that Goldman's own history says selloffs this violent average a gain the very next week. Own the bottleneck and the base, and let the momentum crowd finish being carried out.

item.title: Taiwan Semiconductor beat every number and raised its spending, and the stock fell anyway, because the thing scaring this tape is no longer demand, it is the bill for meeting it
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: TSMC posted a double beat and lifted capex again, pledging another $100 billion for Arizona, and Wall Street sold it.
