The momentum unwind that started in memory is now the widest in decades, Netflix reports after the close, and the day's real argument moved from whether the AI trade is real to who actually gets paid inside it.
item.content:encoded:
TSMC posted a double beat and lifted capex again, pledging another $100 billion for Arizona, and Wall Street sold it. The momentum unwind that started in memory is now the widest in decades, Netflix reports after the close, and the day's real argument moved from whether the AI trade is real to who actually gets paid inside it.

The morning was a rate story out of Seoul. The afternoon is a spending story out of everywhere, and the two are the same lesson from different angles. Here is the session recap, then five stories, then the single names.

Session recap. The tape spent the day punishing good news. Per the Market list, Taiwan Semiconductor beat on revenue at $40.2 billion, beat on earnings at $4.31 a share, guided next quarter up to $45.2 billion, and still fell around 4 percent because it raised capital spending again and committed another $100 billion to Arizona. The momentum unwind that began in memory got historic underneath it, with Goldman's high-beta momentum basket down 24 percent on the month, its worst since April 2009, and Morgan Stanley's tech-momentum measure posting its worst 17-day stretch in 27 years, per the Financial Markets list.

Producer prices came in cooler at 5.5 percent against 6.2 percent expected, UnitedHealth blew out and ran 8.5 percent, and Netflix reports after the close with the options market pricing an 8.5 percent move. The one clean win in chips was SK Hynix, whose new Nasdaq listing raised $26.5 billion and closed at $168 after pricing at $149, per the People list.

1. TSMC proved the demand and got sold on the spending, and that is the whole market in one print. The result was not the problem. Per Diet TBPN, Taiwan Semiconductor beat and lifted its capex guidance, and the Nasdaq slid about a percent on the spending plan rather than the earnings, the same reflex that hit the stock itself. When the best-run company in the supply chain tells you it has to spend more to keep up, the market now hears risk instead of confidence.

That reflex is showing up in the megacaps too. The reason Oracle, Meta, and Microsoft have been the ones getting hit, per a widely shared ValueInvesting writeup, is that the market is actively pricing their leverage and their capex rather than ignoring it, so the crowd has not fully detached from fundamentals. Oracle is the sharpest version, funding a roughly $56 billion buildout with tens of billions in new debt while it lacks the cash flows of the other hyperscalers. The uncomfortable question underneath the whole tape is who is paying for all the tokens, because a lot of the demand traces back to unprofitable startups with no moat.

2. The rotation everyone is arguing about is really two different questions, and they have two different answers. The bullish read is patience. Per AskLivermore, the average tech-out-of-favor rotation since 2000 has lasted 201 days and this one is only 42 days in, which puts the historical turn near mid-October, and in all nine prior episodes the money eventually came back to tech on a Fed pivot or an earnings beat. That is a reason to hold, and it is also a reason not to expect the bottom this week.

The sharper take is that the rotation is not out of tech at all. Per Chip Stock Investor, the move nobody named is the hyperscaler capex flood itself, roughly $482 billion trailing that heads toward $800 billion by year-end and likely past a trillion in 2027, rotating out of big-tech free cash flow and into memory, optical, power, and construction. The real danger day is whenever that spending stops, because margins would snap back on a much larger revenue base.

For the wreckage in the meantime, Shay Boloor laid out the damage from the highs, with Oklo down 76 percent, CoreWeave down 60 percent, AST SpaceMobile down 58 percent, Rocket Lab down 55 percent, and even Arm off 42 percent. The cold inflation prints that cut July hike odds hard, from 42 percent to 17 percent, are the setup for the pivot the patient case is waiting on, though Kevin Warsh spent two days testifying that he has no tolerance for sticky inflation, per VirtualBacon and Blockworks.

3. SpaceX broke its own IPO price, and the people who model it for a living think the floor is far lower. The most-owned new stock in the market cracked. Per Prof G Markets, SpaceX fell below its $135 IPO price for the first time and touched an all-time low of $132.75, now down 40 percent from its peak and more than a trillion dollars off the top, one week after joining the Nasdaq 100. Morningstar's Nicholas Owens puts fair value at $62 a share on a probability-weighted basis, with the medium case alone near $71 and the $130-to-$160 bull case carrying only about a 7 percent weight.

The mechanical problem is supply. The float is only about 4 percent of the company, so every move is amplified, and roughly 7 percent more becomes unlockable by insiders about two days after the August 8 earnings report, against index-inclusion demand Owens estimates at just $8 billion. The sell side has not caught up, with 27 of 31 analysts still rating it a buy and an average target of $242, one desk carrying $800, and Cathie Wood buying about $17 million of it into the drop, per the People list. Starlink is the real asset, but a 4 percent float meeting an insider unlock is a market-structure trap before it is a fundamental one.

4. The single-name tape is quietly picking AMD over Nvidia and hardware over hype. The most detailed bull case of the day is a reappraisal of the chip order. A long ValueInvesting breakdown argues the race has moved from raw chip speed to system-level memory and total cost, and lays out a model where an AMD Instinct node carries 768 gigabytes of memory against 384 on Nvidia's dual-die part, at an average price near $25,000 versus $45,000 and up. Those figures are one anonymous author's engineering math rather than vendor-confirmed numbers, and the case leans on a disputed report that Nvidia slipped a rack design by a year, which Nvidia denies, so treat it as a thesis and not a spec sheet.

What is not modeled is the price action, with AMD up 274 percent over the past year as investors reprice it as AI infrastructure rather than a CPU maker, per Stocks with Josh.

The same preference for the physical layer runs through the smaller names. Applied Digital just brought another 75 megawatts online to reach 175 megawatts operational at a fully leased campus targeting 400, with CoreWeave as the anchor tenant, per this breakdown. And the software-side bet with a real number attached is Robinhood, where the Milk Road thesis is that its roughly 30 million customers each adding trading and spending agents multiplies the transactions it clips, against a McKinsey estimate of a trillion dollars of agent-driven consumer revenue by 2030.

5. Crypto is rotating inside itself again, and the tells are Ethereum and Hyperliquid, not Bitcoin. Bitcoin is the laggard and the leaders are elsewhere. Per Lark Davis, Larry Fink called leverage washed out and said he is bullish over the next 12 months, but Bitcoin keeps getting rejected at its 50-day average and the 200-day near the old $83,000 level remains the line that separates a bounce from a bull market, with the last rejection there dumping it to $57,000 in weeks. Underneath, three fresh wallets pulled 30,000 Ether off Coinbase Prime, the accumulation signal that tends to lead.

The loudest Ethereum bet is Tom Lee. Per this rundown, his BitMine now holds 5.75 million Ether, close to 5 percent of supply, earning roughly a million dollars a day in staking yield, and he added another 20,000 Ether last week while sitting on a paper loss near $9 billion. And the token generating its own demand regardless of Bitcoin is Hyperliquid, which rose 5.5 percent on a day Bitcoin managed 2.1 percent while routing about 99 percent of its trading fees into buybacks, per Milk Road. The near-term caution stays real, with the technicians eyeing a push toward $69,000 that fails and a flush to the mid-$50,000s before the next leg.

Single names to track.

  • $TSM (Taiwan Semiconductor). Beat on revenue and earnings, guided up to $45.2 billion, and fell about 4 percent on a raised capex plan and another $100 billion for Arizona, per Diet TBPN.
  • $SPCX (SpaceX). Broke its $135 IPO price to an all-time low of $132.75 against a Morningstar fair value of $62 and a 4 percent float facing an August insider unlock, per Prof G Markets.
  • $AMD. Up 274 percent on the year as the market reprices it as AI infrastructure, with the bull case now built on memory capacity and cost per node, per Stocks with Josh and the AMD breakdown.
  • $APLD (Applied Digital). Added 75 megawatts to reach 175 operational at a fully leased campus targeting 400, anchored by CoreWeave, per this breakdown.
  • $HOOD (Robinhood). The agentic-commerce bet, roughly 30 million customers each able to run multiple trading and spending agents, up about 50 percent off the year's lows, per Milk Road.
  • $BMNR (BitMine). Holds 5.75 million Ether near 5 percent of supply at about a million a day in staking yield, adding into a $9 billion paper loss, per this rundown.
  • HYPE (Hyperliquid). Outran Bitcoin more than two to one on the day while routing 99 percent of fees into buybacks, per Milk Road.

Psychology. The market handed you two clean experiments today. Taiwan Semiconductor did everything right, beat every line, and got sold anyway, while the drawdown list from Shay Boloor shows Oklo down 76 percent and CoreWeave down 60 percent from names that were untouchable two months ago. The lesson is that in a momentum unwind, being right on the business is not the same as being right on the stock, and the widest momentum reversal in 27 years does not care which company reported.

The trap in a tape like this is treating a rotation as a verdict. Per AskLivermore, the money has come back to tech every single time across nine rotations since 2000, and this one is barely six weeks into an average of nearly seven months, so the crowd calling the top today is likely as early as the crowd that called Micron to $2,000 was late. The people who model SpaceX for a living put fair value at $62 while 27 of 31 analysts still say buy, and both of those cannot be right.

So the discipline into the Netflix print tonight is to separate the timeline from the thesis. If you believe the buildout is real, the capex math says the demand is not the risk, the day the spending stops is, and that day is not on tonight's tape. Let the momentum finish puking, let the insider unlocks clear, and buy the physical layer that is sold out rather than the leveraged expression that round-trips on a headline. Patience is a position here, and it is the only one the history actually rewards.

item.title: The memory crash finally got a real cause, and it was not the AI thesis breaking, it was Korea raising rates for the first time in three years
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Bank of Korea hiked 25 basis points, the KOSPI fell 7 percent, and Micron slipped under $900 as SK Hynix dropped another 11 percent. The Iran war restarted overnight and oil still would not break out, ASML told everyone 2027 is already sold out, and a Chinese memory giant is trading before it even lists.
item.content:encoded:
Bank of Korea hiked 25 basis points, the KOSPI fell 7 percent, and Micron slipped under $900 as SK Hynix dropped another 11 percent. The Iran war restarted overnight and oil still would not break out, ASML told everyone 2027 is already sold out, and a Chinese memory giant is trading before it even lists.

The story everyone feared was the AI trade cracking, and the actual cause turned out to be a central bank in Seoul. That distinction is the whole edge this morning. Here is the pre-open picture, then five stories.

Pre-open preview. The memory selloff has a name now. Per Jose Najarro, the Bank of Korea raised its base rate 25 basis points to 2.75 percent, its first hike since January 2023, and the KOSPI fell 7 percent with SK Hynix down 11 percent after hours and Samsung off 8, since those two names are more than half the index. Micron slipped under $900 to $896 and closed below its 50-day average for the first time in three months. Today brings Netflix and Taiwan Semiconductor earnings and US retail sales, the Iran war is back on with oil still stuck near $84, and the July 29 Fed meeting remains priced for no hike.

1. The memory round-trip was a rate story, not a thesis story, and that changes what you do with it. The distinction matters because it is tradeable. Per Najarro, the Korea hike raises borrowing costs on Korean-financed fabs, and that is why SK Hynix and Samsung led the drop, but it does not touch the demand curve for high-bandwidth memory or the fabs Micron builds with US money. So the sharpest fall is in the names with the local rate exposure, not the ones with the best long-run position.

The bull rebuttal came from the calmest company in the chain. ASML raised its four-year guidance and said its lithography capacity grows 30 percent next year and another 30 percent the year after, with 2027 already sold out and 2028 heading there, per the same breakdown. The argument is that ASML is the true bottleneck, and a bottleneck that is sold out three years forward stretches the cycle rather than ending it. When the pick-and-shovel supplier is booked solid while the crowd panics over a rate hike, the panic is the opportunity.

2. A Chinese memory giant is already trading, and the pros are shorting it before it lists. The freshest catalyst under the memory tape, per threadguy: CXMT, described as China's SK Hynix, goes public July 27, and it is already tradable as a pre-IPO perpetual future on Hyperliquid and Binance. It is trading at roughly ten times its IPO price, an implied valuation near $470 billion against an $80 billion offering, near Tencent territory.

The tell is the positioning. The two largest positions are professional shorts, and an anonymous whale pulled $75 million off Coinbase to accumulate it, so the smart money is split and loud. The open question threadguy raises is whether the CXMT hype is itself feeding the dump in the listed memory names as traders rotate into the shiny new thing.

3. The Iran war restarted and oil still refused to break out, the most important non-event of the week. Per the Market list, the US ran a second wave of strikes on Chabahar and Bandar Abbas, Kuwait intercepted four cruise missiles and 21 drones, and Trump swung from warning Iran to behave to saying they want to settle, all in a day. And oil sat at $84. A market that shrugs off active strikes on the Strait of Hormuz has decided this war is a series of spikes to fade, and that decision is what keeps the disinflation trade alive.

The bigger frame under it, per Capital Flows, is that the re-industrialization of America is the reason rates are not lower. The argument from New Vista Capital is that the US is rebuilding hardware, defense, and energy at the same time it retools software with AI, and the named plays are the vertically integrated defense-tech names, Anduril, Palantir, and SpaceX, plus the automation stories like using physics-based AI to lift General Dynamics artillery-shell output. The macro point is that a country rebuilding its industrial base while China out-produces it in shipbuilding by 200 times does not get cheap money.

4. The bank boom is the SpaceX-IPO machine, and PayPal is the deal it wants next. Per Prof G Markets, Jamie Dimon said it is getting close to as good as it gets for the banks, on a perfect storm of a resilient economy, high real rates that fatten net interest margins, and a resurgence of dealmaking, with all five big banks underwriting the SpaceX IPO and the OpenAI and Anthropic offerings flagged as the next tailwinds.

The live deal is PayPal, where Stripe and the private-equity firm Advent have put up a $53 billion offer with $50 billion of bank financing behind it, per the Market list. Michael Burry, a holder, says he will not sell at this price, and the stock still ran 15 percent, so the market thinks a higher bid is coming.

5. Crypto is quietly rotating, and the tell is XRP. Under the Bitcoin weakness, the flows are diverging. Per Lark Davis, Bitcoin ETFs bled hundreds of millions while the seven US spot XRP ETFs pulled in positive inflows, roughly $60 million in June and $1.49 billion cumulative since launch, with more than 970 million XRP now locked in them. The analytics firm Santiment reads it as capital rotating into XRP and Solana while Bitcoin and Ether ETFs bleed, the kind of divergence that shows up when smart money is positioning at a potential bottom rather than chasing the leader.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • ASML. Raised four-year guidance with 2027 sold out and capacity growing 30 percent two years running, the disciplined bottleneck that stretches the cycle, per Najarro.
  • $MU (Micron). Under $900 and below its 50-day average on the Korea rate hike rather than any demand miss, per Najarro.
  • CXMT. China's memory giant, public July 27, already trading as a pre-IPO perpetual at an implied $470 billion with pros short, per threadguy.
  • $PYPL (PayPal). Up 15 percent on the $53 billion Stripe and Advent bid, with Michael Burry holding out for more, per the Market list.
  • $ASTS (AST SpaceMobile). Priced $1 billion of convertible notes due 2034 into a correction, the stock having round-tripped from $133 in June to $57, per the Market list.
  • XRP. The rotation tell, with $1.49 billion of cumulative ETF inflows while Bitcoin ETFs bleed, per Lark Davis.

Psychology. Two days ago Micron was worth more than Berkshire and the crowd was calling for $2,000, and this morning it is under $900 because a central bank in Seoul raised rates a quarter point. That is the whole lesson of the week compressed into one name. The move up was leverage dressed as conviction, and the move down did not need the AI story to break, it just needed the borrowing cost on a Korean fab to tick higher, and the leverage did the rest.

The tell is who is calm and who is not. ASML, the company that actually controls the supply, raised its guidance and said 2027 is sold out, while the leveraged holders got liquidated on a 25-basis-point hike and the smart money is already short the next Chinese memory IPO before it lists. The people closest to the real economics are the least rattled, and the people furthest out on leverage are the ones getting carried out.

So the discipline check into the Netflix and Taiwan Semiconductor prints today is to separate the trade from the theme again. The theme is intact, ASML is booked through 2027 and the demand is real, but the leveraged expressions of it, the 2x memory ETFs and the pre-IPO perps, are the ones that just showed you what they do on a bad headline. If you want the memory cycle, own the bottleneck that is sold out, not the leverage that round-trips on a rate hike. Let Micron prove it can hold $900 and let the chip earnings print before you decide the panic was the top or the gift.

item.title: Micron was worth more than Berkshire at the open and broke below $900 by the close, and the money leaving the chips went into Apple instead
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Producer prices came in cold too, so the Fed hike is basically off the table, but the session was a rotation, not a rally. Semis crashed while the Nasdaq held on megacap strength, the 2x SK Hynix ETF fell 15 percent on day two, and oil quietly refused to break out even with the strait shut.
item.content:encoded:
Producer prices came in cold too, so the Fed hike is basically off the table, but the session was a rotation, not a rally. Semis crashed while the Nasdaq held on megacap strength, the 2x SK Hynix ETF fell 15 percent on day two, and oil quietly refused to break out even with the strait shut.

The morning looked like a memory coronation and the afternoon looked like a hangover, and the gap between them is the whole session. Underneath it, the market bought a second cold inflation print and rotated hard out of what ran. Here is the recap, then five stories.

The close. Producer prices (the wholesale-inflation read, the PPI) came in at 5.5 percent against a 6.2 expected, cold on the heels of Tuesday's cold consumer number, and the July 29 Fed meeting is now priced 95 percent for no change, per the Stocks and Polymarket lists. But this was not a broad rally. Semiconductors were in freefall while the Nasdaq held green, the worst semis-down-on-an-up-Nasdaq day since November 2024, with money rotating into megacap software as Apple printed a new all-time high near the $5 trillion club, per the Financial Markets list. PayPal ran 16 to 20 percent on its takeover bid, and Bitcoin held $65,000.

1. The memory trade round-tripped in a single day, and the round-trip is the message. Tuesday's melt-up got its coronation at Wednesday's open, with Micron back over $1,000 and worth more than Berkshire Hathaway. By the close it had broken below $900, per the People list, and the 2x long SK Hynix ETF that launched into the frenzy fell about 15 percent on its second day. The SK Hynix ADR itself had tagged a record $193.90 on Tuesday, up 25 percent, so the whole complex went vertical and then gave a chunk of it back inside 24 hours.

The rotation underneath is the important part. Semis crashed while Apple, Microsoft, and Google caught a bid, money leaving the crowded, levered memory trade for the megacaps that had lagged. The bull case did not change, the Milk Road desk still says buy the dip on Micron and SanDisk, citing Dylan Patel's call that memory demand grows 20 to 40 percent a year through 2028 and Morgan Stanley lifting hyperscaler capital spending to $1.2 trillion in 2028. But a trade that can be worth more than Berkshire at 9:30 and cracked by 4:00 is telling you it is running on positioning, not on news.

2. Oil refused to break out even with the strait shut, and that quietly saves the disinflation. The single most important thing the tape did today was almost nothing. Despite Iran reshutting the Strait of Hormuz and a US naval blockade, crude did not spike to a new high. Per the Financial Markets list, the average price of WTI crude is $80 in July against $82 in June and $98.50 in May, so even after a 15 percent bounce off the low, July is on pace for a lower average than June. The blockade is priced, and a Trump headline that Iran wants a deal capped the rest.

That matters because the whole disinflation story rested on cheap energy, and this morning that looked fragile. The counter-read from Mark Zandi is that underlying inflation is still stuck near 3 to 3.5 percent and the June drop was mostly oil, so a real crude spike would reverse it. The market's answer today was that the spike is not coming, and that is why it bought the cold PPI and let the hawkish new Fed chair fade into the background.

3. The bottleneck moved past GPUs, and the rotation has names. The cleanest framing of where the AI money goes next, per the Milk Road note: the GPU scarcity trade is over, and the new binding constraints are memory, optics, packaging, and power. The picks are specific, Micron for memory (high-bandwidth memory sold out through mid-2027, operating margins of 80 percent that beat Nvidia's), Corning for optics on a new Nvidia partnership to build US optical-connectivity manufacturing, and Infineon for power, raising prices a second time this year as its AI data-center revenue goes from 1.5 to 2.5 billion euros.

The cautionary note is that the highest-beta version of this trade broke today. The neoclouds cracked hard, with Nebius down 36 percent even after signing a $1 billion compute contract and CoreWeave off 40 percent, per the Milk Road memory panel. The theme is right and the leveraged expressions of it are the ones getting punished, the same lesson the SK Hynix ETF taught this morning.

4. PayPal is being fought over, and the plumbing under stocks is going on-chain. The takeover is live and the holders want more, per the Stocks list: PayPal jumped past $55 on the $60.50-a-share Stripe and Advent bid, prediction markets put the deal at 74 percent, and shareholders are calling the offer too low. The bigger structural story landed the same day. The Depository Trust and Clearing Corporation, which settles nearly all US stock trades, launched a pilot with JPMorgan, BlackRock, Goldman Sachs, and Vanguard to tokenize stocks and Treasurys, per the same list, and BlackRock crossed $15 trillion in assets for the first time. The tokenization trade stopped being a crypto pitch and became a project run by the people who own the market.

5. Two fresh names and one cautionary post-mortem. The genuinely new single name is Aehr Test Systems, per Jose Najarro, which jumped 30 percent after hours to $93.90 and guided fiscal-2027 revenue growth of 160 to 200 percent as its chip-testing business pivots into AI accelerators and memory. The second is CleanSpark, per Stocks with Josh, which jumped more than 10 percent on a $6.6 billion, 20-year data-center lease at its Georgia campus, reframing a Bitcoin miner as an AI-infrastructure play, with a stop just below $12 and a target at $15.

The post-mortem is Intel, and it is a warning about the whole sector. Per Pat Gelsinger on All-In, Intel got demolished because bean-counters ran it over technologists, handing $100 billion back to shareholders in buybacks while skipping a fab for a decade, passing on the iPhone chip, and killing its own GPU a week after he first left. His sharper warning is geopolitical, that Taiwan has under three weeks of energy reserves and a blockade would brown out the fabs that need 90 days to restart, an impact he calls greater than the Great Depression with no shot fired.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $AEHR (Aehr Test Systems). Up 30 percent after hours to $93.90 on a fiscal-2027 guide of 160 to 200 percent revenue growth, pivoting into AI-accelerator and memory testing, per Najarro.
  • $CLSK (CleanSpark). Up double digits on a $6.6 billion, 20-year data-center lease, a stop below $12 and a $15 target, per Josh.
  • $PYPL (PayPal). Past $55 on the $60.50 Stripe and Advent bid, deal odds 74 percent, holders calling it too low, per the Stocks list.
  • $ASTS (AST SpaceMobile). Per Rick Orford, the carrier-moat satellite play validated by AT&T and Verizon across nearly 60 mobile networks, rated hold but with analyst targets implying roughly 70 percent upside.
  • $INTC / TEM / VST (Intel, Tempus AI, Vistra). The non-obvious Trump-and-Pelosi overlap buys, per Joseph Hogue, smaller and more policy-driven than the mega-cap names both also hold.
  • Bitcoin. Cathie Wood called the bottom with a $750,000 to $1.25 million five-year target, per Lark Davis, and the level to reclaim is the 200-day average near $74,000, per his positioning read.

Psychology. The memory crowd got its entire emotional cycle compressed into one trading day. Micron was worth more than Berkshire at the open and broke below $900 by the close, someone on the tape was still calling for $2,000 Micron while the 2x SK Hynix ETF fell 15 percent, and the neoclouds that everyone piled into cracked 40 percent. That is not a market digesting news, that is a market unwinding leverage in real time and pretending it was a plan.

The tell is where the money went. It did not chase the crack lower and it did not double down, it rotated into Apple at a record and the megacap software that had done nothing, the move smart money makes when it thinks the easy part of a trade is over. Bitcoin is the calm version of the same instinct, with Cathie Wood and the on-chain crowd talking about accumulation and an October bottom rather than reaching for a leveraged bet on a name that already tripled.

So the discipline check into Netflix earnings tomorrow and the megacap prints later this month is to notice which of your positions needs the story to keep getting louder to keep working. The 2x memory ETFs and the neoclouds are that kind of position, they require the melt-up to continue, and they round-tripped in a day when it paused. The names worth holding through the next oil headline or hot data point are the ones the smart money rotated into today, not the ones it rotated out of. Let Micron prove it can hold $900 before you decide the coronation was real.

item.title: Iran shut the strait overnight and the US answered with a blockade, so the cheap oil that gave the Fed cover just went away and the memory trade came roaring back
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Micron is worth more than Berkshire again, SK Hynix ran 27 percent and killed the Korea discount, and Aehr guided miles past the Street. Bitcoin crossed $65,000 as the on-chain crowd stacked its bottom signals, and PayPal is suddenly in play at a $53 billion bid.
item.content:encoded:
Micron is worth more than Berkshire again, SK Hynix ran 27 percent and killed the Korea discount, and Aehr guided miles past the Street. Bitcoin crossed $65,000 as the on-chain crowd stacked its bottom signals, and PayPal is suddenly in play at a $53 billion bid.

The market spent yesterday selling a cold inflation print, and overnight the reason that print was cold started to reverse. Iran reshut the Strait of Hormuz, the US put warships back around it, and oil is bid again. Here is the pre-open picture, then five stories.

Pre-open preview. The overnight regime change is geopolitical. Per the Market list, Iran's Revolutionary Guard declared the Strait of Hormuz shut until US attacks stop, and Washington reinstated the naval blockade with more than 20 warships after another round of strikes on southern Iran. Oil is up, gold is red below $4,050 as the dollar firms, and Asian stocks still rose on the soft US inflation data, per the Financial Markets list. The calendar adds producer prices (the PPI, the wholesale-inflation read) this morning and Netflix and Taiwan Semiconductor earnings Thursday, and the July 29 Fed meeting is now priced at only a 7 to 17 percent chance of a hike, per the People list.

1. The disinflation that let the Fed stand pat is sitting on a barrel of oil, and Iran just kicked it. Yesterday's inflation print was cold in large part because energy fell 5.7 percent, and energy fell because oil had come off. That is the piece now unwinding. The strait is shut, the blockade is back, and the oil bid returned overnight, per the Financial Markets list. The trade some are already in is the obvious one, threadguy is long Brent from around $82 after Trump's 20 percent Hormuz toll sent crude up 8 to 10 percent in a session, the first move that size since late April.

The read-through is that the Fed's room to relax just narrowed. A cold June number bought the market relief, but if crude climbs back toward $90 on a real blockade, the July inflation print reverses and the hawkish new chair gets his excuse. Kevin Warsh is already leaning that way, telling Congress that inflation is a choice, so the disinflation trade and the oil trade are now the same bet running in opposite directions.

2. The memory trade did not just survive the leverage scare, it took out its price targets. Two days after Korea's leverage-squared crash, the memory names roared back. Micron is back above $1,000 and worth more than $1.1 trillion, which once again makes it larger than Berkshire Hathaway, per the People list. SK Hynix ran about 27 percent, tagged the $195 target before settling near $181, and in doing so erased the discount its US listing had opened, per the Market list. The 2x long ETF that launched into the chaos held, with high-bandwidth memory sold out through 2027.

The read underneath the bounce is that the shakeout was forced selling, not a thesis break. Jordi Visser called it capitulation, pointing to record margin debt liquidated on the highest-volume back-to-back sessions in a month with the lows printing on the heaviest 30-minute volume right before the intraday reversal, per the Hermes list. The theme is widening past the majors too. Aehr Test Systems ($AEHR) beat and guided fiscal-2027 bookings to $130 to $150 million against a Street number near $85 million, and the stock ran 26 to 36 percent, per the Market list.

3. The Bitcoin bottom case got hard sub-levels, and they all point at the same number. The most detailed on-chain read of the week, per Milk Road with Matt Crosby: the levels that mark accumulation bottoms are converging near $50,000, with realized price around $53,000, the long-term-holder cost basis near $50,000, and a deeper floor metric near $48,000. Bitcoin is trading at its production cost, its 200-week moving average sits around $63,000, and at the $57,000 to $58,000 cycle low it was in the cheapest 5 percent of its entire history. Crosby's timing overlay of the 2022 capitulation points at July 12, so his read is that the time-based bottom may already be in, and Bitcoin crossed $65,000 overnight, per the People list.

The honest counterweight is that the same crowd cannot agree on when. Benjamin Cowen's seasonal read has the cold CPI fueling a rally into late July or early August, the same July-relief pattern as 2018, before a possible final low later in the year. So the levels say close and the calendar says maybe not yet, and that is exactly why the discipline here is dollar-cost-averaging into the zone rather than calling the exact bottom.

4. PayPal is in play, and the bid is real money. The biggest new single name, per the Market and People lists: Stripe and the private-equity firm Advent have bid $60.50 a share for $PYPL (PayPal), a roughly $53 billion offer at about a 28 percent premium, with $50 billion of financing behind it. A fintech incumbent that spent two years as a value trap getting a cash bid from the private-market leader in payments is the kind of event that reprices the whole sector, and it lands the same week Oracle's collapse (down 62 percent from its September high, with a 2x short ETF launching today) shows how fast the market punishes the AI names that miss.

5. The AI-payoff skeptics keep stacking receipts under the buildout. Under the memory euphoria, the return-on-investment question got louder. An MIT study cited on the Hermes list found 95 percent of enterprise generative-AI pilots show no profit-and-loss return, IBM just fell 25 percent because its clients are diverting budget away from it, and New York became the first state to freeze data-center construction. Set against that, Kalshi launched forward-price curves for renting Nvidia B200, H200, and A100 compute, pricing hyperscaler compute spend above $700 billion this year, per the Kalshi list. The spend is real and enormous, the proven payoff is not there yet, and that gap is the single biggest thing hanging over every name in this briefing.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $MU (Micron). Back above $1,000 and larger than Berkshire again, with high-bandwidth memory sold out through 2027, per the People list.
  • $AEHR (Aehr Test Systems). Beat and guided fiscal-2027 bookings to $130 to $150 million against an $85 million Street number, up 26 to 36 percent, per the Market list.
  • $PYPL (PayPal). In play on a $60.50-a-share Stripe and Advent bid, roughly $53 billion at a 28 percent premium, per the Market list.
  • $SNDK (SanDisk). The highest-beta memory expression, with one voice on the Stocks list putting a $2,500 target on it over 12 months. Treat the number as the euphoria tell it is.
  • $ORCL (Oracle). Down 62 percent from its September high with a 2x short ETF launching today, per the Financial Markets list. The cautionary counterweight to the memory melt-up.
  • Bitcoin. Crossed $65,000 with the accumulation levels clustered near $50,000 and the 200-week average at $63,000, per Milk Road. A no-hike July 29 is the catalyst that opens $70,000.

Psychology. The memory crowd got exactly the vindication it was praying for, and it got it fast. Two days after Korea tripped a circuit breaker and the leverage-squared trade looked broken, Micron is back over Berkshire, SK Hynix ran 27 percent, and someone on the tape is already slapping a $2,500 target on SanDisk. That is not a market that learned anything from Monday, that is a market that treated a 15 percent crash as a dip and came back more convinced.

The Bitcoin side is the calmer mirror image, where Milk Road, Crosby, and Cowen are all quietly saying the same thing, that the levels are cheap and the move is to accumulate rather than to call the low. The contrast is the tell. In memory the crowd is euphoric and reaching for leverage and price targets, and in Bitcoin the smart voices are patient and talking about production cost and dollar-cost-averaging, and only one of those postures survives an oil shock.

So the discipline check this morning is to notice which of your positions is running on conviction and which is running on leverage. Iran just reshut the strait, oil is bid, and the Fed chair is looking for a reason to stay hawkish, the exact backdrop that punishes a crowded, levered trade like the 2x memory ETFs and rewards a patient one like averaging into Bitcoin near its production cost. Let PPI print and let oil show its hand before you decide the memory names get to be worth more than Berkshire without a fight.

item.title: June inflation printed its biggest monthly drop since 2020 and the market sold it, while IBM had its worst day since 1987 and the money it lost went straight into memory
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Core CPI came in at 2.6 percent and July hike odds collapsed from 42 to 15 percent, yet stocks gave back the pop because the new Fed chair is hawkish and oil is a live wire. IBM fell 25 percent as clients diverted spending into the memory chips that ripped, and Korea just showed what leverage squared does on the way down.
item.content:encoded:
Core CPI came in at 2.6 percent and July hike odds collapsed from 42 to 15 percent, yet stocks gave back the pop because the new Fed chair is hawkish and oil is a live wire. IBM fell 25 percent as clients diverted spending into the memory chips that ripped, and Korea just showed what leverage squared does on the way down.

Today handed the market a genuinely great inflation number and the market shrugged, because the story underneath the print is a violent rotation, not a soft landing. Here is the session recap, then five stories.

The close. June CPI came in ice cold, headline 3.5 percent against 3.8 expected and core (which strips out food and energy) at 2.6, with the monthly reading down 0.4 percent, the biggest drop since May 2020, per the CPI breakdown. Odds of a July rate hike cratered from 41.7 percent to 16.6, yet the tape sold the news, spiking the S&P about 60 handles and giving back 40, per the Financial Markets list. The Nasdaq held a 1.3 percent gain and semis ran 4.5 percent while software fell, small caps are now on track for their best year since 2003, and the VIX (the market's fear gauge) sits at a calm 17.

Two things capped the rally. New Fed chair Kevin Warsh testified hawkish, saying inflation is a choice and holding rates at 3.5 to 3.75 percent, so the debate is now one hike or none with zero cuts priced this year. And oil whipsawed, spiking toward $86 when Trump reinstated the Hormuz blockade and then reversing when he walked the 20 percent toll back within a day.

1. IBM's worst day since 1987 is the clearest read-through to the memory bull all year. IBM fell about 25 percent and erased $50 to $65 billion of market cap on a soft quarter, its largest drop in decades, per the Market list. The reason matters more than the number. CEO Arvind Krishna said clients diverted their spending into servers, storage, and memory to get ahead of price hikes, and did not anticipate the magnitude, so the miss is not weak demand, it is demand leaving software for hardware.

The tape proved the point in real time. Software names sold off in sympathy, with Microsoft, Adobe, Palantir, Salesforce, and Figma all lower, while the memory complex ripped, SK Hynix up as much as 15 to 20 percent to a new high, Micron up 5 to 6 with its calls jumping 40 percent, and SanDisk up 7, per the Market AM list. Memory gross margins now top Nvidia's, Micron at 80 percent, SK Hynix at 72, and SanDisk at 69 against Nvidia's 66. When the money leaving the biggest software name in the world shows up the same day in memory margins, the rotation is not a theory anymore.

2. Cold CPI, hawkish chair, and one live oil wire, so the Fed cannot fully relax. The print was the kind of number that should have run stocks all day, energy fell 5.7 percent and pulled headline inflation down hard. The reason it did not stick is the same Fed that spent the spring flirting with hikes. Warsh is not the dovish chair the market wanted, and with September hike odds still above 50 percent and no cut priced before year-end, a soft CPI buys relief rather than a pivot.

The wildcard is the thing that made this print good in the first place. Energy fell because oil had come off, and oil is one Trump headline away from erasing that, as today showed when Brent spiked toward $86 on the Hormuz blockade and then gave it all back on the walk-back, per the Financial Markets list. The disinflation that let the Fed stand pat is sitting on a barrel of crude in the Strait of Hormuz, and that is not a stable foundation.

3. The memory trade got its own leverage circus, and Korea is the warning label. SK Hynix's two leveraged US ETFs launched today into the melt-up, and the skew tells you where the crowd is, the 2x long taking 722,000 shares against just 102,000 for the short, a seven-to-one tilt, with the long product up 9 percent, per the People list. That is the American version of what already broke in Seoul.

The cautionary tale is on the Prof G episode: Korea has launched 16 single-stock leveraged ETFs since late May, tripling to over $9 billion, and those products plus Samsung and SK Hynix now make up more than 70 percent of Korean trading volume. The guest called it leverage squared, margin loans buying 2x products, and it is exactly why SK Hynix could fall 15 percent Monday and rise 15 percent Tuesday. The BofA fund-manager survey names the risk directly, 82 percent call long semiconductors the most crowded trade on the board, per the People AM list. A crowded trade with a fresh leverage layer bolted on is not safer for the new toy.

4. Neoclouds are the next leg of the buildout, and the balance sheet is the whole call. The freshest capital-spending number, per Milk Road, is Morgan Stanley lifting hyperscaler capex to $1.23 trillion in 2027 and $1.4 trillion in 2028, now counting SpaceX as a hyperscaler, with neocloud revenue projected to compound 58 percent a year to roughly $400 billion by 2031. The two names to know sit on opposite ends of the risk spectrum. $CRWV (CoreWeave) has a $99.4 billion order backlog against a $50 billion market cap, but carries $24.9 billion of debt and burns cash. $NBIS (Nebius) grew first-quarter AI-cloud revenue 841 percent to $389 million with $9.3 billion of cash and almost no debt, plus a ClickHouse stake worth about 30 percent of its market value.

The distinction is the trade. In a business where most of the more than 100 emerging neoclouds will fail, per the same note, the one with the cash survives the moments the levered one cannot, and Nvidia is now backstopping GPU financing for these players directly to keep the revenue floor under them. Milk Road's targets are $NBIS at $300 to $325 and $CRWV at $150, but the durable version of the trade is the balance sheet, not the backlog.

5. Bitcoin's bottom looks close, but everyone can see the same October. The on-chain read is constructive without being a green light, per the On-Chain Mind panel: Bitcoin's median holder cost basis sits near $63,000, its production cost near $60,000, and the share of supply in profit has fallen to 45 percent, which has marked the 2020 and 2022 bottoms. The structural floor calls cluster at $50,000 to $60,000, with a bottom only confirmed above $74,000, and both the long-term-holder cost crossover and the four-year cycle point at October. Bitcoin bounced to $64,000 on the CPI print today with Ether leading for once, up 5 percent past $1,800 as more than $100 million of shorts got liquidated.

The catch is that the whole market sees the same date. Per Milk Road, VanEck wants a full Bitcoin allocation by October and Jordi Visser says Bitcoin and Ether become the crowded trade within a year, and that is precisely why one trader argues the move front-runs the consensus and comes sooner. When everyone is waiting to buy the same low, the low rarely waits for them.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $SKHY (SK Hynix). Per Edelbridge, the HBM memory near-monopoly at 56.4 percent global share, trading at a forward price-to-earnings of just 7 with 72 percent operating margins and a locked allocation in Nvidia's next-generation Vera Rubin chips. The weak spot is geopolitics, helium and the Strait of Hormuz.
  • $NBIS / $CRWV (Nebius, CoreWeave). The neocloud pair, per Milk Road, Nebius the cash-rich survivor and CoreWeave the levered backlog bet.
  • $VST (Vistra). Per Rick Orford, 44,000 megawatts of generation and a 20-year, 2,600-megawatt nuclear deal with Meta, at 13 times earnings, as data-center power demand is set to more than double by 2030.
  • $QCOM (Qualcomm). Per Chip Stock Investor, a quiet data-center pivot targeting $15 billion of AI data-center revenue by 2029 from zero, via the AlphaWave and Modular acquisitions, at roughly $190.
  • $NFLX (Netflix). Per Stocks with Josh, the earnings-dip trade around Thursday's print near $76, with a $1.8 million call buy at the $78 strike.
  • $IONQ (IonQ). Per Rick Orford, the quantum infrastructure bet acquiring US chipmaker SkyWater for vertical integration, speculative with no proven moat and no hard numbers yet.

Psychology. The two records that bracket this session tell the whole story. Yesterday SanDisk printed the highest monthly RSI ever recorded on any stock, and today IBM had its worst day since 1987, and they are the same trade seen from both ends, money fleeing everything that is not memory and piling into everything that is. The BofA survey caught the crowd in the act, with 82 percent calling long semiconductors the most crowded position on the board, right as SK Hynix's 2x ETF opened to a seven-to-one long skew.

That is what a late-stage rotation feels like from the inside, total conviction that this one trade cannot lose, expressed through more and more leverage. Korea already ran the experiment, where single-stock 2x products became 70 percent of the volume and the index swung 15 percent in a day in both directions. The American memory names are now getting the same machinery, and the market sold a great CPI print rather than chasing, the tape quietly telling you the easy buyers are getting scarce.

So the discipline check is about which trade you are being handed. Buying the 2x SK Hynix ETF at a new high, the day after the underlying printed a record RSI, is the leverage-squared trade Seoul just showed you the downside of. The harder and better positioning is where the survivors are, in the cash-rich neocloud like Nebius over the levered one, in the memory near-monopoly at seven times earnings rather than the 2x wrapper on top of it. Let Warsh and the oil wire settle before you decide the crowd was right to need this much leverage to stay long.

item.title: CPI lands this morning and the leveraged SK Hynix ETFs launch into a name already down 38 percent, so the crowd gets a bigger lever right as the pros reach for cash
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The trading desks are rotating out of crypto and into crude, Benjamin Cowen says Bitcoin's real bottom is late September rather than December, and the structural bull case for US assets got a fresh catalyst date. Meanwhile Robinhood Chain keeps flipping Hyperliquid on volume without taking its moat.
item.content:encoded:
The trading desks are rotating out of crypto and into crude, Benjamin Cowen says Bitcoin's real bottom is late September rather than December, and the structural bull case for US assets got a fresh catalyst date. Meanwhile Robinhood Chain keeps flipping Hyperliquid on volume without taking its moat.

Two events define the session before it opens. June CPI prints this morning, and the two leveraged SK Hynix ETFs go live the same day the underlying is already 38 percent off its high. Here is the pre-open picture, then four stories.

Pre-open preview. The calendar is the catalyst. June CPI (the inflation print that settles the July 29 Fed debate) lands this morning, and a bullish weekly close usually has its low in by Wednesday, so today and tomorrow set the week's tone, per the trading desk at Cookbook. The same morning brings a 2x long and a 1x short SK Hynix ETF, arriving after the stock fell 15 percent Monday to 38 percent below its June high, with technical gap-fills sitting at 1,680 and then 1,440 that would wipe out leveraged holders, per threadguy's recap.

The one floor under it is Leopold Aschenbrenner, reportedly bidding $7 to $10 billion for the US listing at a 10 percent premium. Bitcoin sits around $61,000, and the levels to watch are $60,000 to $62,000 as the week's low and $64,500 to $65,000 as the reclaim that changes the picture.

1. The clearest voices on Bitcoin agree the real bottom is still weeks away. Benjamin Cowen's structural read is that 2026 is a lower-volatility carbon copy of the 2018 bear market, with Bitcoin squeezed between its 200-week moving average (the long-run trend line) and the bear-market resistance band above. His fractal has the dip into CPI happening now, a bounce, then the gains given back by mid-to-late August, the same round-trip 2018 and 2022 both ran. His cycle-bottom call is late September or early October, and he is blunt that waiting for a December low is greedy.

The trading desk arrives at the same place from the tape. Per Cookbook, the levels converge on $60,000 to $62,000 as the week's low and a reclaim of $64,500 to $65,000 as the trigger to get interested, with one trader holding a partial long from $58,000 hedged with a short from the $64,000s. The shared warning is about altcoins. Cowen's point, made for the twentieth time, is that even if Bitcoin holds its line, the alts bleed through August and September the way they did in 2018 while Bitcoin sat at $6,000, because the business cycle does not support a rotation into them.

2. The rotation the desks are actually making is out of crypto and into crude. The live tactical trade, per Charlie at Cookbook: long crude oil with a wide stop, on the view that the Strait of Hormuz conflict produces a bouncing ball of diminishing price spikes rather than one clean panic. He closed his Hyperliquid position for a loss when the thesis broke, called the ETH/BTC ratio done, and is pivoting toward traditional markets on the blunt logic that the opportunities in crypto are getting smaller by the day.

That squares with the oddity of the oil tape. Five months into the conflict, with Trump now taxing cargo through the strait, crude is only back to roughly where it traded on March 1, per threadguy. The crude trade is not a bet that oil explodes, it is a bet that a market this calm about an active war is underpricing the tail, and that the next spike finds a buyer. It is the cleaner expression of the same caution driving money out of the alts.

3. The structural bull case for US assets got a fresh catalyst date. Underneath the daily chop is the reason US valuations stay elevated, laid out by Capital Flows: China runs roughly a $1 trillion goods surplus behind a closed capital account, so that money has nowhere to go but into US assets, and that forced recycling rather than sentiment is why the S&P has held rich multiples for a decade. The dollar is 58 percent of world reserves and 88 percent of currency trades, and BowTied Bull adds that foreigners own about 21.4 percent of US stocks and a quarter of US bonds, while Trump Accounts are quietly turning the S&P 500 into the country's default retirement bid.

The actionable piece is a date. Per Capital Flows, the CME launches single-stock futures on Nvidia, AMD, and other tech names on July 27, which are the pre-staged instruments for the one tail that breaks this whole structure: a move on Taiwan. TSMC is the single choke point of the AI arms race, so a Taiwan-invasion headline is a same-instant signal to short the chips, and now there will be a clean way to do it. The same note argues gold does not revisit $3,000, because its rally was a hedge on the trade system itself rather than a bet on dollar devaluation.

4. Robinhood Chain keeps flipping Hyperliquid on volume, but the moat has not moved. The nuance worth holding, per threadguy: Robinhood Chain has now beaten Hyperliquid in 24-hour decentralized-exchange volume three days running at roughly $1 billion a day, a week after launch, with 17 million transactions, 350,000 addresses, and $250 million in deposits. That is real adoption, and $HOOD has decoupled from Bitcoin, up 80 percent off its May lows. But his verdict on the actual competition is that Hyperliquid keeps its trader-and-liquidity moat, because the depth that serious traders need is too established for a brokerage chain to pull away, at least yet. The trade is long the Robinhood story and skeptical of the Hyperliquid obituary at the same time.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • Crude oil. The desks' rotation trade, long with a wide stop on the view that a calm war tape underprices the next Hormuz spike, per Cookbook.
  • $HOOD (Robinhood). Decoupled from Bitcoin and up 80 percent off the May lows as its chain flips Hyperliquid on volume, though the trader moat holds, per threadguy.
  • $NVDA / $AMD. The CME lists single-stock futures on both July 27, the clean hedge instrument for a Taiwan-TSMC shock, per Capital Flows.
  • $SKHY (SK Hynix). The 2x long and 1x short ETFs launch today into a stock 38 percent off its high, with gap-fills at 1,680 and 1,440 and Aschenbrenner reportedly bidding the ADR at a premium, per threadguy.
  • Bitcoin. Squeezed between the 200-week average and resistance, with a cycle bottom Cowen puts in late September, and $60,000 to $65,000 the range that decides the week, per Cowen.

Psychology. The most honest sentence in the whole batch came from a trader describing his own rules, not the market. Round-tripping sucks, Magus wrote on the Cookbook desk, so he would rather sell too early than watch a gain turn into a loss, and Charlie backed it up by closing his Hyperliquid trade for a loss the moment the thesis broke rather than hoping it back. That is what discipline sounds like from people who do this for a living, and it is the opposite of what today's product launch invites.

Because the other thing happening this morning is a 2x SK Hynix ETF going live so the crowd can bet bigger on a name already down 38 percent, right as Aschenbrenner tries to catch it and Cowen warns the real bottom is two months out. The tape is handing retail a bigger lever at the exact moment the professionals are shortening their own. When the pros are rotating to cash and crude and selling their crypto early, and the new toy is leverage on the thing that just cracked, the crowded side of the boat is obvious.

So the discipline check into CPI is to notice which trade you are being sold. Buying the 2x SK Hynix launch or catching the memory knife is the leveraged-Korean trade Cowen and the desks are stepping away from. If you want to be positioned, the desks are telling you where they went, into crude with a wide stop and into cash, waiting for Bitcoin to reclaim $65,000 or for the September low to actually arrive. Let the print come first, and let the level prove itself before you add.

item.title: Korea tripped its circuit breaker and SanDisk printed the highest RSI ever recorded on any stock, and the memory blowoff still would not admit it was a blowoff
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The session opened with a trillion dollars gone from Asia and $300 billion off the US at the bell, then Apple hit a record and rescued the tape. Trump put a 20 percent toll on the Strait of Hormuz, SpaceX broke below its deal price with a governance story underneath, and CPI lands tomorrow.
item.content:encoded:
The session opened with a trillion dollars gone from Asia and $300 billion off the US at the bell, then Apple hit a record and rescued the tape. Trump put a 20 percent toll on the Strait of Hormuz, SpaceX broke below its deal price with a governance story underneath, and CPI lands tomorrow.

The cracks the morning warned about arrived, and then the tape half-healed anyway. That gap between what broke and what closed is the whole story tonight. Here is the session recap, then five stories.

The close. It opened ugly. Roughly $1 trillion came off Asian markets overnight and Korea's KOSPI fell 8.95 percent into a circuit breaker, its worst since Lehman, with SK Hynix down more than 15 percent, and the US wiped over $300 billion at the open, per the Kalshi list. Then the megacaps carried it back. Apple printed a new all-time high above $4.7 trillion, the S&P clung to 750 where a $1.7 billion put wall sat as support, the Dow actually closed green, and the Nasdaq finished down only about 1.2 percent, per the Stocks and Market lists. Semis took the pain, with the $SOXX off 5 percent and gold slipping toward $4,000.

Two things set up tomorrow. June CPI (the inflation print that settles the July 29 Fed debate) lands in the morning alongside the launch of two leveraged SK Hynix ETFs, a 2x long and a 1x short, per the People list. And oil is now a policy story, not just a war one, after Trump declared the US the guardian of the Strait of Hormuz and put a 20 percent fee on all cargo moving through it.

1. The memory blowoff cracked, and the clearest signal is a record nobody should want. SK Hynix fell more than 15 percent in Seoul, its biggest drop ever, the day after ringing the Nasdaq bell on its new US listing, and the whole complex followed, with $SNDK down about 10 percent and Micron off 5 and a half. The tell is on the Lark Davis read: SanDisk's monthly RSI (a momentum gauge where 70 is considered overbought) hit 99.3, the highest reading ever printed on any stock in history. That is not strength, that is a parabola running out of buyers.

The setup into tomorrow is almost a dare. The two new leverage products launch at 4am, a 2x long and a 1x short on a name that just fell 15 percent, and Leopold Aschenbrenner is reportedly buying $7 to $10 billion of the SK Hynix ADR at a 10 percent premium into the drop, per the same stream. Somebody is going to use those ETFs to pick a bottom in a stock still in free fall, and the exchange in Seoul had to halt trading to slow the selling. Respect the level, not the leverage.

2. SpaceX broke below its deal price, and there is a governance story hiding under the buy ratings. The cleanest new idea tonight is a short-seller's story about SpaceX. Per Patrick Boyle on Prof G Markets, $SPCX joined the Nasdaq 100 on fast-track rules and 18 of 19 analysts slapped a buy on it the same week, yet the stock fell below its $135 IPO allocation price to a post-listing low near $140, down 34 percent from its peak, leaving every retail buyer underwater. It trades around 101 times sales while burning roughly $5 billion a quarter and growing only about 15 percent.

The part worth your attention is why the ratings are so uniform. The price targets run from Morgan Stanley's $300 to Raymond James at $800, which implies a $10 trillion company at 542 times sales on a projection of $4.9 trillion in AI revenue by 2035. Boyle ties the unanimity to money, since SpaceX has roughly $235 billion of spending to fund by 2030 and no bank writing a sell rating wants to lose the fees. His flag is that the SEC quietly terminated the 2003 research-analyst settlement about seven months ago, the rule that separated analysts from bankers after the dot-com scandals, and he expects something ugly to surface in these numbers. Whatever you think of the stock, that is the real story.

3. The whole AI trade is priced to 2028, and the next hyperscaler earnings are the referee. The framing that ties the memory crash to the bull case comes from Ben Bajarin, who has covered semiconductors since 2000. His argument is that the industry may cross $1 trillion in annual revenue this year, four years early, and that the growth is almost all price per chip rather than more units, because the foundries run at 70 to 75 percent utilization by design and cannot conjure new wafer capacity. The catch is that everything is priced to a 2028 scenario, so the binary signal is whether the hyperscalers' next earnings say they are not slowing capital spending and show free cash flow inflecting positive.

The demand-destruction counter showed up in the tape today. Micron and Samsung printed what Bajarin calls some of the most absurd earnings ever and their stocks fell anyway, and global smartphone shipments dropped 11 percent to the worst second quarter in 13 years, blamed directly on the memory shortage pricing consumers out, per the People list. Higher prices per chip are wonderful until they start killing the units.

4. Crypto shrugged off the war again, but Bitcoin quietly lost a line. Bitcoin barely moved on the strikes and the oil spike, holding around $62,000, but it lost the $64,000 level that had been support, per the Crypto list. The Milk Road desk splits on what that means: John sees a bathtub bottom, a long boring grind toward $57,000, while Martin turned more bullish on the logic that if a war only knocks $1,000 off, the weak hands are already gone. The structural support is real, with whales buying 270,000 Bitcoin worth about $16 billion, three times what the ETFs sold, a Fidelity model floor near $56,500, and spot ETF inflows turning positive at $197 million to end an eight-week outflow streak.

The louder crypto story is a rotation. Money is leaving the Solana names and pouring into the Robinhood Chain and Ethereum ecosystem, per Blockworks, with Jito, Jupiter, and Metaplex all down 15 to 18 percent while Uniswap, Arbitrum, and their peers ripped. Uniswap is the cleanest beneficiary, now doing $5.2 million a day in fees with its fee switch finally turned on, which makes the token a revenue-sharing asset for the first time.

5. Two fresh single names, one a pharma giant and one a part you have never thought about. The pick with the most conviction behind it is Eli Lilly as the AI reinvestment play, framed as the next Google. The case, per Milk Road: $LLY is a $1 trillion company guiding revenue from $65 billion to $83 billion in a year, throwing off about $10 billion in free cash flow from Mounjaro and Zepbound, and plowing it into its own data center with more than 1,000 Nvidia chips, an AI drug-discovery platform, and a partnership with Google's Isomorphic Labs, with $75 billion committed to deals since 2023.

The stranger idea is bearings. Per a Morgan Stanley note, bearings are the one robotics part that is architecture-agnostic, since every motor needs them no matter how the robot is designed, and the firm sees the robot-bearings market growing roughly 300 times through 2050, with OpenAI listing precision bearings as one of six critical components in its hardware manufacturing request. A humanoid needs more than 70 of them, the top six makers control over half the market, and the US-listed pure play in that oligopoly is Timken ($TKR).

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $SPCX (SpaceX). Below its $135 deal price at a post-listing low near $140, down 34 percent from the peak, on 101 times sales with a governance question over its unanimous buy ratings, per Prof G Markets.
  • $MRVL (Marvell). Per Rick Orford, record Q1 revenue near $2.4 billion with Q2 guided to $2.7 billion and a $55 billion custom-silicon market by 2028, though it trades near 97 times forward earnings. It fell 5.5 percent with the semis today.
  • $TSLA (Tesla). Jefferies raised its target to $400 from $375 on a Q2 delivery beat, and the stock broke to 392 support into the July 22 print, per the People list.
  • $LLY (Eli Lilly). The pharma-as-AI-reinvestment name, guiding to $83 billion in revenue and building its own compute, per Milk Road.
  • $TKR (Timken). The US-listed way into the Morgan Stanley robot-bearings thesis of 300-times growth through 2050, per the MS note.
  • $AMZN / $SHOP. Per Rick Orford, the AI-commerce pair, with AWS at $38 billion of quarterly revenue and $14 billion of operating income, and Shopify growing 34 percent on more than $100 billion of merchandise volume at a richer 82 times forward earnings.
  • Seasonality. Per ClearValue Tax, 2026 is historically the weakest year of the cycle (median 5 percent, positive only 58 percent of the time) while 2027 is the strongest (median 22.5 percent, up 91.7 percent of the time), an argument for positioning now for next year.

Psychology. The single most revealing number today was not a loss, it was a record. SanDisk's monthly RSI hit 99.3, the highest reading ever recorded on any stock, on the exact day SK Hynix fell 15 percent and rang the Nasdaq bell, and cash on the sidelines sits near a record-low 0.42 percent of market cap. That is what a top-of-cycle crowd looks like from the inside, all-in on a parabola, treating a circuit-breaker crash in Korea as a dip to buy rather than a warning to heed.

The tell is in what the crowd did with the megacaps today. It did not sell the news, it hid in Apple at a $4.7 trillion record and told itself the semis crash was contained, the same reflex that has paid all month. Meanwhile the people who study this for a living, Boyle on SpaceX's rigged ratings and Bajarin on a trade priced to 2028, are pointing at exactly the assumptions the crowd has stopped questioning.

So the discipline check into CPI is about the leverage. The 2x SK Hynix ETF launching tomorrow exists to let you bet bigger on a name that just fell 15 percent, and a 99.3 RSI is the market telling you the easy buyers are spent. If you are tempted to catch the memory knife or fade the oil spike on the open, size it like the SanDisk chart is a warning and not an invitation, keep $57,000 on Bitcoin and 750 on the S&P in front of you, and let the CPI print come before you decide the crowd was right to stay all-in.

item.title: The memory trade is cracking in Seoul at the exact hour SK Hynix starts trading here, and oil, credit, and a short-seller are all leaning the same way
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: SK Hynix fell 10 percent overnight and Korea tripped its circuit breaker while Iran and Washington argue over whether Hormuz is open, oil sits up but contained, and Goldman warns the hyperscaler bond market is choking. Micron has a clean line to defend, semiconductor earnings start Tuesday, and Michael Burry is short the whole thing.
item.content:encoded:
SK Hynix fell 10 percent overnight and Korea tripped its circuit breaker while Iran and Washington argue over whether Hormuz is open, oil sits up but contained, and Goldman warns the hyperscaler bond market is choking. Micron has a clean line to defend, semiconductor earnings start Tuesday, and Michael Burry is short the whole thing.

The weekend did not resolve the war and this morning did not resolve the memory trade. Both arrive at the open as live risks, and for once the bear has company. Here is the pre-open picture, then five stories.

Pre-open preview. Oil is the first thing paying for the weekend. US crude is up 4 to 5 percent after Iran again declared the Strait of Hormuz closed and the US again declared it open, coordinating roughly 20 tankers through overnight, per the Market and Kalshi lists. The level to watch is a gap at $83.20 on crude futures, and Brent is still under $80, so this is a spike, not yet a crisis. Gold lost $4,100 as the dollar jumped, a risk-off-into-dollar move rather than a classic panic, per the Financial Markets list.

Equity futures lean slightly red, and several traders are already calling it a war rug-pull that closes green. The bigger tell is in Asia. SK Hynix fell 10 percent in Seoul, the KOSPI dropped 3 percent, and the exchange activated a sidecar to halt program selling, per the Financial Markets list. The calendar is loaded: SK Hynix starts regular-way $SKHY trading here today, the $SKHX 2x long and $SKHZ short ETFs launch Tuesday at 4am, and June CPI (the inflation print that settles the July 29 Fed debate) lands Tuesday morning, per the People list.

1. The memory supercycle is showing its first real cracks, and two of them are brand new. For a week the memory trade shrugged off everything. This morning it is not shrugging. Beyond the 10 percent SK Hynix drop and the Korean circuit breaker, two fresh datapoints cut against the "infinite demand" story, per the Stocks list. Murata is cutting prices on its MLCC capacitors, and Nvidia dropped the 800-volt direct-current requirement from its Rubin Ultra rack, a near-term headwind for the Vertiv and silicon-carbide names that rallied on exactly that spec.

Neither breaks the multi-year thesis, but both puncture the idea that nothing can go wrong. Micron now has a line to defend rather than a launchpad. Per Stocks with Josh, $MU failed to close above 996 over the weekend, so the upside target at 1089 is off the table until it reclaims 996, the stop is 950, and losing that opens 900 and then 861. The whole complex trades off whether Korea's selling washes into the US session or exhausts itself into the $SKHY debut.

2. The bear finally has a real thesis, and it is about debt, not just valuation. The fresh crack this morning is in credit. Per the Financial Markets list, the 2-year Treasury yield pushed above 4.23 percent, its highest since February 2025, and Goldman's trading desk is circulating a note about carnage in the hyperscaler bond market, warning it is very difficult to fund another $360 billion over the next year when the hyperscalers need roughly $1 trillion annually. Banks are sitting on their largest-ever net short in corporate bonds. And Michael Burry's AI shorts surfaced in the filings, roughly $456 million against $PLTR, $187 million against $NVDA, and about $100 million against the $SOXX semiconductor ETF.

The efficiency side of the bear case got louder too. A sharp Reddit thread lays out the pattern that keeps denting the Nvidia capex story: DeepSeek erased $589 billion of Nvidia market cap in a day last year on a cheap-training claim, GLM-5.2 is a 744-billion-parameter open model the maker says trained entirely on Chinese Huawei Ascend chips, and a robotics vision model matched a Meta model at a twentieth of the size. The honest counter is Jevons, that cheaper compute has so far expanded demand rather than shrinking it, so this is a genuine two-sided fight rather than a settled short. Either way, the multiples now have a coherent thing to be afraid of.

3. Semiconductor earnings start Tuesday, and they will referee the whole argument. The week's real judge is the tape from the chip names themselves. Per Jose Najarro, $AEHR (Aehr Test Systems) reports Tuesday after the close, $ASML Wednesday before the open, and $TSM (Taiwan Semiconductor) Thursday after the close. Aehr is the fresh story, having raised its full-year bookings guide above $92 million on a record $41 million production order from a lead hyperscale AI customer, with new silicon-photonics follow-ons and memory flagged as an emerging customer.

The other two set the tone for the whole trade. Najarro is explicitly not a buyer of $ASML at a forward price-to-earnings near 46 against a decade median around 32, though he stays long-term bullish, and he calls $TSM the swing factor, watching its capital-spending guide and whether it lifts 2026 revenue growth from above 30 percent toward 35 to 40. Strong TSM guidance pulls up $AMD, $AVGO, $MRVL, and $NVDA. A cautious one confirms the bears.

4. Opendoor is the cleanest new single-name idea, and a law nobody read handed it a gift. Per a detailed Reddit breakdown, the new housing law that markets treated as a blunt ban on institutional buyers actually blocks large investors from accumulating 350-plus single-family homes while expressly exempting homes that are newly built, renovated, or converted for sale and not held as rentals. That exemption is a statutory lane cut almost precisely around the $OPEN (Opendoor) business model, which turns inventory rather than building a rental empire, and it kills the cleanest regulatory bear case against the stock.

The operating turn underneath it is real, whatever the ugly headline numbers say. Homes purchased rose 45 percent quarter over quarter, Opendoor signed more than 5,000 acquisition contracts (double the prior quarter and the most since 2022), inventory sitting longer than 120 days fell from 33 percent to 10 percent, and fixed operating costs dropped from $39 million to $33 million. Management guided to roughly 25 percent sequential revenue growth and about breakeven adjusted EBITDA, targeting positive adjusted net income on a forward basis by the end of 2026.

5. Robinhood Chain flipped Hyperliquid over the weekend, and the stock has decoupled from crypto. The crypto-equity crossover keeps compounding. Per the Crypto list, Robinhood Chain overtook Hyperliquid in 24-hour decentralized-exchange volume, $433 million against $296 million, on more than 17 million transactions and about $250 million in deposits, and $HOOD is up 80 percent off its May lows and no longer tracking Bitcoin. A Substack breakdown adds the mechanics, that the chain runs on Arbitrum's technology with Ether as its gas token and is aimed squarely at tokenized real-world assets, with Robinhood's Vlad framing a listing on it as the new Binance listing. Watch whether the on-chain volume is real adoption or launch-week momentum, the same test the daily tape keeps setting.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $MU (Micron). Per Stocks with Josh, reclaim 996 to re-arm the move toward 1089, a stop at 950, and downside to 900 then 861 if Korea's selling carries over.
  • $AEHR (Aehr Test Systems). Reports Tuesday, having raised full-year bookings above $92 million on a record $41 million hyperscale order, with photonics and memory as new customers, per Najarro.
  • $OPEN (Opendoor). The housing-law exemption plus a Q1 operating inflection, guided to breakeven EBITDA and forward profitability by year-end, per the Reddit DD.
  • $NFLX (Netflix). Per Josh, needs to reclaim $76 to set up a bullish swing into Thursday's earnings, with a 65-to-76 range in play.
  • $HOOD (Robinhood). Up 80 percent off the May lows and decoupled from Bitcoin as its chain flips Hyperliquid, per the Crypto list.
  • WD-40. The anti-hype hedge, up 12 percent Friday on 24 percent revenue growth to $195 million and a raised full-year outlook, per a Barron's summary. Sometimes doing the basics well beats the AI trade.
  • Gold. Per Benjamin Cowen, the midterm-year pattern points to a bottom between July and October near his support band around 3,824, then a reacceleration into 2027.

Psychology. This is the first morning in a while where the tape is handing the reader more than one honest warning at once, and the test is whether the crowd can hear any of them. The memory complex is melting down in Seoul with a circuit breaker tripped, yet the loudest voices on the Stocks list are still posting $2,200 Micron and $3,250 SanDisk targets, and the reflex to call the oil spike a war rug-pull and buy the dip is already forming before the cash open. That reflex has paid every time this month, and that is exactly what makes it dangerous the one morning the cracks are real.

The countersignal is who is on the other side. Michael Burry is short Palantir, Nvidia, and the semis into this, Goldman is warning its own clients about the hyperscaler bond market, and Nvidia just quietly pulled a spec that a whole basket of stocks had rallied on. When the crowd is raising price targets while the credit market chokes and the early money builds shorts, the odds that this dip is different go up, not down.

So the discipline check into the open is to respect the possibility that today is not a rug-pull to buy but a first crack to size for. If you are tempted to add memory names on the SK Hynix debut or chase the oil trade, remember Micron has a line at 996 and a stop at 950 for a reason, and CPI lands tomorrow. Let $MU prove it can hold 950 and let oil prove the $83.20 gap before you decide the crowd was right to keep buying.

item.title: The memory trade is the only trade that mattered this week, and it survived a short-seller, a revenue miss, and a war without breaking
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Micron gapped up on a Burry short Monday, cracked 26 percent by Tuesday on a Samsung miss, then ran straight back over $1,000 on a $250 billion capex commitment and the biggest foreign listing in US history. Here is the full bull and bear case on Micron, SK Hynix, and the HBM supercycle, the names that ride it, and how crowded the boat has already gotten.
item.content:encoded:
Micron gapped up on a Burry short Monday, cracked 26 percent by Tuesday on a Samsung miss, then ran straight back over $1,000 on a $250 billion capex commitment and the biggest foreign listing in US history. Here is the full bull and bear case on Micron, SK Hynix, and the HBM supercycle, the names that ride it, and how crowded the boat has already gotten.

This was a one-story week. Iran and the Strait of Hormuz owned the headlines, but on the tape the entire market was a proxy for one question: is the AI memory shortage real and durable, or is it 2018 in a nicer costume. Every session this week answered it, and the answer kept coming back the same, so this memo goes all the way on it.

How the week actually played. Monday $MU (Micron) gapped up about 7 percent overnight after breaking ground on a high-bandwidth-memory plant in Hiroshima with Japanese government money, and in the same session Michael Burry disclosed a short and called the whole AI-semiconductor complex a bubble about to pop, per the Stocks list and this Burry breakdown. The stock faded the gap all day, a first warning.

Tuesday it cracked. Samsung posted a second-quarter operating profit up roughly nineteen times year over year and still missed on revenue, and the stock fell almost 8 percent and dragged the whole memory complex with it, per this account of the Samsung reversal. By the close it was the worst day of the year for the group, $SNDK down 14 percent, $MU through $900, and month-to-date $WDC was off 33 percent, $SNDK 30 percent, and $MU 26 percent, per the rotation note. A best-in-class quarter that gets sold is the market telling you the good news was already owned.

Then it healed, and fast. Wednesday the US bombed Iran and oil jumped 5 percent, and the crowd answered a group down 16 percent in two weeks by shoving a record $5.4 billion into the $SOXX semiconductor ETF in a single day, four times its old record. Thursday Micron committed more than $250 billion to US memory through 2035, up from $200 billion a year earlier, and the stock ran 6.5 percent back over $1,000 with shorts including Burry reportedly scrambling to cover, per the Micron capex breakdown.
