Friday SK Hynix priced the biggest first-time US share sale by a foreign company ever, and by Saturday its shares had run about 13 percent on day one on $18 billion of volume, per the week-levels note.

The bull case, in full. Start with the structure, because it is the whole argument. Three companies make the world's DRAM (the working memory in every server and phone), Samsung at 39.3 percent, SK Hynix at 28.9, and Micron at 22.8, so roughly 90 percent of supply sits with three names through 2027, per the melt-up note. Only those three make HBM (the stacked high-bandwidth memory that every AI accelerator needs), and SK Hynix says its HBM is sold out into 2027.

The valuation is the part that makes bulls loud. Gavin Baker's framing, carried in the same note, is that memory is the cheapest thing in the AI stack at 3 to 5 times earnings while power, cooling, and optical names carry multiples that imply a very different future, so one side is mispriced. Micron trades around 9 times earnings with contracts covering about half its revenue at floor prices above the last cycle's peak margins. Its Cloud Memory unit grew revenue from $3.4 billion to $13.8 billion in a year while operating margin went from 46 to 78 percent, per the capex breakdown.

Then the demand story, which the bulls stretch out for decades. Micron CEO Sanjay Mehrotra argues a humanoid robot carries ten times the memory of a self-driving car and that the real multi-decade demand cycle has not even started, per Lark Davis. The SK Hynix chair went further this week, saying doubling 2025 capacity within five years still will not meet demand and that memory is no longer a cyclical business. The year-to-date scoreboard is the bull case with no commentary, $SNDK up 707 percent, $MU up 243, and $WDC up 238, per the People list, and a mid-year audit of 30 finance YouTubers found the winners clustered on Micron, AMD, and Marvell while the losers sat in software names like Adobe and Salesforce.

The bear case, in full, because it is better than the bulls admit. The strongest bear document of the week was not Burry, it was a teardown of the HBM4 timeline. Stacking fourteen to sixteen memory layers drives manufacturing temperatures toward 260 degrees, warping the silicon and pushing yields below the roughly 65 percent break-even, and HBM3 took a year to pass Nvidia's qualification while twelve-layer HBM3E took nineteen months. The read-through is that real HBM4 volume likely lands in 2027 and 2028, not late this year, only Samsung has cleared baseline qualification, and one respected note even expects Micron to get zero initial HBM4 allocation from Nvidia. Micron itself says it has no line of sight to supply catching demand before 2028.

The rest of the bear case is timing and history. The 2018 memory glut is the reason nobody should treat 78 percent margins as permanent, and Micron's own new fabs are years from output, first Idaho wafers in mid-2027 and a second fab in late 2028. China is the wildcard, with CXMT testing next-generation bonded DRAM and closing the gap faster than expected, and the one thing that would truly crack the three-supplier scarcity story is Chinese DRAM sold cheap into the US. Burry's short sits on top of all of it, betting on roughly a 30 percent drop, and the honest version of his case is that 9 times earnings is a warning that earnings already peaked as often as it is a bargain, per Rick Orford's normalization take.

The tell that decides it. The cleanest signal all week was the Samsung reaction. A company can print a record quarter, up nineteen times on profit, and still get sold 8 percent, and that only happens when the good news is fully in the price. That cuts both ways. It means the easy money in this trade has been made, and it means the next leg needs genuinely new information, an HBM4 qualification, a pricing print, a capacity number, not just another headline about how big the cycle will be.

What the reader might actually do. If you already own the theme, this week was a gift and a warning at once. The gift is that the trade survived a short-seller, a revenue miss, and a war in the same five sessions, the way durable trends do. The warning is that you are now late enough that position sizing matters more than direction. The Kyber optics delay points the same way it did Monday, at $AMD, $MRVL, and $AVGO gaining as hyperscalers accelerate custom silicon, and at $AAOI keeping its pluggable transceivers bid for two more years.

On the single names, the cleanest expressions are still the obvious ones. $MU is the balance-sheet play, $SNDK is the highest-beta expression of the same trade at 707 percent year to date, and $SKHY, which converts to regular-way trading Monday, is the purest HBM margin story at a roughly 48 percent operating margin against Micron's 26. For levels, Josh's read on Micron is a bounce off $86 that ran to $99.60 and then failed to close above it, so he flips cautious into Monday with an air pocket toward $90 and $86.10, and a reclaim of $99.60 re-arms the move toward $108.90 and $125.

What to watch next week. Monday SK Hynix converts from the when-issued line to regular-way $SKHY. Tuesday the $SKHX 2x long and $SKHZ short single-stock ETFs launch, and June CPI (the inflation print that settles the July 29 Fed debate) hits the same morning. Netflix reports Thursday as the first big-tech print of the season. The one variable that would change the whole call is an HBM4 qualification result, because the bull case is priced for the shortage to run to 2028 and the bear case says the yields are not there yet, and the first real qualification number tells you which one is right.

Psychology. This week was a clinic in how a crowd behaves when a parabola cracks and then heals. Monday the timeline was posting $1,800 targets on Micron and $3,000 on SanDisk and saying Micron under $1,000 is a smash buy no matter what Burry does. That is exactly the sound euphoria makes right before a revenue miss takes 26 percent out of the group. The euphoria did not break the trade. A Samsung miss did, and the difference matters, because it means the crowd was not wrong about the story, only careless about the price.

Then came the more dangerous move, the capitulation-buy. After the worst day of the year the crowd put a record $5.4 billion into $SOXX in a single session with fund-manager cash at a twenty-year low. That is not fear, it is total conviction that every dip is a gift, and that belief is cheapest to hold right after the best trade in the market cracks. It is also the one that gets punished when the second leg down does not bounce.

The named portrait of the mood is on the Crypto list. A trader who lost $4.4 million on the Hynix run turned around and re-longed $30.17 million of the same exposure at 2x leverage, with another account opening a $20 million 3x Nvidia long into the weekend. That is a scorned position trying to get even, and it is happening while options sentiment sits at December 2020 highs and the Shiller PE (price against ten years of averaged earnings) kisses its dot-com record.

So the discipline check going into the new week is about your own hands, not the tape. The story is real and you can hold it with conviction, but ask whether you are adding because the thesis advanced or because the chart already ran, because those are different trades wearing the same ticker. If you are sitting on a loss from Tuesday's crack, size any re-entry like that loss taught you something rather than like the market owes you the money back. The levels are on the page, $99.60 on Micron, $195 to $200 on the Hynix line, the CPI print Tuesday. Let the trade come to you instead of chasing it into the ETF-inflow record.

item.title: Iran says the strait is closed, Trump says it is open, and oil already gapped, so the calm trade meets its first real bill Monday
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Brent settled near $76 after a 5 percent week and ran further on the weekend tape while crossings fell to a tenth of normal, and futures are called moderately red into a loaded week. Underneath the war, the AI-power supply chain got a concrete map, the memory trade heads into its biggest catalyst week yet, and the market started asking whether the AI spend actually pays.
item.content:encoded:
Brent settled near $76 after a 5 percent week and ran further on the weekend tape while crossings fell to a tenth of normal, and futures are called moderately red into a loaded week. Underneath the war, the AI-power supply chain got a concrete map, the memory trade heads into its biggest catalyst week yet, and the market started asking whether the AI spend actually pays.

Market's closed, so this is the setup for a week that arrives with a live oil shock and a full catalyst calendar on top of it. The weekend did not settle the war, it escalated it, and it handed the tape a real gap to open into. Here is the week ahead, then five stories.

The week ahead. The overnight fact is a standoff over the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's Revolutionary Guard says it is closed until further notice, and on Meet the Press Trump countered that the strait is open to commercial shipping, with the US military saying it is positioned to keep it open, per the Kalshi and Market lists. The tape is not waiting for the words to agree, Brent settled near $76 after a 5 percent week and ran about 3 percent further on the weekend 24-hour venues, per the Crypto list, while commercial crossings fell to roughly 11 a day, about a tenth of the normal run rate.

The rest of the week is heavy on its own. SK Hynix converts to regular-way $SKHY trading Monday, the $SKHX 2x long and $SKHZ short ETFs launch Tuesday alongside June CPI (the inflation print that settles the July 29 Fed debate), $NFLX and $ASML report, and Tesla reports July 22. Futures are called moderately red into Monday absent a weekend peace headline, per the Market AM list, so respect the gap rather than fading it blind.

1. The oil shock is real but the structural bears are already leaning on it. The bull read is simple, a fifth of the world's seaborne oil moves through Hormuz and the throughput just collapsed, so any sustained closure gaps crude and everything levered to it. The counter is that Trump and CENTCOM are publicly calling the strait open, and Mike McGlone's commodity framing on the Financial Markets AM list puts Brent resistance near $83 against a base case that rotates back toward $40 as US and Canada near a 9-million-barrel-a-day surplus by 2027.

So the trade is a spike to fade rather than a new bull market in oil, and the refiners that already ran, $VLO and $MPC at 52-week highs per the Financial Markets PM list, are the crowded expression. Watch Monday's open for whether the gap holds past the first hour or fills.

2. The memory trade heads into its biggest catalyst week with a mechanical bull case underneath it. The $DRAM memory ETF crossed $25 billion in assets for the first time ever, per the Kalshi AM list, and the SK Hynix listing mechanics are the story into Monday. Per the Stocks PM list, the roughly $26 to $28 billion ADR was more than seven times oversubscribed and funds sold $MU, $SIVE, and $AAOI to fund it, so the midweek Micron wobble reads as a rebalance rather than a thesis break.

The conviction is getting stretched in a way worth naming. Dylan Patel now floats $MU at $4,000 on memory capacity growing only 20 to 30 percent a year against demand that doubles, per the Stocks AM list, and Jordi Visser points at Samsung trading near 4 times earnings on a 19-fold profit jump as the cheap way in, per his long market read. The oligopoly math is unchanged, Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron hold about 90 percent of DRAM through 2027. The mechanical rebound is a real trade, the $4,000 target is a story you do not need to own the stock, and the honest move is to keep those two separate.

3. The AI-power trade finally has a named supply chain, not just a slogan. The most useful new map of the week, per Dylan Patel's breakdown carried by Melvin: AI racks are moving from 48-volt to 800-volt direct-current power because the current at a megawatt rack would melt copper otherwise, and that transition creates four investable layers. The first is power semiconductors made of silicon carbide and gallium nitride replacing silicon, with Infineon estimating about $175,000 of chip content per megawatt of rack power against nearly zero today, and the names are $IFNNY, $ON, $WOLF, $NVTS, and $STM.

The other three layers are just as concrete. Power conversion runs through $VRT (Vertiv, Nvidia's lead 800-volt architectural partner), $ETN, and $MPWR. The grid-to-site gear is $GEV (GE Vernova), whose orders are running at twice its shipment rate, a forward-demand tell. And behind-the-meter generation, bypassing the five-year grid-connection queue, is $BE (Bloom Energy). The number that frames the whole trade, per the same thread, is that solar plus four hours of battery now costs $57 a megawatt-hour against $102 for new gas, so the power gets built wherever it is cheapest and fastest.

4. The market started asking the uncomfortable question: does the AI spend actually pay. The bill-shock stories piled up this weekend. Joseph Hogue's seven-stock breakdown cites a Fortune 500 firm overspending $500 million in a single month, Meta burning 60 trillion tokens in 30 days, and Uber blowing its annual AI budget in three months, and points the trade at the cost-control layer, $NET (Cloudflare, AI gateway), $NOW (ServiceNow, agent orchestration), $DDOG (Datadog, agent observability), and $PLTR. The counterweight is real, a PwC survey found 56 percent of CEOs report no revenue or cost benefit from AI yet, the "pilot purgatory" Chamath flagged on the Crypto PM list.

The bull rebuttal is that the spend is real adoption rather than waste. Per Visser's read, SemiAnalysis's own AI bill went from under $100,000 to about $11 million annualized in six months once Claude Code became usable, and his frame for the whole tape is to short abundance (anything a computer can replicate) and stay long scarcity (memory, compute, power). The drawdown, he argues, was a speed crash, a sentiment cleanse, not the top. This is the debate that decides the second half, and both sides now have real numbers.

5. Amazon's custom silicon quietly became a $20 billion business, and the neocloud moat got more expensive. Per Rick Orford, Amazon's custom-chip business (Trainium, Graviton, and Nitro) hit a $20 billion annualized run rate in the first quarter on triple-digit growth, Trainium 3 is nearly sold out, and Anthropic has committed up to 5 gigawatts and OpenAI about 2 gigawatts of it through AWS. That is Amazon building a real second source to Nvidia inside its own cloud, and $MRVL rides it on a multi-year supply deal.

The neocloud side got a moat update from the Milk Road weekend note: the cost to build a gigawatt of compute jumped from $50 billion to $100 billion in about three months, and Nvidia is backstopping the GPU financing for the neoclouds, acting as landlord and bank at once. Melvin's blunt version is that if you are not buying $NBIS (Nebius) or $CRWV (CoreWeave), you are missing the names with locked capacity nobody can afford to replicate.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $SOFI (SoFi). Per Invest with Henry, CEO Anthony Noto has bought roughly 130,000 shares this year at an average near $17.29, about $2.5 million of his own money, with the stock at $18.78 and Q1 revenue up 41 percent to $1.1 billion. Still below the $20 line that turns the chart.
  • $NOW (ServiceNow). Per Stocks with Josh, a trade setup off a 36 percent drawdown with Nvidia publicly endorsing the partnership, a 95 to 110 near-term range, a stop at 87.50, and 136 as the upside target.
  • $TSLA (Tesla). Into the July 22 print, June saw Model Y take the top sales spot across China, the UK, Switzerland, and Australia, and Japan up 183 percent year over year, while the AI5 chip reached tape-out on Samsung's 2-nanometer Texas line, per the People PM list.
  • $GLXY (Galaxy Digital). Per the Crypto PM list, roughly $25 against a $38 to $41 fair value on the Helios data-center contract with CoreWeave, with a refinancing and a second tenant as catalysts.
  • $BABA (Alibaba). Per Hogue, the open-source play, with Qwen past 700 million downloads and up about 20 percent in three weeks, as Chinese models pressure US labs on price.
  • Bitcoin. Standard Chartered reiterated $100,000 by December but buried a warning that below $60,000 the selling pressure builds, with $57,000 as the level that held as the cycle low, per Lark Davis. Novogratz frames a $65,000 to $85,000 range with $60,000 as the line that decides it, per the Crypto PM list.

Psychology. The tell this weekend is a market that spent a week congratulating itself for not caring, walking straight into the first thing it actually has to price. Bitcoin fell a few hundred dollars on Iran declaring the strait closed and recovered half of it within an hour, per the Crypto AM list, and the loudest voices on the memory trade are posting $4,000 Micron targets and a record $25 billion in the DRAM ETF at the exact moment the group heads into a five-catalyst week. That is what late-cycle confidence looks like, a crowd so well trained to buy the dip that it stops checking whether this dip is different.

The countercurrent worth respecting is that the people who moved early are getting cautious rather than bolder. Michael Saylor sold about 3,588 Bitcoin, his largest since 2020, Jordi Visser sold his Micron in mid-May well before the top and only plans to buy it back, and the smart-money power trade is rotating into unglamorous names like Vertiv and GE Vernova rather than adding to the leaders. When the early money is trimming winners and the late money is raising targets, the tape is telling you which end of the trade you are standing on.

So the discipline check going into Monday is about the gap. If you are tempted to buy the memory names or Tesla on the open because every dip this month has paid, remember you would be buying the most crowded book in years into an oil shock the market has not settled and a CPI print two days out. Size for the gap, keep the levels in front of you, $60,000 on Bitcoin, $20 on SoFi, Brent's $83, and let Monday show you whether the crowd was right to stop caring or just lucky until now.

item.title: Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz closed overnight, so the calm the market bought all week meets a real oil gap at Monday's open
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The IRGC says the strait is shut until further notice after a warning shot, and futures inherit that Monday morning while a fifth of the world's oil hangs on it. Underneath, the memory trade has a new mechanical bull case, Galaxy Digital looks cheap on one data center alone, and the smart money is rotating into Tesla ahead of earnings.
item.content:encoded:
The IRGC says the strait is shut until further notice after a warning shot, and futures inherit that Monday morning while a fifth of the world's oil hangs on it. Underneath, the memory trade has a new mechanical bull case, Galaxy Digital looks cheap on one data center alone, and the smart money is rotating into Tesla ahead of earnings.

Market's closed, so this is a setup, and the setup changed overnight. Iran formally closing the Strait of Hormuz is the kind of headline the tape spent all week deciding not to care about, and now it lands with a fresh number attached. Here is the week ahead, then five stories.

The week ahead. The overnight wildcard is oil. Per Kobeissi on the Stocks list, Iran's Revolutionary Guard declared the Strait of Hormuz closed until further notice late Saturday after firing a warning shot at a vessel, and Monday's open inherits that gap risk with a fifth of the world's seaborne oil moving through that chokepoint. Monday also brings SK Hynix converting to regular-way $SKHY trading and the Senate returning to move the CLARITY Act (the crypto market-structure bill) in its narrow window before the August recess.

Tuesday is the heavier day. June CPI (the inflation print that settles the July 29 Fed debate) hits pre-market, and the $SKHX 2x long and $SKHZ short single-stock ETFs launch the same morning. Earnings season widens with $NFLX, $ASML, and $UNH on the calendar this week, and $TSLA reports July 22. Watch $64,000 on Bitcoin as the binary the Stocks list keeps flagging, a hold points to $70,000 to $71,000 and a loss opens $57,000 to $58,000, with John Gillen leaning toward the lower path in cash.

1. The memory trade has a new bull case, and it is mechanical, not narrative. The read on the Stocks list reframes last week's Micron wobble as a plumbing event, not a thesis break. SK Hynix's Nasdaq listing raised somewhere near $26 to $28 billion and was more than seven times oversubscribed, and funds sold $MU, $SIVE, and $AAOI to free up cash to buy it, on top of thirteen straight days of foreign outflows from Korea worth $15 to $20 billion. If the selling was to fund a subscription rather than to exit the trade, the bounce is mechanical once that flow clears.

The conviction around Micron itself is getting loud. MentoviaX calls this the strongest earnings cycle in the company's history, and Jordi Visser and Milk Road Macro are now floating a $2 trillion Micron within a year with a $4,000 price target. That is the sound of a crowded trade, and the honest way to hold it is to separate the mechanical rebound (real, and tradeable) from the $4,000 call (a story you do not need to believe to own the stock). The torque names riding the same theme, per the same list, are $OPTX, $ASYS, $SIVE, $SHMD, and the interconnect shovels $AAOI, $COHR, $CAMT, $AMKR, and $SNDK.

2. Marvell got a Goldman number that makes the AI-networking trade concrete. Per Melvin on the Stocks list, Goldman now models the AI networking market growing roughly ten-fold from $15 billion to $154 billion by 2028, and $MRVL is the name pointed at it. Marvell did $8.195 billion of revenue in its last fiscal year, up 42 percent, and guides to about $11 billion next year and $15 billion the year after, with the data center now 76 percent of the business and more than 50 custom-chip wins including Google's TPU, Amazon's Trainium, and Meta. This is the same memory-and-compute buildout viewed from the wiring, and the interconnect basket ($AAOI, $SIVE, $SMTC, $CRDO, $MRVL) is where it expresses.

3. The smart money is rotating out of memory and into Tesla ahead of earnings. The rotation call on the Stocks list is explicit: Milk Road Macro moved capital into $TSLA, which ran about 10 percent last week while Micron lagged, and calls it the most popular position on its desk into the July 22 print. The catalyst is real, with Texas legalizing statewide robotaxi operation, so the Cybercab story now has a jurisdiction to prove itself in. This is the same pattern as Friday's neocloud-and-power rotation, money leaving what already ran to buy what has a fresh catalyst, and Tesla into earnings is the highest-beta version of it.

4. Galaxy Digital looks cheap on one data center, and a smaller name sits on a binary. Per the Chairman's Ledger post on the Stocks list, $GLXY (Galaxy Digital) should be worth about $38 on its Helios project alone against roughly $25 today, with a bull case of $41. Helios is an 800-megawatt data center leased to CoreWeave on a fifteen-year contract worth about $900 million of annual EBITDA (operating cash earnings), and a refinancing from 9.1 to about 7 percent would roughly double the free cash flow to equity, with a second tenant expected by end of summer.

The riskier cousin is $NUAI, a behind-the-meter Texas play where a new CEO just bought $5 million of stock at a 20 percent premium. A hyperscaler letter of intent would take it from $5 toward $20 to $30, a five-to-six-times binary that cuts both ways.

5. Amphenol is the boring AI name doing record numbers. Per Rick Orford, $APH (Amphenol) makes the connectors and interconnects that hold an AI rack together, and the business is running hot, record quarterly sales of $7.66 billion up 58 percent, record orders of $9.4 billion, and a book-to-bill of 1.24 (new orders running ahead of shipments, a forward-demand tell). The catch is the price, 42 times earnings and 11 times book, so this is a quality-at-a-premium name rather than a value one, and it is diversified enough across defense, aerospace, and autos that it is not a pure AI bet. Real fundamentals, rich multiple, pick your framework.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $GLXY (Galaxy Digital). Per the Stocks list, roughly $25 against a $38 to $41 fair value on the Helios and CoreWeave data-center contract, with a refinancing and a second tenant as the near-term catalysts.
  • $TSLA (Tesla). The desk's rotation trade into the July 22 print, up 10 percent last week on the Texas statewide robotaxi approval, per the Stocks list.
  • $MRVL (Marvell). The AI-networking pure play with a Goldman target market of $154 billion by 2028 and guidance to $15 billion of revenue, per the Stocks list.
  • $APH (Amphenol). Record $7.66 billion in sales and a 1.24 book-to-bill, trading at 42 times earnings, per Rick Orford.
  • $SOFI (SoFi). Per Stocks with Josh, rejected again at $19.90 to $20 and needing two daily closes above $20 to turn bullish, with $17.75 as the support pivot that flips it range-bound if lost.
  • $POET (POET Technologies). Per Josh, back in his $7.30 to $8.22 accumulation zone after resetting from above $20, and he is buying without a reversal signal yet.

Psychology. The whole week was a market teaching itself not to flinch, and this weekend is the test of whether that was discipline or just fatigue. The tape spent five sessions buying into a war, a memory crack, and a short-seller, and by Saturday one trader on the Stocks list had a $2 trillion Micron target on a name that fell 26 percent midweek. Now Iran has actually closed the Strait of Hormuz, and the reflex that worked all week, shrug and buy the dip, is about to meet a real oil gap at Monday's open.

That is the trap of a crowd that got rewarded for ignoring risk. The lesson it learned was that nothing matters, and the one time that lesson is most expensive is the first time something does. If you are tempted to pre-load Monday longs into the memory names or Tesla because the dip has paid every time, remember you would be buying the most crowded positioning in years into an oil shock nobody has priced.

So the discipline check this weekend is simple. The mechanical Micron rebound and the Tesla rotation are real trades with real catalysts, but they are not reasons to be maximally long into a Monday that could open with crude gapping and the whole complacency trade getting its first honest test. Size for the gap, keep the levels in front of you, $64,000 on Bitcoin, $20 on SoFi, $7.30 on POET, and let the open tell you whether the crowd was right to stop caring.

item.title: The crowd got more stretched today while the smart money quietly sold its winners and bought power and neoclouds
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Global equity funds took in $56.4 billion, the Shiller PE is on the verge of its dot-com record, and Berkshire is sitting on $397 billion of cash, yet the rotation underneath sold Robinhood and Hyperliquid into strength and bought Nebius, CoreWeave, and Vistra. SK Hynix's ADR ran 13 percent on day one, Robinhood Chain flipped Base, and Ethereum's ratio against Bitcoin broke an eleven-month downtrend.
item.content:encoded:
Global equity funds took in $56.4 billion, the Shiller PE is on the verge of its dot-com record, and Berkshire is sitting on $397 billion of cash, yet the rotation underneath sold Robinhood and Hyperliquid into strength and bought Nebius, CoreWeave, and Vistra. SK Hynix's ADR ran 13 percent on day one, Robinhood Chain flipped Base, and Ethereum's ratio against Bitcoin broke an eleven-month downtrend.

Market's closed, so this is about positioning, and the split widened today. The visible crowd kept piling into the same longs while the money that actually moves rotated out of this week's winners and into the power-and-compute plumbing. Here is the week ahead, then five stories.

The week ahead. Monday the Senate returns and SK Hynix converts to regular-way $SKHY, with the $SKHX 2x long and $SKHZ short ETFs launching Tuesday, per the Financial Markets list. Tuesday is June CPI, still the week's binary. $NFLX reports Thursday July 16 as the first big-tech print, with options implying a 8.3 percent move after the stock fell in 11 of the last 13 weeks. The July 29 Fed meeting sits 66 percent priced to hold, and the CLARITY Act (the crypto market-structure bill splitting SEC and CFTC oversight) gets roughly three Senate working weeks before the August recess, with Polymarket at 40 percent it passes, per the Crypto list.

1. The real tell today was the rotation: sell the crypto and fintech winners, buy the neoclouds and the power names. Milk Road's PRO desk ran 16 trades this week and the direction is clean. They sold $HOOD (Robinhood) at $112.79 for a 55 percent gain and $HYPE (Hyperliquid) around $71 for 135 percent, then bought $NBIS (Nebius) at $199 and $CRWV (CoreWeave) at $81 to $82. That is profit-taking on the retail-flow names and re-entry into the companies that rent out GPUs.

The demand data underneath the neocloud bet is real. The same desk logged Google Cloud crossing $20 billion in a single quarter, up 63 percent year over year with backlog nearly doubling to $462 billion, and AWS growing 28 percent on a $150 billion run rate with Bedrock now past 125,000 enterprise customers. If those backlogs are real orders, the picks-and-shovels layer gets paid before the application layer does.

The power trade is the other half of the same idea. A Reddit DD lays out $VST (Vistra) at a forward PE near 15 and a PEG (price-to-earnings against growth, under 1 is cheap) around 0.5, with its Cogentrix acquisition and power deals signed with Amazon, Meta, and a Kuwaiti sovereign fund. Tom Nash's five-stock list lands on the same seam from the hardware side, naming $VRT (Vertiv, data-center cooling) with revenue up 27 percent and operating income up to $2 billion, a ten-fold gain in three years. Data centers need power and cooling before they need another model, and the market is finally pricing that in order.

2. Robinhood Chain did not just take half of Solana, it flipped Base. The update from the Crypto list: Robinhood Chain now does more daily DEX volume (decentralized-exchange trading) than Base, Coinbase's own chain. Deposited assets crossed $300 million, up about 300 percent week over week, and roughly $100 million of Ether has been bridged in, a ten-fold jump, because the chain uses $ETH as its gas token. That last detail is the one to hold, a successful Robinhood Chain is now a live source of new Ether demand.

The adoption numbers are early but loud. The chain has 194,000 daily active addresses and 100-millisecond block times against Solana's 400, and its 37,000 tokenized-equity holders already make up 6.9 percent of everyone holding tokenized stock anywhere, a week and a half after launch. The August test from the AM edition still stands, hold the volume or it was a launch-week spike, but the trend since this morning went straight up.

3. The memory supercycle keeps compounding, and Monday it gets its own trillion-dollar ticker. SK Hynix's US-listed shares ran 13 percent on their first day on $18 billion of volume, the biggest ADR debut since Alibaba, and the name now sits in the trillion-dollar club, per the Financial Markets and People lists. BNP's framing is that this is still 1998 in the analogy, early in the cycle rather than the blow-off top.

Micron gave the numbers that justify the story. Per a detailed Reddit breakdown, $MU raised its planned US investment past $250 billion through 2035 and its Cloud Memory unit grew revenue from $3.4 billion to $13.8 billion in a year while operating margin went from 46 percent to 78 percent. It also committed $500 million to GlobalWafers for a Texas facility on a ten-year supply deal. The year-to-date scoreboard tells you where the crowd already is, $SNDK is up 707 percent, $MU 243 percent, and $WDC 238 percent, per the People list.

One caution worth keeping. The fab timing is years out, first Idaho wafers in mid-2027 and a second fab in late 2028, and the 2018 memory glut is the reason nobody should treat 78 percent margins as permanent.

The names that actually worked this year back the thesis anyway. A mid-year audit of 30 finance YouTubers found the winners clustered on Micron, AMD, and Marvell, with the top performer up 101 percent on Micron and Marvell and the runner-up up 83 percent on an AMD position that itself gained 165 percent. The losers sat in software and consumer names like Adobe, Salesforce, and Nike. The hardware-and-power trade is not a forecast, it is already the year's scoreboard.

4. The AI-capex bill finally got a real number, and the hyperscalers do not all pass. A sharp reverse-DCF post worked the discounted-cash-flow math backwards, asking what growth each hyperscaler's price already assumes. To justify $362, $GOOGL has to compound revenue at 25.7 percent a year, eight points above consensus, or lift margins from 38 to 52 percent, so the stock is priced to beat the Street. $MSFT screens the other way, a fair value near $483 against a $383 price, meaning the selloff built in a cushion. $META sits right on its line around $637 versus $603.

That squares with what moved Meta this week. Per the Market list, the re-rate has a named cause, Muse Spark 1.1 shipping at roughly a quarter of OpenAI and Anthropic pricing, and the stock reclaimed its 200-day average per the Kalshi list. Cheap inference is the whole bull case, and the market is paying for it while quietly pricing the $1.4 trillion in state penalty demands at zero.

5. Ethereum's ratio against Bitcoin broke its downtrend, and Bitcoin sits on the line that decides July. The fresh technical, per the Crypto list: ETH/BTC posted its first weekly close above an eleven-month downtrend, with whales accumulating 270,000 Bitcoin around $59,000 and a relative-strength target near $76,000. On Ether's own chart the level to hold is $1,820. The demand sink behind it is concrete, digital-asset treasury companies now hold about 7 percent of all Ether supply and throw off $400 million to $500 million a year in staking revenue they are bound to reinvest, per Milk Road.

Bitcoin itself is unchanged and coiled. Hold $64,000 and the path is $70,000 to $71,000, lose it and $58,000 opens, and John Gillen still leans toward the lower path while sitting in cash. Standard Chartered's Geoff Kendrick put a longer marker down, $100,000 by the end of 2026 and $200,000 by the end of 2027, per the Financial Markets list. Near-term the CLARITY vote is the catalyst and $64,000 is the referee.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $PLTR (Palantir). Tom Nash's top pick, 56 percent revenue growth, 360 percent operating-income growth, and a rule-of-40 score of 140, now down about 50 percent from its November 2025 high near $130. High multiple, real growth, cheaper than it was.
  • $NFLX (Netflix). Reports Thursday with options implying an 8.3 percent move, down 35 percent across 11 of the last 13 weeks, per the Financial Markets list. Oversold into the first big print of earnings season.
  • $APP (AppLovin). Per Milk Road, 59 percent revenue growth and 85 percent EBITDA margins, trading near $527 against a $660 fair-value estimate, but weighed by SEC scrutiny and roughly $195 million of insider selling in 90 days. The open question is whether its AXON ad engine works outside gaming.
  • $VPG / $AMBA (Vishay Precision, Ambarella). Lark Davis's robot picks, a tactile-sensor maker with book-to-bill at 1.21 and an edge-AI vision-chip name still priced as an auto supplier before any robotics re-rating.
  • $BOT / Agility Robotics (Robo Strategy fund, Agility). The same list flags the closed-end $BOT trading at a 3-times premium to its net asset value, a real risk, while Agility (the Digit humanoid in Amazon warehouses) comes public by SPAC around September as the first pure-play public humanoid.
  • $SPCX (SpaceX). Now the sixth-largest name in the Nasdaq 100 a month after its IPO at a $2.14 trillion cap, with Cathie Wood adding 116,971 shares, per the People list. It round-tripped below its open today, and Milk Road stopped out at $155.80 after Meta signaled it will build its own compute. The AI-pivot story is real (a $6.3 billion Reflection AI compute deal at $150 million a month) but the stock trades at 115 times sales.
  • $CRWD vs $S (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne). Per a head-to-head breakdown, CrowdStrike is the steadier compounder with roughly 6 percent to its analyst target while SentinelOne carries up to 48 percent upside as the higher-risk catch-up trade.

Psychology. The cleanest read today is the gap between who is buying and who is selling. The visible crowd added to the most crowded book in years, global funds took in $56.4 billion in a week and the Shiller PE (price against ten years of averaged earnings) neared its dot-com record, per the Kalshi list, while Berkshire sat on $397 billion of cash and Milk Road's desk sold its 55 percent and 135 percent winners into the strength. When the fast money is ringing the register on $HOOD and $HYPE and the slow money is holding a record cash pile, the tape is telling you the easy part of this move already happened.

Retail is doing the opposite, panic-bridging onto Robinhood Chain as it goes vertical and re-longing the same memory names that burned traders this week. The bias that setup breeds is chasing, buying the 194,000-address chart or the $SNDK-up-707-percent screen precisely because it already ran. The Hynix revenge trade from this morning is the portrait of where that ends.

So the weekend check is about your own hands. If you are tempted to buy something this weekend, ask whether you are buying it because the thesis is fresh or because the chart already paid someone else. The rotation into $VST and $NBIS is people selling what worked to buy what has not moved yet, and that is the harder, better trade than adding to the vertical. The levels are on the page, $64,000, $1,820, the CPI print Tuesday. Let them come to you.

item.title: Options traders are the most bullish since December 2020 while the rate market prices a coin-flip on a hike, and Tuesday's CPI decides who is wrong
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The Shiller PE is at the dot-com line, dealers are pinned long into Monday, and the futures curve quietly gives the Fed real odds of hiking this year. Hynix goes regular-way Monday, Netflix opens earnings season Thursday, and a revenge trade in the memory complex tells you where the crowd's head is.
item.content:encoded:
The Shiller PE is at the dot-com line, dealers are pinned long into Monday, and the futures curve quietly gives the Fed real odds of hiking this year. Hynix goes regular-way Monday, Netflix opens earnings season Thursday, and a revenge trade in the memory complex tells you where the crowd's head is.

Market's closed, so today is about the setup, and the setup is stretched in one direction. The crowd spent the week getting maximally long while the rate market started pricing the opposite scenario, and this coming week carries the catalysts that settle it. Here is the week ahead, then six stories.

The week ahead. Monday is loaded. The Senate returns July 13 with roughly three working weeks to move the CLARITY Act before the August recess, SK Hynix converts from the when-issued $SKHYV line to regular-way $SKHY trading, and CBOE opens pre-market options trading on the megacaps ($TSLA, $NVDA, $META, $GOOGL, $AMD, $AAPL, $AMZN, $MSFT, $PLTR, $INTC), per the Stocks list.

Tuesday is June CPI, the week's binary, with Capital Flows expecting a disinflationary print. $NFLX reports Thursday July 16 as the first big-tech print, Josh's levels are a $76 reclaim to get long against a range down to $65. The July 29 Fed meeting is priced 66 percent hold, 34 percent hike.

On the tape itself, $SPY closed pressing 755 where dealers sit on $5.9 billion of positive gamma (hedging flow that pins price), with 752 as the line that cancels the up move. And Iran's deadline to publicly declare the Strait of Hormuz open lands today, with Kalshi pricing 38 percent odds oil trades above $74.99 by end of July.

1. Everyone got long at the same time, and the bond market is the only one hedging. The positioning stack, per Kalshi and the Stocks list: options traders at their most bullish since December 2020, hedge funds bought tech at the fastest pace since October, dollar positioning the most bullish since 2015, $600 billion of market cap added in three hours Thursday, and Kalshi gives 69 percent odds the S&P touches 8,000 this year. Barchart adds the valuation kicker, the Shiller PE (price against ten years of averaged earnings) is on the verge of taking out its dot-com record. Mr_Derivatives notes the VIX is the lowest since January 12 and is already positioning for an August vol event.

Now the other side of the ledger. Capital Flows points out the July 29 meeting is priced 66/34 hold versus hike with about 51 basis points of hikes priced to the terminal rate, the first time in years the market genuinely cannot decide if the next move is up. His plumbing detail: the December 2026 SOFR contract (futures on the overnight funding rate, the market's Fed-path bet) is now the largest by open interest in SOFR history, up 28 percent in about two months, and the 2026/2027 calendar spreads price just 6 basis points of cuts. If the Fed overtightens into disinflation, those spreads reprice violently, and that is his asymmetric trade.

His base case is not bearish, and the data backs him: nominal GDP running 5.8 percent, ISM manufacturing accelerating five to six prints in a row, industrials and financials at all-time highs. The point is narrower. The crowd is long at dot-com valuations while Kalshi prices a 50 percent chance the Fed's next move this year is a hike, and Berkshire is sitting on a record $397 billion of cash. Somebody in that picture is mispositioned, and Tuesday's CPI is the referee.

2. The memory trade gets its second act Monday, and the chart underneath it just went on probation. Follow-through from the debut, per the Market list: the SK Hynix chair says doubling 2025 capacity within five years still will not meet demand, his words being that memory is not a cyclical business anymore. Regular-way $SKHY trading starts Monday, Mr_Derivatives bought the when-issued line targeting $195 to $200, and the $SKHX 2x long and $SKHZ short ETFs launch Tuesday, per the People list. The supply concentration is the thesis in one stat: Samsung at 39.3 percent, Hynix at 28.9, and Micron at 22.8 hold about 90 percent of DRAM through 2027. Micron itself committed $250 billion to US memory build-out, per the Financial Markets list.

The chart is less cooperative than the story. Josh's read on $MU: the bounce off his $86 call ran to his $99.60 target, but the week failed to close above it, so he flips bearish into Monday, watching an air pocket toward $90 and $86.10 with $95 as his stop on longs. A reclaim of $99.60 early in the week re-arms the move toward $108.90 and his $125 breakout target.

Around the complex, $SNDK has been the best mover on the memory-supercycle theme with Monday decisive per ripster and Wujastyk, and Rosenblatt reports the optical shorts in $AAOI and $LITE plan to cover in late July and flip long for the 2027 co-packaged-optics cycle, per the Market list. One framing to keep from Capital Flows on X: $MU is trading on fundamental flows while $NVDA trades as market beta, so the memory names are where the actual AI-demand signal lives.

3. Bitcoin is still parked at $64,000, but the seller has a new name: the miners. The fork is unchanged from yesterday, per the Crypto list, low $70,000s if $64,000 keeps holding, a flush into the high $50,000s if it gives way, and Gillen himself leans toward the lower path while sitting in cash. What is new is the supply story. Saylor says miners are rotating their fleets into AI compute and selling their Bitcoin to fund it, and declares the four-year halving cycle broken.

Lyn Alden puts numbers on the decoupling: Bitcoin tracks global M2 (worldwide money supply) about 83 percent of the time, and this is the 17 percent, with treasury-company absorption gone and the AI chip trade draining the marginal liquidity. The relative charts agree, Bitcoin sits at a 29-month low against the S&P and gold at an 11-month low against it, per Mr_Derivatives.

The counter-current is institutional. Matt Hougan describes retail at peak despair while he logged the most investor meetings in a single day this year, and Bitwise colleague Ryan Watkins calls this the most excited he has been since 2023. On the policy calendar, SEC Commissioner Hester Peirce still expects the CLARITY Act (the SEC-versus-CFTC jurisdiction bill) to pass this summer while Polymarket prices 39 percent, and one of them is simply wrong, per the People list.

The overnight lists add the machinery: CFTC chair Selig calls the bill must-pass, French Hill holds a New York field hearing this week, and Senate Democrats led by Warren want hearings on Trump's crypto profits instead. Watch one liquidity flag under all of it, the BoJ balance sheet is down $726 billion from peak, and yen carry unwinds have a habit of showing up in crypto first.

4. Robinhood Chain took over half of Solana's DEX volume in 48 hours, and now it has a date to prove it is real. The numbers, per aixbt: $1 billion of DEX volume in the first 48 hours, more than half of Solana's daily total, while Solana printed its 2026 volume low on July 9. App deposits crossed $300 million, up 300 percent week over week, with 37,000 tokenized-equity holders already 6.9 percent of everyone who holds tokenized stock anywhere, per tokenterminal. The test is dated: hold $400 million-plus of daily volume by August 10 or the migration is churn, with Blast the cautionary comp after its $2.9 billion TVL bled 70 percent in 90 days.

The retail-agent angle is the next leg, Robinhood will let US users connect third-party AI agents to trade crypto, per the Polymarket list, and Kraken is relaunching its app around agentic trading. The mood on the ground is pure FOMO, threadguy describes panic-bridging onto the chain while the bridge buckled under demand. For the institutions, Blueprint Finance CEO Nick Roberts-Huntley (ex-Point72) told Milk Road that BitGo is committing custodied assets into his Concrete vaults without leaving qualified custody, quoting over $1 billion by year-end, and he sees $ETH at new all-time highs within a couple of years. ETH staking just hit an all-time-high 33.5 percent of supply.

5. Meta added $230 billion in two days, and the legal demand sitting across from it is $1.4 trillion. The bull print, per the People list: about $230 billion of market cap added in under 48 hours after Muse Spark 1.1 shipped, from a superintelligence team that cost roughly $15 billion to assemble. MilkRoadMacro's valuation frame is that $META carries a PEG of 0.87 (price-to-earnings against growth, under 1 is cheap for the growth) versus Microsoft at 1.34 and Google at 1.47, and CheddarFlow flagged unusual million-dollar far-out-of-the-money call buying in $META and $MSFT into the weekend. The SemiAnalysis argument driving it: Meta is the only hyperscaler on track to be world-class at data, talent, and compute at once, with 3,000 engineers building nothing but reinforcement-learning environments.

Hold that against the Prof G segment on the same company: the four-state penalty demand runs to $1.4 trillion, a number that would consume over 90 percent of the company's value, with nearly 30 parallel state cases in federal court, $375 million already lost in New Mexico, and child-access bans spreading across 40-plus countries. The guest's frame is that Meta is in its big-tobacco moment, with whistleblower documents showing it knew the harm and kept going. Both stories are live at once. The market is paying up for the AI lab and assigning the litigation a zero, and litigation zeros have a way of becoming numbers.

6. XRP just printed a reading it has shown once in twelve years, and the confirmation level is spelled out. Lark Davis walks the data: the weekly RSI (momentum oscillator) hit oversold for only the second time in $XRP's history, the second-lowest reading ever, and the first occurrence preceded one of its most violent rallies roughly two years later. MVRV (aggregate holder profit and loss against cost basis) sits at a record negative 45 to 47 percent, the shape of prior capitulation bottoms. Flows are diverging from the majors, $1.49 billion of cumulative inflows into seven US spot XRP ETFs and 970 million XRP locked while Bitcoin ETFs bled hundreds of millions daily, with active addresses up 72 percent in two weeks.

His gate is unambiguous, a weekly close back above the 200-week moving average, the long-run trend line XRP has spent three of the last ten years below. Until that prints, the data is a setup and nothing else. The fundamental hook is Garlinghouse claiming Ripple touches $16 trillion in annual payments and clearing with digital assets at essentially zero percent of it, plus the proposed XLS-65 and XLS-66 lending protocols for borrowing against tokenized treasuries on-chain.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • Healios Pharma (small-cap, roughly $400 million). Per Rick Orford, lead compound HLP-003 is deuterated psilocin for major depressive disorder, dosed twice total. Phase 2 showed 74 percent immediate remission and 71 percent still in remission at 12 months, versus 4-point-something score improvement for J&J's $2 billion-a-year Spravato. Phase 3 readout guided for calendar Q4 2026, enrollment at 88 percent, FDA breakthrough designation in hand, on the White House priority-voucher shortlist, funded through 2027 after an oversubscribed raise, patents to 2041.
  • $RKLB (Rocket Lab). Per the Stocks list, Peter Beck cut his salary to $1, took no bonus, and canceled 392,155 RSUs, while the Iridium acquisition adds roughly $872 million of 2025 revenue.
  • $POET (POET Technologies). Per Josh, the prior 200 percent runner has fully reset from above $20 to about $8, his accumulation zone is $7.30 to $8.22 on the weekly, and he is buying without a reversal signal yet.
  • $CRM (Salesforce). Per Josh, range defined at $148 support and $172 resistance with a doji rejection at the 9-day average near $170. Through $172 and the measured path opens toward $184.
  • $DKNG (DraftKings). Per Mr_Derivatives, August NFL seasonality is the trade and Michael Burry is long.
  • $CRCL (Circle). The federal trust-bank charter is now approved, and the stock is still down 20 percent over six months, per the People list. Policy tailwind, broken chart, pick your framework.
  • $HSCDF / $NCTKY (Harmonic Drive / Nabtesco). Per MilkRoadMacro, the humanoid-robot picks-and-shovels pair: Harmonic Drive holds 85 percent of strain-wave reducers, Nabtesco about 60 percent of the other gear type every robot needs, while Unitree's robot price fell from $87,000 to $25,000 with a sub-$4,000 model coming.

Psychology. One trade from the overnight tape says everything about where the crowd's head is. Per lookonchain, a trader who lost $4.4 million on the Hynix run turned around and re-longed $30.17 million of the same exposure at 2x leverage, and a second account opened a $20 million 3x long on $NVDA perps into the weekend. That is not analysis, that is a scorned position trying to get even, and it is happening while options sentiment sits at December 2020 levels and the Shiller PE kisses its dot-com record.

The crypto room is the mirror image. Hougan's peak-despair-plus-packed-calendar observation and Watkins locking in his bullishness describe a market where the retail crowd left exactly as the suits arrived, and Saylor's miners are selling the asset to buy AI compute at the top of the other crowd's euphoria.

So the check this weekend is simple. If you are tempted to pre-load Monday longs so you do not miss the 755 break, remember you would be buying the most crowded positioning in six years two days before a CPI print, with the Fed openly discussing hikes. If you are sitting on a loss from this week, the Hynix revenge trade is your cautionary portrait, size the re-entry like the first loss taught you something. The levels are on the page, $64,000, $99.60, 752. Let the tape come to them.

item.title: Meta ripped through $650 on BofA's half-price buildout math, the crowd threw $200 billion at the Hynix book, and crypto's bulls and bears just agreed on a second-half plan
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: BofA says Meta is adding AI capacity at $22 billion a gigawatt, half the going estimate, and the stock recaptured its 200-day on the news. Bitcoin is sitting on the $64,000 line with dated bottom calls stacking beneath it, and the Treasury Secretary gave crypto its policy speech of the year. Here is the session.
item.content:encoded:
BofA says Meta is adding AI capacity at $22 billion a gigawatt, half the going estimate, and the stock recaptured its 200-day on the news. Bitcoin is sitting on the $64,000 line with dated bottom calls stacking beneath it, and the Treasury Secretary gave crypto its policy speech of the year. Here is the session.

Two stories owned the tape today, Meta's melt-up and the SK Hynix debut, and they are the same bet on AI infrastructure at two different prices. The quieter action was in crypto, where the bulls and the bears spent the day converging on the same trade. Here is the close, then six things.

The close. Risk-on around two anchors. Per the Market list, Meta ran more than 6 percent through $650 into the $660s, recapturing its 200-day moving average (the long-term trend line traders treat as the bull-bear divide) with a gap left to fill at $668.50, while the SK Hynix ADR began when-issued trading around midday under SKHYV after this morning's $149 pricing.

The index-level gate has not moved: Mr_Derivatives argues that if Nvidia, still near bear-market territory, and Microsoft, in one, turn up, the S&P can test 7,900 to 8,000, since the two are roughly 12 percent of the index. Bitcoin held just above $64,000, the level tonight's whole crypto argument turns on. One flag into tomorrow: a Prof G Markets guest argued that oil is priced for the best case in the Middle East, so yesterday's Iran relief is carrying more weight than it should.

1. Meta went vertical because BofA did the capex math and got half the number everyone feared. Per the Market list, BofA's note pegs Meta's new AI capacity at roughly $22 billion per gigawatt, 6.5 gigawatts added on $145 billion of capex, against the prior $45 billion per gigawatt assumption. The kicker is the resale case: if Meta sells half that capacity at $15 billion of revenue per gigawatt, that is up to $150 billion of incremental revenue, and anything under $30 billion per gigawatt of cost beats Amazon's and Google's cloud economics of $10 to 16 billion per gigawatt. BofA's own caveat: the in-house Iris chip only starts manufacturing in September, so the savings barely touch 2026 costs.

The tape treated the note as a regime change. Cathie Wood bought about $21 million of the stock, Jim Cramer called the model change worth 100 points, and amitisinvesting frames it as a 33 percent grower at 18 times forward earnings with $700 to $750 in play into the July 29 report, where the sell side already models $200 billion-plus of 2027 capex. Meta also broke ground on another giant data center yesterday, per the Teradyne episode. The dissent came from inside the same feed: WisemanCap's reaction was "everyone is long META now? Give me a break." Hold that thought for the close of this note.

2. The Hynix book hit $200 billion, and the valuation skeptics showed up the same afternoon. Per the Market list, Bloomberg's detail on the debut is heavier than this morning's: roughly $200 billion of demand across 500-plus accounts, with the top 25 taking about two thirds of the deal, and JPMorgan lit its headquarters with the South Korean flag. Daniel Newman calls it a multi-year memory run with supply constraints past 2030, and the rack math backs him, HBM (the stacked memory AI accelerators feed on) climbing from low hundreds of thousands of dollars per AI rack toward $1 million-plus with Nvidia's Rubin Ultra generation. Cramer expects a wave of analyst initiations Monday when SKHY converts to regular-way trading.

The counterweight landed the same day. Rick Orford reads Micron at 9.2 times earnings as the market pricing a cycle peak rather than offering a bargain, memory being a business where supply eventually catches up and margins compress, and he makes the same normalization argument about Nvidia's below-peer multiple. Cheap multiples on cyclical AI hardware are a warning label as often as an invitation. For the debut itself, Mr_Derivatives offers last month's SpaceX analog: SPCX popped 17 percent intraday on day one and closed up 7, so the shape of the first print is usually the froth, and the real vote comes later.

3. Bitcoin is parked on $64,000, and for once both sides put dates on the calendar. Milk Road's John Gillen maps it cleanly: hold $64,000 on the retest and the move runs to $70,000 to $71,000, lose it and the sweep goes to $58,000 with the channel bottom near $57,000. He leans toward the lower path, and he ties the fork to Washington: the CLARITY Act (the bill that decides whether the SEC or the CFTC regulates crypto) sits at 40 percent odds on Polymarket, down 25 points, with the Senate back July 13 and roughly three working weeks before the August recess as the last realistic 2026 window. Heisenberg independently flags $60,000 holding and $70,000-ish on a breakout, two sources landing on the same upside number.

The bottom-callers got specific too. Lark Davis says the monthly stochastic RSI (a slow momentum oscillator) is bouncing from the exact zone that marked the 2015, 2018, and 2022 bear bottoms, and separately that Bitcoin is its most oversold against gold since roughly 2011, a condition that preceded a multi-year rally of about 660 percent. Benjamin Cowen runs the bear case with the same destination: 2026 is tracking the 2018 midterm-year script nearly beat for beat, realized price near $53,000 (the average cost basis of every coin, a level midterm years historically dip below) argues one more leg down, and his bottom window is late September to early October. Note what just happened there. The loudest bull and the loudest bear both end at "accumulate the second half."

On the Saylor overhang, Ansom and Matt Hougan make the same read: Strategy sold $225 million into the low, the market absorbed it, so the seller everyone feared is priced. And Tom Lee's frame for the whole complex is that crypto today is where memory stocks were before the parabola, the ignored downstream AI trade, though he holds nearly 5 percent of ETH's supply through BitMine, so weigh the source with the claim.

4. The Treasury Secretary told markets who writes the rules of money, and a stablecoin bank got its charter the same week. The item that came in hot this afternoon: Jordi Visser says the required reading is Scott Bessent's speech and Mohamed El-Erian's New York Times op-ed calling it remarkably important. Bessent's line, verbatim: digital assets, stablecoins, tokenization, and new payment systems will help shape the future of money, and the United States should not consign itself to the sidelines while that future is built elsewhere. Matt Hougan distills it as principle three, America will write the rules of the next economy. Visser's practical conclusion is that with agentic commerce coming you can no longer afford to have no view on crypto.

The follow-through was concrete. Per the Market list, Circle received final OCC approval (the national bank regulator) for Circle National Trust, a federally regulated trust bank positioned to manage USDC reserves. And Lark flags Fed chair Kevin Warsh adding Marc Andreessen to a Fed task force, his blunt read being that the liquidity people are now the crypto people.

5. The Ethereum setup is stacking, and the trigger is a daily close above $1,810. Lark's trade is precise: long from $1,750 with a stop at $1,700, a daily close above $1,810 validates a double-bottom breakout, and the target is $2,100 near the 200-day. Tom Lee bought 20,000 more ETH into it. The institutional case behind the chart came from John Gillan, six years a BlackRock VP on the Aladdin platform: BitMine and SharpLink together hold about 7 percent of ETH's supply throwing off $400 to 500 million a year in staking rewards, the new EthLabs org is backed by both, and Standard Chartered's Geoff Kendrick told him ETH reaches $40,000 by 2030. Gillan's own nearer call is a resolution above $5,000.

Keep the two honest counterweights in frame. David Hoffman, one of Ethereum's loudest longtime voices, sold his ETH and debated Gillan on it. And Gillan concedes the Clarity Act has drifted toward protecting the banks, with BNY's $50 trillion custody franchise and Jamie Dimon actively fighting public chains. The bull case here is real and it is also crowded with people paid to believe it.

6. The rotation chatter got loud: out of AI software, into physical AI and the beaten-up middle. Trader Mayne's one-liner captures the mood, AI software has run its course and physical AI owns the next five to ten years. The specifics were everywhere today. Per the Market list, the photonics complex is being bottom-called with names attached: Lumentum sold out roughly two years, likely into 2029, Applied Optoelectronics holding a $471 million-per-month revenue projection for late 2027, AXT controlling about 40 percent of indium phosphide substrate supply (the base material for optical chips), and Lee_Trades counting a 40 percent swing off the panic lows. The same list ran the humanoid-robot pairings map, Mitsubishi with Highlanders by 2027 joining Figure with BMW, Apptronik with Mercedes, and Optimus with Tesla.

The neocloud crowd is pressing. Investor wliang holds 86,000 shares of $WYFI at a $34.50 average and is targeting 100,000, likes $NBIS, $IREN, $WULF, and $CIFR, and calls the TeraWulf-Anthropic deal validation of the whole thesis. Lark is adding Marathon at its 200-day and notes Galaxy owns the biggest single US data center at 1.66 gigawatts while still being priced like a crypto company. Orford's caution belongs next to all of it: IREN is still becoming an AI infrastructure company, capital-intensive and unproven, and that description fits half the basket.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $GRAB (Grab). Per Joseph Hogue, the Southeast Asia super app at $3.93: Q1 revenue up 24 percent to $955 million, loan book up 130 percent to $1.4 billion, 2026 EBITDA guided above $700 million, Street average target $6.10. Earnings August 10 with options pricing almost no move, and his trade is the January $3.50/$4.50 call spread at a $0.43 net debit.
  • $TER (Teradyne). Per Milk Road AI, half of a chip-test duopoly (35 percent share against Advantest's 65) with 66 percent of test revenue AI-linked and 100,000 robots deployed. The most honest detail: the analyst pitching it will not buy at this valuation even after a 30 percent drawdown.
  • $PENG (Penguin Solutions). Per Market Bubble, a strong quarter, raised full-year guidance, and a CXL angle (the interconnect standard for pooling memory across servers) in a different lane from the Hynix-Micron crowd.
  • $UNI (Uniswap). Per Lark, the picks-and-shovels winner of Robinhood Chain's volume surge since the flow routes through it, fee switch now live. His long from $2.91 already hit the $3.50 target, next level the 200-day at $3.91.
  • $CRCL (Circle). Per the Market list, the OCC trust-bank approval makes it the cleanest equity expression of the Bessent policy story above.
  • $LITE (Lumentum). Per the Market list, the leader of the photonics-bottom call, with order books described as sold out into 2029.
  • $JTO (Jito). Per 0xResearch, its JTX self-custodial trading front end launches Monday July 14, moving Jito from backend plumbing to the interface layer where Solana interfaces already beat Binance's best pricing by 7 to 8 basis points. A dated catalyst on a beaten-down sector.

Psychology. Read today's two crowds side by side. In AI hardware, the money threw $200 billion at a $26.5 billion book, JPMorgan hung a flag on its building, and even the bulls were exasperated, WisemanCap's "everyone is long META now" being the sound of a trade getting heavy. In crypto, Cowen counts crypto YouTube at about 400,000 views a day against 3 to 4 million in 2021, apathy deep enough that the marginal seller may simply be done. Euphoria in one room, boredom in the other, and the interesting fact is that the boredom trade is the one where Lark the bull and Cowen the bear now agree on the plan, accumulate through the second half with defined levels.

So the discipline check tonight is about which impulse you feel. If it is the urge to chase Meta up 6 percent or buy the Hynix open, notice that the entry only feels safe because everyone else already took it, and the SpaceX debut's fade from plus 17 to plus 7 shows how day-one froth resolves. If it is the urge to look away from crypto because it has been dead money, notice that both camps just handed you levels, $64,000 and $1,810, with stops and dates attached. Trade the plan that exists at the price nobody is excited about, and size the crowded one like the crowd can leave.

item.title: SK Hynix debuts on the Nasdaq today as the biggest US listing ever by a foreign company, and overnight the Iran oil panic that ran the tape yesterday quietly reversed
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The memory IPO priced at $149 for a $26.5 billion raise that topped Alibaba, and it trades when-issued this morning under SKHYV. While it did, oil slid on fresh US-Iran deal talk, the yen roared on a repatriation pledge, and every member of the Magnificent Seven except Apple sits in a double-digit drawdown. Here is the pre-open.
item.content:encoded:
The memory IPO priced at $149 for a $26.5 billion raise that topped Alibaba, and it trades when-issued this morning under SKHYV. While it did, oil slid on fresh US-Iran deal talk, the yen roared on a repatriation pledge, and every member of the Magnificent Seven except Apple sits in a double-digit drawdown. Here is the pre-open.

Yesterday afternoon the story was Trump killing the ceasefire and oil ripping toward $80. Overnight it flipped. Trump said Iran called to make a deal, crude slid, the yen soared, and the whole risk picture cooled into the one event the market actually cares about this morning, the largest US listing ever by a foreign company. Here is the pre-open, then the six things that matter into a Friday with Delta reporting and CPI next week.

The pre-open. Set up green but thin. The cash close yesterday was strong, with the S&P 500 up 0.81 percent to 7,543.64 and the Nasdaq up 1.30 percent to 26,206.89 per the People list, and semis led with the SMH up 2.5 percent, Micron up 4.5 percent, and Sandisk up 7.6 percent. Overnight the macro backdrop softened in the bulls' favor. Per the Financial Markets list, gold slipped below its perch above $4,100 on less hawkish Fed minutes and fresh US-Iran optimism, and in Tokyo the 10-year JGB yield fell 10 basis points to 2.775 percent with the yen soaring, adding about $174 billion to Japanese stocks at the open. Per Stocks with Josh, jobless claims came in strong at 215,000, Delta reports this morning as the first real read on the consumer, and next week's CPI is the number that decides the tape into big-tech earnings. The SPY has to close above 752 today to keep its breakout alive.

1. SK Hynix debuts today, and the book is the biggest ever for a foreign name. This is the event. Per the People list, the SK Hynix ADR priced at $149, sold 177.9 million shares to raise $26.5 billion, and drew demand near $200 billion, making it the largest first-time US share sale by a foreign company ever, past Alibaba's $25 billion in 2014. The top 10 accounts took about half the deal. It trades when-issued this morning under SKHYV and converts to regular-way trading under SKHY on Monday, with the leveraged single-stock ETFs starting Tuesday and options later in the week. Trading is expected to open around midday, so the opening print does not exist yet, and anyone quoting one before then is guessing.

There are two cleaner ways to play it than chasing the open. Per the Stocks list, SK Square holds a treasury that is mostly SK Hynix stock and trades at roughly a 49 percent discount to that stake, with the US listing as the re-rate catalyst, and the Korea proxy is the KOSPI itself, now bouncing off its weekly trend with 6,800 to 7,100 as the line that is either defended here or lost for a long time.

2. The overnight reversal cooled oil and split the Fed read. Respect the flip. Per the People list, crude slid after Trump said Iran had called to make a deal with Qatar and Pakistan mediating, the exact opposite of yesterday afternoon's oil-toward-$80 move. Per the Financial Markets list, the softer tone came with the yen repatriation trade, as Katayama pledged to push the GPIF into Japanese assets and JGB yields fell across the curve. The catch is the Fed is not sending one signal. Ed Yardeni read the same minutes as confirming a hawkish pivot with the S&P stuck near 7,500, and Verdence's Megan Horneman still warns the market is underpricing a second-half rate hike. A cooler Middle East helps stocks, but it does not settle whether the next Fed move is a hike.

3. Sandisk is the new memory leader, and the whole complex is leaning one way. The standout was not Micron. Per the People list, Sandisk ran about 9 percent intraday to close up 7.6 percent on a leaked Meta memo detailing multi-year flash-storage supply plans, and Wedbush lifted its target to $2,000 against a stock already up roughly 800 percent this year. Micron closed up 4.5 percent and Barchart's line is that it will make more money in 2026 than in the prior 35 years combined. Per the Stocks list, the fresh Samsung angle is a reported talk with Anthropic to make a custom AI chip on Samsung's 2nm node. The entire memory and semi trade is now crowded to the same side into the Hynix print, and that is exactly when a leader like Sandisk at 800 percent becomes the risk as much as the reward.

4. Meta set up for July 29, and the Mag 7 is quietly in a drawdown. The megacap tape has a clean level and a warning underneath it. Per the Stocks list, Meta hit $639.36 after hours and finished up 4 percent, with resistance stacked at 642, 656, 673, and 691, a close stop at $615, and targets of $700 to $750 into its July 29 earnings, where the sell side now models more than $200 billion of 2027 capex. Nvidia is pointed at Heisenberg's 208.65 to 210 near-term target, matching yesterday's upside line. The caution is breadth. Per the Financial Markets list, Ben Carlson notes every Magnificent Seven name except Apple is in a double-digit drawdown from its high, with Microsoft down 29 percent, Meta down 20, Tesla down 17, and Nvidia down 14. The index is holding while its generals are bleeding, and that gap closes one way or the other.

5. The power trade got called a gift, and a bear put a number on the glut. The neocloud fight sharpened. Per the People list, Morgan Stanley's Stephen Byrd called last week's power-stock selloff completely wrong and a gift, with TeraWulf trading below $4 per watt against $19 of deal value and Cipher Mining carrying a $48.50 target versus $23, on new deals at 16 to 20 percent yields. TeraWulf is seeking $3.5 billion to fund a data center leased to Anthropic and is up 81 percent over six months. Per the Stocks list, IREN starts delivering its Horizon 1 site to Microsoft in about 10 days, turning on revenue from its contracted book. The bear got specific too. Per the Financial Markets list, Bernstein now counts 12 years to clear the 338 gigawatt data-center pipeline, up from 10 a month ago, and that is the glut case in one number.

6. Bitcoin holds while Robinhood's chain eats Hyperliquid. The structure firmed and the venue map shifted. Per the Crypto list, Bitcoin is holding around $61,300 to $63,000, and Matt Hougan reframed the Saylor scare, that Strategy sold $200 million and the price barely moved because Strategy is no longer the marginal buyer, with Bitcoin actually rallying on the news. The bigger move is on-chain. Robinhood's chain did $433 million of 24-hour DEX volume against Hyperliquid's $296 million and topped $1 billion in two days, with 194,000 daily active users, though 95 percent of that is still crypto-native and almost none of the 28 million Robinhood retail users have bridged over yet. And in a notable turn, JPMorgan walked back its Strategy warning and now says the real threat to Bitcoin is TradFi permissioned chains like its own Kinexys, not Strategy.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $SNDK (Sandisk). Per the People list, the memory leader of the day on a leaked Meta flash-supply memo and a Wedbush target hike to $2,000, up about 800 percent this year. The theme's highest-beta expression, so it moves hardest in both directions.
  • $WULF (TeraWulf). Per the People list, Morgan Stanley's top power pick below $4 per watt versus $19 of deal value, now raising $3.5 billion for an Anthropic-leased data center, up 81 percent in six months.
  • $META (Meta). Per the Stocks list, a clean setup into July 29 earnings, resistance 642 to 691, stop $615, targets $700 to $750, with the sell side modeling $200 billion-plus of 2027 capex.
  • $NFLX (Netflix). Per the Stocks list, down 1.7 percent after hours as the Journal reports it is exploring live TV channels and bundles with engagement softening, and eyeing sports and World Cup rights. A pivot story before it is a number.
  • $VLO $MPC $PBF (refiners). Per the Financial Markets list, all three sit at 52-week highs even as crude slid, the tell that the strength is in crack spreads and product demand rather than the barrel.
  • $KWEB (China internet). Per the Stocks list, posting its best month since September, with $NIO flagged as the next China name to move. A rotation trade, so it lives and dies on the group.

Psychology. Look at where the conviction is pooling this morning. Per the People list, the entire memory and semi complex is bid into the SK Hynix debut, Sandisk is up 800 percent on the year, Micron is being told it will out-earn its last 35 years combined, and Morgan Stanley is calling the power selloff a gift. Every one of those is a reason to be long, and they are all the same trade wearing different tickers. When a debut is oversubscribed seven times and the proxy and the leader and the analyst are all leaning the same direction, the crowd is not diversified, it is concentrated and telling itself a story about breadth.

Now hold the honest number next to the euphoria. Per the Financial Markets list, every Magnificent Seven name except Apple is already in a double-digit drawdown, Microsoft down 29 percent, while the index sits near a record because a handful of names carry it. That is the market quietly doing two things at once, celebrating at the index level and bleeding underneath. The discipline today is not to fight the Hynix trade, it is to size it like what it is. Ask whether you are buying memory because the setup is good or because everyone in your feed already owns it, and remember that the safest-feeling trade in the room is usually the most crowded one. If you need the debut to pop to feel right about the position, the position is already too big.

item.title: The ceasefire Trump floated at 1am was dead by afternoon, oil ripped back toward $80, and the tape still spent the day buying chips into SK Hynix's debut tomorrow
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: This morning the market bought the war on a deal tweet. By the close Trump said it is over and called Iran scum, crude reversed 6 to 7 percent higher, and rate-hike odds jumped to 56 percent, and none of it stopped the biggest one-day semiconductor rotation in months. Here is the close, then the six things that matter into Friday.
item.content:encoded:
This morning the market bought the war on a deal tweet. By the close Trump said it is over and called Iran scum, crude reversed 6 to 7 percent higher, and rate-hike odds jumped to 56 percent, and none of it stopped the biggest one-day semiconductor rotation in months. Here is the close, then the six things that matter into Friday.

The morning story flipped on itself. At 1am Trump said Iran wanted a deal and the selloff went green. By afternoon he said the ceasefire was over, the US launched another round of strikes, and oil that had been falling on the deal hope ripped 6 to 7 percent back toward $80. And yet the tape did something strange. It kept buying semiconductors all day, straight into SK Hynix listing tomorrow. Here is the close, then the six things that matter.

The close. Green where it counts, and the count is chips. Per the Financial Markets list, crude that sold off on the morning deal talk reversed hard once Trump killed the ceasefire, with WTI back near $74 and Brent near $78, and Mohamed El-Erian flagged the cross-asset tell that oil is now dragging global bond yields around more than the Fed is. Per the Kalshi list, the odds of a rate hike before year end climbed to 56 percent and the NY Fed survey has one-year inflation expectations up another 3.7 percent, the hottest in years. The dovish Fed minutes from this morning already look dated. Under all of it, the money rotated into the one trade that does not care about Tehran.

1. SK Hynix lists tomorrow, the deal got upsized, and Samsung's drop is the warning. The lead moved overnight. Per the People list, the SK Hynix ADR priced at $149, about 3 percent above the Korea close and more than 7 times oversubscribed, and the raise was lifted to roughly $28 to $29 billion, which if it holds is the largest ADR listing on record, topping Alibaba's $21.8 billion in 2014. The leveraged ladder is set, with 2x-long $SKHX and 1x-short $SKHZ following Monday. Per the Stocks list, Jim Cramer's warning is to price it in the hole or watch hedge funds fade it.

Here is the counter nobody wants next to the hype. Per the People list, Samsung guided to a record quarter, 89.4 trillion won in sales and 17.1 trillion in operating profit, and the stock fell anyway on softer AI-memory expectations. A record print that gets sold is exactly the read-through you carry into tomorrow's debut.

2. Micron committed $250 billion and the whole semi complex ran with it. The rotation was real and it had a leaderboard. Per the Stocks list, Micron raised its US investment plan to more than $250 billion through 2035 with the first pour at its Clay, New York fab, targeting roughly 40 percent of its DRAM made domestically, and $MU jumped about 6.5 percent back over $1,000 a share with shorts including Michael Burry reportedly scrambling to cover. Per the Market list and the People list, the one-day tape ran deep, $AEHR up 14 percent, $LITE up 13, $KLAC up 12, $ARM up 11, $AMAT up 10, $AMKR up 10, $MRVL and $MU up 7, $AMD up 6, $ASML and $INTC up 5. BofA's read is that AI spending is shifting from the hyperscalers to the chipmakers, and today the tape agreed.
