3. Meta went from too much compute to not enough, and the neoclouds ripped. This is the reversal that reframes the week. Per the Stocks list, a week ago the fear was Meta had excess compute, the story that sank the neocloud names. Today the report is Meta plans to double compute to 14 gigawatts by 2027 and starts building its in-house Iris chip, designed with $AVGO and made at $TSM, in September. Per the Market list, Wolfe now models Meta guiding to roughly $220 billion of 2027 capex against a $160 billion street number, and the read-through lifted $NBIS, $IREN, and $WULF. The level to watch is $META holding $600 after it round-tripped from a $577 morning dip back to $606.

4. Nvidia and AMD both sit on lines into Friday. The two biggest names have clean levels. Per the Stocks list, $NVDA has to hold above 198.6 into Friday or the setup reverses down hard, with the upside retest at the 50-day near 209, tighter than this morning's 200-day-near-$190 frame. Per the People list, $AMD is trying to break 542 to 551 toward a new high, now carrying a bigger market cap than JPMorgan and Walmart and about 90 percent of the way to a trillion dollars. Hold the lines and the rotation has room. Lose them and the whole complex gives it back.

5. Oil coming back is the quiet risk to everything else. Respect the crude tape. Per the Financial Markets list, the moment the ceasefire died, oil reversed its morning drop and pushed 6 to 7 percent higher, Qatar halted its LNG ramp after the Hormuz strikes, and Goldman's note is to watch the oil path over the crude spot number because escalation or negotiation decides the next leg. Sustained crude above $75 puts the inflation channel back in play right as the Fed's hike odds are climbing. The bulls got their chip rotation. The bears got their oil.

6. Bitcoin's bottom signals keep flashing while Saylor becomes a seller. The structure firmed even as the biggest holder flipped. Per the Crypto list, Bitcoin sits near $62,750, up about 2.6 percent on the week, and Scott Melker says almost every indicator seen at prior bottoms is hitting now, the 200-week average, oversold RSI on the high timeframes. Lyn Alden sharpened her call, that capital rotates back to crypto only once the fast money finishes selling, and her trigger is the AI names topping out. Per 0xResearch, Bitcoin took the Iran shock like a tech stock, down 2.5 percent at worst then steady, while $350 million of altcoin longs got liquidated Wednesday.

The seller is the story though. Per the Crypto list, Michael Saylor sold Bitcoin for the first time, about $216 million, to fund his STRC dividend, and blames the coin's lag on miners rotating into AI compute and selling their coins to pay for it. Barclays still initiated $MSTR a buy at $130 and now calls it a fintech, not a crypto proxy. And in a real shift, Robinhood put a perpetuals exchange in front of its 28 million customers and picked Lighter, not Hyperliquid, which knocked $HYPE.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $VRT (Vertiv). Per Rick Orford, the cleanest AI-power and cooling name for the Nvidia GB200 racks, first-quarter sales up 30 percent to about $2.65 billion with margins up 430 basis points, and it raised full-year guidance to $13.5 to $14 billion. It trades near 48 times forward earnings, so the growth has to keep showing up.
  • $IREN. Per Rick Orford, the Bitcoin miner turning its renewable campuses into AI cloud, now sitting on a $9.7 billion Microsoft deal and a $3.4 billion Nvidia deal with Dell supplying Blackwell racks. The re-rate is whether the market prices it as AI infrastructure instead of a cyclical miner.
  • $APP (AppLovin). Per Milk Road, the ad marketplace trades near $527 against a probability-weighted fair value around $660, with revenue up 59 percent and an 85 percent EBITDA margin, and the stated plan is to start buying under $500 and go full size near $400 to $430. The whole bet is whether the AXON engine generalizes past gaming, and the CEO just sold $51 million of stock.
  • $NBIS (Nebius). Per the Stocks list, the neocloud with a $49 billion backlog, $27 billion from Meta and up to $19.4 billion from Microsoft, first-quarter revenue up 684 percent, carrying a $300 year-end target. The purest lever on the Meta compute reversal above.
  • $VECO (Veeco). Per one Reddit teardown, the picks-and-shovels way to own the memory buildout without the memory-price risk, the tool-of-record maker for the laser annealing that HBM stacks need, at a roughly $3.5 billion cap with an Axcelis merger waiting only on China approval.
  • $SOFI. Per Invest with Henry, the fintech near $18.32 with members up 35 percent and 2026 guidance around $4.65 billion, reports July 29, and the stated buy zone is under $20.

Psychology. Watch what the crowd did with the same headline twice in one day. This morning it bought the war because Trump hinted at a deal. This afternoon he said the deal was dead, called Iran scum, sent oil ripping, and the market bought again, this time into a chip rotation and an IPO priced tomorrow. That is a tape that has decided the direction in advance and is reading every headline, good or bad, as a reason to stay long the thing it already wanted to own. The danger in that is not the war. It is that conviction this smooth stops checking itself. When both the ceasefire and the collapse of the ceasefire are bullish, you are no longer reacting to news, you are just rationalizing a position.

Now do the discipline part on yourself. The honest signal today was not the green screen, it was Samsung. Per the People list, it printed a record quarter and the stock fell, because the good news was already owned. Hold that next to your own SK Hynix or Nvidia position going into Friday and ask the uncomfortable version of the question. If the thing you are long printed a perfect number and still dropped, what were you actually being paid for. Per the Crypto list, Michael Saylor sold his coins for the first time and told everyone why, and the crowd's response was to call it a buy signal. Sometimes the person selling is not sending a message. Sometimes he just needs the cash, and the story you build around his sale is the one you will be holding when the level breaks.

item.title: Iran hit US bases overnight, the US struck back, and then one 1am Trump tweet that Iran wants a deal flipped the whole selloff green
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The market is so tired of this war it bought the dip before dawn, the Fed minutes said nobody wants to hike until 2027, and the entire tape now hinges on memory as SK Hynix lists Friday into a 7x oversubscribed book. Here is the open.
item.content:encoded:
The market is so tired of this war it bought the dip before dawn, the Fed minutes said nobody wants to hike until 2027, and the entire tape now hinges on memory as SK Hynix lists Friday into a 7x oversubscribed book. Here is the open.

The overnight tape was a war movie with a happy ending nobody quite believes. Iran fired missiles at US bases, the US hit back with a second round, and then a single pre-dawn line from Trump that Iran wants to negotiate erased the entire risk-off move. Underneath, the Fed took hikes off the table and the whole market reorganized itself around one question: did memory just bottom. Here is the open, then the six things that matter.

The open. Whippy but net green. Per the Market list, overnight the US launched a second, larger round of strikes on Iranian targets around the Strait of Hormuz while Iran fired missiles at US bases in Kuwait, Bahrain, and Qatar, and then at roughly 1:15am Trump said Iran called and wants a deal, which turned Nasdaq futures from deep red to green. Brent is still up about 2% above $75, and one trader summed up the mood as the market having priced the war in both directions.

The Fed gave the bulls cover. Per ClearValue Tax, the June FOMC minutes showed a unanimous hold with not a single official wanting a hike, the internal survey does not pencil a first cut until the second quarter of 2027, and after the release the odds of a July hike fell to about 30%. Memory is the fulcrum today, with the Kospi down 25% from its high even as SK Hynix lists Friday into a book 7x oversubscribed.

1. The war became a buy-the-dip in a single overnight tweet. This is the tell about where sentiment actually is. Per the Market list, a genuine escalation, Iran striking US bases and the US answering, got fully faded before the US open the moment Trump floated a deal, with traders openly saying the market stops going lower on bad news and the bottom is near. Per threadguy, this is the first war people got bored of, the tape has simply stopped trading it. That is either resilience or complacency, and the two look identical until they do not.

2. The Fed minutes said nobody wants to hike, and the first cut is a year away. The macro anchor is a Fed with no plan. Per ClearValue Tax, every participant backed holding in June, a few saw a case for raising but still voted to hold, and the dots split on the year with many seeing rates roughly where they are and many others seeing them higher. The honest read is that inflation is running hotter on tariffs, energy, and, notably, demand from the AI buildout, which the Fed itself flagged as showing no signs of slowing. A reactive Fed that will not hike but cannot cut is a green light with a short leash.

3. Memory is the whole market, and Friday is the referendum. Everything routes through one sector. Per the Financial Markets list, SK Hynix lists Friday with roughly a 48% operating margin against Micron's 26%, and per the People-to-follow list the deal raises about $24.5B, the second-largest US debut ever, trading when-issued Friday under SKHYV with Baillie Gifford and Coatue indicated up to $7B. Penguin Solutions $PENG ripped more than 20% on a memory guidance raise, now up 293% since Trump's March buy.

The bull case on Micron got concrete. Per Rick Orford and a detailed Reddit DCF, $MU trades at 9.2 times earnings with a data-center run rate over $100B, is practically sold out through 2027, has locked 16 strategic customer agreements worth $20B of recurring revenue, and models out anywhere from $900 to $1,550 fair value. Per the Stocks list, the chart is holding the lower bound of its uptrend channel with 971 the level that confirms the reversal. The bear is not fundamentals, it is commoditization. Per threadguy, the risk is China's CXMT selling cheap DRAM into the US so buyers like Apple are no longer pigeonholed into Micron and SanDisk. Friday decides which story the tape believes.

4. Nvidia is sitting on the line, and it is the cheapest it has been in years. The single most important chart bounced. Per the Market list, $NVDA bounced twice off its 200-day near $190, trades at 16 times 2027 earnings against an average target near $317, and per the Kalshi list the whole Magnificent Seven is now the cheapest it has been versus the S&P in over a decade, with tech insiders buying at a record pace.

The bears are not gone. Per Stocks with Josh, the trend is still down with lower highs and lower lows, and he sees real support only at 172 and the range neckline at 165, guessing Michael Burry likely covered his short in that zone. Hold the 200-day and the cheapest Nvidia in years is a gift. Lose it and 165 is the next stop.

5. Bitcoin's bottom keeps building, and the next buyer is not Saylor. The structure improved again. Per the Crypto list, reactive speculators now hold just 26% of Bitcoin's realized cap, below the 40% line that has marked every major bottom, and Michael Saylor's record sale was absorbed with the coin barely dipping. Per Milk Road and Bitwise, the thesis is that Strategy is no longer the marginal buyer and the baton is passing to institutions, with Vanguard's $11T hiring of a head of digital assets read as the same one-way door BlackRock walked through.

The skeptics have a point too. Per the Crypto list, Peter Schiff is hammering that the biggest buyer became a seller and the coattail trade flipped, while Lyn Alden's base case is no new highs this year but no new lows either. Bitcoin is chopping around $60,000 to $62,000, a contested bottom, not a confirmed turn.

6. Under all the bottom talk, the memecoin casino says the froth has not left. Respect this counter-signal. Per threadguy, two memecoins on the new Robinhood Chain, Cash Cat and Anom, ran to nine-figure market caps in a week after Vlad Tenev followed the account, all while real on-chain volume sits at all-time lows. Per Capital Flows, the money that is not gambling is chasing Hyperliquid treasuries like $PURR instead. Nine-figure gambling on dead-quiet volume is the sound a market makes late, not early.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $SKHY (SK Hynix). The Nasdaq debut prices Friday, 7x oversubscribed and the second-largest US listing ever, trading when-issued under SKHYV with 2x-long $SKHX and 1x-short $SKHZ ETFs following Monday. The purest read on whether the memory trade bottomed, and the cleanest way to trade it besides Micron.
  • $PENG (Penguin Solutions). Per the Market list, a small-cap memory name up more than 20% on a roughly $100M guidance raise and 293% since March. High beta on the same theme, so size it like one.
  • $GE (GE Aerospace). Per one Reddit teardown, a 45% ROE quality business whose revenue grew 24.7% while EPS fell 1.8%, trading at nearly 51 times forward earnings against a six-model fair value of $119 that pegs it 67% overpriced. A great company at a price with no floor if the earnings dip is not transitory.
  • $ETN (Eaton). Per Rick Orford, the electrical-equipment maker framed as the AI-power analog to Nvidia, sitting inside the electrification and data-center demand mismatch. A power-infrastructure compounder, though valuation still has to work.
  • $PFE (Pfizer). Per Stocks with Josh, the stated buy is $23 for a 6 to 7% yield, with August 4 earnings and a history of running up about 20 days prior, which points at roughly July 16.

Psychology. Look at what the crowd decided overnight. Iran hit American bases, the US struck back, and the market bought the dip before sunrise on a single tweet that a deal might happen, because it is, in one trader's words, bored of the war. At the same time memory fell 25% in Korea and the response is to call a bottom, pile into a 7x-oversubscribed IPO, and buy Nvidia because it is the cheapest since 2019, all while insiders buy at a record pace. Everyone wants this to be the bottom, and that unanimity is the actual risk. When war is a buy, every dip is a floor, and the IPO buyers and the insiders and the bottom-callers are all leaning the same way, the consensus that it is over becomes the crowded trade.

Now hold the counter-signal next to it. Per threadguy, two memecoins ran to nine figures on record-low volume this week, and that is late-cycle gambling energy, not the fear you get at a real bottom. And per the Crypto list, Bitcoin is doing the honest version of a bottom, the fast money gone, Saylor finished buying, everyone waiting for $40,000, and it simply refuses to break. The discipline today is to notice that the loudest conviction is on the long side of the same three trades, and that the asset with no fans left is the one quietly holding. Feelings are cheapest exactly where nobody wants to feel them.

item.title: The chips broke, a war reignited, and the crowd answered by shoving a record five billion dollars into semiconductor ETFs to buy the dip
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Nvidia lost its 50-day and Bitcoin fell to 62,000 as oil pushed past 80 on the Iran strikes, yet fund-manager cash sits at a 20-year low and every analyst in the room is doubling down. Under the crypto panic, whales just made the biggest accumulation in history. Here is the recap.
item.content:encoded:
Nvidia lost its 50-day and Bitcoin fell to 62,000 as oil pushed past 80 on the Iran strikes, yet fund-manager cash sits at a 20-year low and every analyst in the room is doubling down. Under the crypto panic, whales just made the biggest accumulation in history. Here is the recap.

Today the market had every reason to be afraid and refused. A war reignited, oil spiked, semis broke their trend, and the response was not to sell but to pile in harder. That reflex, buying every dip with no cash left over, is the whole story tonight. Under it, in the asset everyone has given up on, the smart money did the opposite. Here is the close, then the six things that matter.

The close. Risk-off, and it was the war, not the Fed. Per the Market list, the Nasdaq closed down about 1% with the $VIX up hard and millions of $QQQ puts on the tape, driven by the overnight strike on 80-plus Iranian targets and Trump declaring the ceasefire over. Per the Financial Markets list, Brent pushed past $80 and WTI is up roughly $7 in two days as Russia banned diesel exports, while the 10-year yield tore 20 basis points higher this month to 4.6%, a quiet bond rout under the oil headline.

The Fed minutes came out this afternoon and the tape barely noticed. The rate path is the real puzzle: per the People-to-follow list, a soft June jobs print of 57,000 had cut July hike odds toward 18%, but the oil shock is now pulling them back the other way.

1. The dip-buyers showed up in size, and that is the debate. The most important number today was not a price, it was a flow. Per the Crypto list, the $SOXX semiconductor ETF took in $5.4B in a single day, four times its old record, with $7.1B flooding into long semi ETFs to buy a group that had fallen 16% in two weeks.

The chorus is loud to match. Per Milk Road, all five of its analysts are buying the dip, with Melvin doubling down on $NBIS and $CRWV and adding $MRVL, and per the Stocks list, fund-manager cash sits at its lowest in more than 20 years. Everyone is already long the bounce, and that is exactly the condition Joseph Hogue warns about: per Hogue, the Dalbar data shows the average investor earns 7% against the S&P's 10.6% precisely by chasing and panicking, a gap worth $1.3M over 30 years.

2. Nvidia is cheap now, and it is still the fulcrum of everything. The one name that decides the tape got cheaper and weaker at once. Per the Stocks list, $NVDA lost its 50-day average alongside the whole chip complex, but it trades below 20 times earnings, the cheapest since 2019 and a steep discount to Broadcom's 53 times, and it caught a bid after China cleared Alibaba, ByteDance, and DeepSeek to buy roughly 200,000 H200 chips.

The bull catalyst is concrete. Per Rick Orford, Anthropic is now running Claude on Nvidia's GB300 Blackwell through Microsoft Azure, a full hardware-cloud-model agent stack, and 49 analysts rate it a strong buy. The tension is the whole market in one chart: the best fundamental story in tech, sitting on a broken 50-day, into a war.

3. Memory bifurcated, and Micron is the only one still standing. The best trade of the year is splitting in two. Per the Stocks list, $MU was the only name in the complex to hold its 50-day, needing 971 to confirm a reversal, while $SNDK, $WDC, $STX, and $INTC all lost theirs.

The Samsung tell is the one to sit with. Per Prof G Markets, Samsung posted a blowout with revenue more than doubling and profit up 1,800%, and the stock fell 7% and dragged the market, because investors decided it had already gone too far. Michael Burry is short Micron betting on a 30% drop, and memory was the single best group in the market in the first half, up 276% against 49% for the rest of semis. When the best fundamentals produce a selloff, the price already knew.

4. Bitcoin capitulated into the biggest whale buy in its history. The crypto panic hid the real signal. Per Lark Davis, Bitcoin fell to $61,600 on the Iran escalation and is trying to hold wedge support, and per the Crypto list sentiment is flatly dire, with per 0xResearch 25 of 28 crypto sectors closing red on the day.

But look at who was on the other side. Per Lark Davis, whales accumulated 270,000 Bitcoin in late June, the largest single accumulation spike ever, larger than the COVID or FTX bottoms, buying near $59,000 while forced sellers redeemed a record $4.5B out of the ETFs, the worst month ever per Lark Davis. The counterweight to respect, per Benjamin Cowen, is that this is a countertrend window of strength in a midterm year, a rally into late July before a probable lower low into a Q4 bottom. Forced sellers meeting patient whales is how bottoms are built, but the calendar says not yet.

5. The rotation already has names, and they are AI dressed as something else. The money leaving crowded chips is finding cheaper AI exposure. Per Milk Road, Galaxy Digital $GLXY near $25 owns a data-center business, Helios, worth roughly $38 a share on its own through a 526-megawatt CoreWeave deal worth over $1B a year, which means the market is pricing its five other businesses below zero.

Per a second Milk Road note, Nebius $NBIS carries a $300 year-end target on a $49B backlog from Meta and Microsoft, growing revenue 684% while owning its full stack instead of renting and marking up. Per Milk Road, the same rotation has lifted $LLY, $HOOD, and software since June 25 as memory and the neoclouds bled.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $NBIS (Nebius). Per Milk Road, a $300 year-end target on Q1 revenue up 684% to $399M, a $49B backlog, and a guided $7 to $9B annualized run rate, the neocloud that owns its own stack. The risk is the same capex-versus-cash question hanging over the whole group.
  • $PLTR (Palantir). Per Rick Orford, near $130 with an average target of $194 and a BofA high of $255 on 72% revenue growth, but priced at 142 times earnings, so the August 3 earnings are the whole trade. Above 40% growth and a raise is the bull, a drift to 30% is the bear.
  • $GLXY (Galaxy Digital). Per Milk Road, a sum-of-parts where the Helios data-center business alone covers the price and a loan refinancing from 9% toward 7% would roughly double free cash flow to equity. The catalyst is the first reported Helios rent next earnings.
  • $COIN (Coinbase). Per Milk Road, the agentic-payments play that owns stablecoins, the Base chain, and an agent marketplace, framed as the pick-and-shovel if machines start transacting.
  • $TER (Teradyne). Per Milk Road, a chip-equipment name down about 30% that one analyst is buying into the semis washout, a cyclical way to play a memory bottom without the single-name risk.

Psychology. Watch what the crowd actually did today. The chips broke their trend, a war reignited, oil ran past $80, and the answer was to shove a record $5.4B into semiconductor ETFs, per the Crypto list, with fund-manager cash at a 20-year low, per the Stocks list. That is not fear, it is total conviction that every dip is a gift, and it is the most expensive belief there is right after the best trade in the market cracks. When everyone is already fully long the bounce and holding no cash, there is nobody left to buy it higher.

Now look at Bitcoin for the clean mirror. Per Lark Davis, forced sellers just dumped a record amount out of the ETFs straight into the single largest whale accumulation in history, 270,000 coins bought near $59,000 while the crowd called it dead. One tape is euphoric dip-buying into a broken chart with no dry powder left, the other is patient whales quietly eating a panic. Per Joseph Hogue, the question that separates the two is the only one that matters tonight: if you did not already own it, would you buy it here, and do you have anything left to buy it with. The edge is never where the crowd has already spent all its cash.

item.title: The US bombed Iran overnight and oil jumped 5%, so the complacency that ran the tape for a week gets its first real test at the open
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: A strike on 80-plus targets after Iran hit ships in Hormuz put a war premium back in crude just as the FOMC minutes land, and underneath it three separate analysts say Google, Oracle, and SpaceX all fail a cash-flow test. Nvidia's 200-day is the line that decides the rest. Here is the open.
item.content:encoded:
A strike on 80-plus targets after Iran hit ships in Hormuz put a war premium back in crude just as the FOMC minutes land, and underneath it three separate analysts say Google, Oracle, and SpaceX all fail a cash-flow test. Nvidia's 200-day is the line that decides the rest. Here is the open.

Two things happened while you slept. The US struck Iran and oil spiked, and the calm that let the market shrug off a memory crash for a week suddenly has a reason to crack. Under the geopolitics, the real story is quieter and more dangerous: the bill for the AI buildout is coming due, and the DCF models are starting to name names. Here is the open, then the six things that matter.

The open. Risk-off, and geopolitics is driving. Per the Market list, the US struck more than 80 targets in Iran overnight after Iran attacked three commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, WTI is up about 5%, and the S&P closed down 0.5% with the $SMH semis off 4% into yesterday's bell. Per the Financial Markets list, the Treasury revoked Iran's oil-export license and LNG transit through Hormuz has halted, with one trader calling for oil to see $80 before $60.

The FOMC minutes land this afternoon into a genuine paradox. Per the Financial Markets list, futures now price the Fed hiking in 2026 even as financial conditions sit at their loosest in a decade, and per the Market list, this was already the worst four-day start to a July for the Nasdaq since 2009. SK Hynix lists Friday, oversubscribed.

1. The capex bill is coming due, and the models are naming names. This is the structural story under the war headline. Three independent cash-flow teardowns landed on the same day, all saying the same thing. Per one Google DCF, Alphabet's operating cash flow soared from $125B to $165B while free cash flow barely moved off $73B, because capex nearly doubled to $91B and guides to $175B or more in 2026, and every single valuation scenario lands below today's price.

Per an Oracle breakdown, $ORCL generated $32B of operating cash and still posted negative $23.7B of free cash flow on data-center spending, and the stock is already down 45% and lower in 19 of the last 24 days. Per a SpaceX short thesis, $SPCX trades near 400 times EBITDA with its balance sheet being poured into hastily built AI data centers. The disease is the same from mega-cap to fresh IPO: the cash goes in and does not come back out.

2. Nvidia's 200-day average is the line that decides the whole tape. Everything hinges on one chart. Per the Market list, $NVDA is sitting right on its 200-day moving average, the level every desk is watching as the hold-or-break for the entire AI trade, and per Stocks with Josh, Michael Burry has increased his short near $198 while the stock grinds out a clean downtrend.

The bull has one strong card left. Per Stocks with Josh, Peter Thiel points out that Nvidia is the only name in this whole complex actually making money, against debt-fueled peers like OpenAI and Anthropic still heading for IPOs, and per the Crypto list Nvidia closed green yesterday even as the sector bled. Hold the 200-day and the rotation was healthy. Lose it and the capex bears get their catalyst.

3. Memory tried to reverse, and the pros cannot agree if it is real. The most crowded trade of the year is the one in question. Per the Stocks list, $MU bounced about 2% overnight as "the Koreans bought the dip," but this is a name that just crashed 25% in two weeks from $1,259 to $938 after being the single best performer in the S&P.

The framing that matters, per the Market list, is Morgan Stanley calling this a peak in the rate of change rather than the end of the cycle, still modeling 35 to 40% earnings growth into 2027 and preferring DRAM over NAND. Per the Financial Markets list, Micron just did $41B of revenue at 85% margins with HBM4 approved for Nvidia's Vera Rubin, so the fundamentals are not the question. The chart is. Watch the reclaim of 966 against support near 810.

4. The pick-and-shovel on the whole buildout is grid power. If the capex is real, the bottleneck is electricity. Per one small-cap thesis, Tecogen $TGEN makes natural-gas-powered chillers now being designed into AI data centers precisely because they do not draw grid power, freeing every saved watt for compute and speeding permitting, and Vertiv is already featuring and installing them. At a $120M market cap with more than a gigawatt showcased, it is a lottery ticket on the exact constraint everyone else is spending into. Per the Polymarket list, AI data-center demand just pushed commercial electricity use past residential for the first time.

5. Bitcoin is basing while everyone waits to buy it 20% lower. The setup keeps quietly improving under the gloom. Per Jesse Eckel, Bitcoin is back to $64,000 after the scare to $57,000, the zone held through the Saylor sale and the Fed fear, and his read is a possible lower low into October before the cycle flips higher. Per the Crypto list, the mood is grim, with Novogratz saying Saylor selling is not a good look and Lyn Alden saying nothing is coming to save Bitcoin, it survives on its own merits. That is what capitulation sounds like, and 17% of all supply has now sat unmoved for ten years.

6. Robotics is the cleanest place the rotation money is looking. The trade leaving crowded chips wants a new story. Per Miles Deutscher, the robotics map runs from the $BOTZ and $ROBO ETFs through Tesla, where Optimus is roughly 80% of the bull case, to the pure-play picks, and the one he details is Ouster $OUST, a lidar maker near $26.80 doing record revenue up 55% as rival Luminar went bankrupt in December. Per the Polymarket list, China alone will build more than 100,000 humanoids this year.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $TGEN (Tecogen). Per the thesis, the gas-chiller play on data-center grid constraints, $120M cap, backed by a Vertiv storefront and a growing backlog. Binary and tiny, so size it like the option it is, watching for the first disclosed data-center order.
  • $OUST (Ouster). Per Miles Deutscher, record product revenue up 55%, $175M cash and no debt, plugged into Nvidia's Drive platform, with a DCF near $46 against a high-$20s price. The lidar survivor after Luminar's bankruptcy.
  • $PFE (Pfizer). Per Stocks with Josh, oversold below its pre-COVID price at a 6 to 7% yield, with every prior sub-30 RSI cross rebounding to 60-70 and earnings August 4 that historically front-run by 20 days. The stated buy is $23.
  • $ORCL (Oracle). Per the breakdown, a real battleground: negative free cash flow and a stretched balance sheet against 37% earnings growth at a 0.64 PEG, down 45% and, per Capital Flows, sitting on its 200-week average as the most asymmetric setup in the AI cycle.
  • $SKHY (SK Hynix). The Nasdaq debut lands Friday and is already oversubscribed, the largest ADR listing on record. A cleaner memory vehicle than single-name Micron into a tape that cannot decide if the sector bottomed.

Psychology. Look at what just happened to the crowd's favorite trade. Micron ran 214% to become the single best performer in the S&P this year, and then dropped 25% in two weeks, and this morning, per the Stocks list, the dip-buyers are already stepping back in on one reversal candle. That is the exact reflex that gets punished after a parabola, buying the first green bar because the name made you money on the way up.

Meanwhile Michael Burry is adding to his Nvidia short and three separate analysts just showed the cash flow does not support the prices, and now a war overnight is testing whether the calm was ever real. When the most crowded winner cracks, the 200-day on Nvidia is the line, not your hope that the dip is over.

Now watch Bitcoin do the opposite. Per the Crypto list, Saylor is selling, Novogratz is negative, and Lyn Alden says nothing is coming to save it, and it is holding $64,000 anyway while 17% of supply refuses to move. The two tapes are mirror images. One is a beloved winner everyone is rushing to re-buy on a candle, the other is a hated asset everyone is waiting to buy 20% lower and quietly missing. The discipline today is to distrust your own relief on the semis bounce and to respect the bid under the thing nobody wants. Feelings are loudest exactly where they are most expensive.

item.title: The memory trade had its worst day of the year and the fear gauge did not even blink, and that calm is the scariest part of the session
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: SanDisk fell 14%, Micron broke below 900, and the entire chip complex bled out, yet the VIX sat at 16 while healthcare printed a record high. This was a rotation, not a panic, and the calm on a red day is the thing to watch. Here is the recap.
item.content:encoded:
SanDisk fell 14%, Micron broke below 900, and the entire chip complex bled out, yet the VIX sat at 16 while healthcare printed a record high. This was a rotation, not a panic, and the calm on a red day is the thing to watch. Here is the recap.

Today answered a question the morning posed. When the memory trade finally broke, would the market break with it? It did not. The chips had their worst day of the year and the money simply walked across the street into healthcare and the mega-cap laggards, without a flicker of fear on the tape. That calm is either the healthiest thing you will see all year or the setup for the worst. Here is the close, then the six things that matter.

The close. A memory-led bloodbath that the broad market shrugged off. Per the Market list, $SNDK fell 14%, $AMD and $MRVL dropped 9 to 10%, and the whole SOX complex bled while $MU broke below $900, with per the AM tape memory the worst corner of the S&P on the month ($WDC down 33%, $SNDK 30%, $MU 26%). The Nasdaq closed down about 1% and roughly $200B came off US market cap.

And yet the fear never showed. The $VIX sat near 16, healthcare $XLV printed a fresh all-time high, $AAPL closed within half a percent of its own record neck and neck with $NVDA for the largest company on earth, and $MSFT pushed toward $400. Per the Financial Markets list, the NY Fed's one-year inflation expectation jumped to 3.7%, the highest since September 2023, right before tomorrow's FOMC minutes.

1. The memory bloodbath is either a top or a rotation, and the pros are betting rotation. The bull and bear cases collided on the same tape. Per the Stocks list, James Thorne called it plainly, the AI trade is not over but the easy trade in it is, so sell the parabolic crowding in the chips and buy the fear elsewhere, while Jim Cramer said flatly there is no top here, just a brutal rotation.

The bull rebuttal is that the fundamentals never wobbled. Per the AM tape, Samsung's memory is sold out through 2027 even after its stock fell 7%, and SK Hynix lists on the Nasdaq Friday raising $28B as the maker of roughly 70% of Nvidia's next-generation memory. So the question under every red candle today is whether you are watching a supply glut form or a great trade simply getting un-crowded.

2. The market refused to flinch, and that calm is the real signal. Read the non-reaction, not the drop. A 1% Nasdaq decline and $200B of losses produced a $VIX of 16 and, per the Kalshi list, a tape where 60% of tech stocks are already in a bear market while the index barely moved because the money rotated instead of fleeing.

That is the same complacency this morning's record-low put/call skew was screaming about, now confirmed in live color. Per the People list, the four biggest AI spenders are on pace for $710B of capex in 2026 and only Google has beaten the S&P this year, and that is why one desk framed the real risk as the law of very large numbers cooling the AI trade without ever needing a crash.

3. The macro turned cautious and hawkish in the same breath. This is the uncomfortable part. Per Milk Road, its macro index just flipped from risk-on to caution and cut recommended risk exposure from 100% to 75%, driven by a real-economy score of negative 0.87 after last week's payrolls shock, with the Atlanta Fed now tracking Q2 growth at just 1.2%.

The growth scare would normally mean rate cuts are coming. The inflation print says the opposite. Per the Financial Markets list, inflation expectations are climbing across every horizon just as growth rolls over, a stagflation-tinged setup that lands straight into tomorrow's FOMC minutes and then the July 14 gauntlet of core CPI plus the big-bank earnings.

4. SpaceX joined the Nasdaq 100 today, and the mechanics behind it are uglier than the pop. The index inclusion is not the clean win it looks like. Per Prof G Markets, Simplify's Michael Green laid out how $SPCX got in under brand-new fast-track rules that cut the required trading history to 15 days and removed the float requirement entirely, so tens of millions of index investors now own it through funds like $QQQ even though the S&P looked at the same company and said no.

Green's charge is blunt, that the real winners are insiders getting liquidity while lockups expire over the next few months, and that three-quarters of the value sits in a money-losing AI business he says should make it "XAI, not SpaceX." Shares still fell about 5% today despite a Raymond James street-high $800 target, per the Kalshi list. The buy-the-rumor, sell-the-news warning from this morning played out on day one.

5. Bitcoin based while Saylor sold the low. The most important crypto tell was what did not happen. Per threadguy and Lark Davis, Michael Saylor's $216M sale executed right into the $60,000 wick that marked the local bottom, and per the Crypto list Bitcoin has now held above $60,000 for 151 straight days through the Iran war and a record ETF outflow, with ETFs buying $265M yesterday, more than Saylor sold.

The strongest evidence is on-chain. Per On-Chain Mind, short-term holders have fallen to about 26% of realized cap, a level that has coincided with nearly every major Bitcoin bottom in history, with the fast money flushed out and the average coin aging back to four years. The counterweight to respect, per Benjamin Cowen, is the clean 2018 fractal that says June's $57,000 sweep could still precede one more leg down, exactly as $5,700 did eight years ago. Per 0xResearch, the risk money is already rotating ahead of Bitcoin, with Solana's ecosystem up 6% on the day while Bitcoin sat flat.

6. The next rotation already has a name, and it is robotics. Follow where the parabolic-chip money wants to go next. Per Milk Road, Morgan Stanley now models a $7.5T humanoid market by 2050, with humanoid revenue climbing from about $5B this year toward $30B in 2030, and the cleanest picks named as Nvidia's Isaac and Jetson stack, Micron for the roughly 10x memory a humanoid carries, and Agility Robotics, which relists from the $CCXI shell as $AGLT with its Digit robot already in nine customers including Toyota. China already installs more than 80% of the world's humanoids through Unitree and UBTech. It is early and expensive, but this is the story the rotation crowd is reaching for.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $VKTX (Viking Therapeutics). Per Rick Orford, the weight-loss biotech ran from $28 to $38 in a month on buyout hopes and the new Medicare GLP-1 demo, with a Metacrine-style deal implying roughly $86 a share, but Eli Lilly's already-approved pill leaves Viking years behind. A small, respect-not-love position, a coin flip on data or a takeover.
  • $WDC (Western Digital). Per Rick Orford, the storage side is the bull counter to today's memory carnage, up 940% in a year on AI data demand with cloud at 89% of revenue and the stock at just 8x earnings, though it now trades above the average analyst target.
  • $MRVL (Marvell). Per Chip Stock Investor, Jensen Huang called it the next trillion-dollar company, but a reverse DCF says the stock already prices 47% profit growth every year for five years, so the business is great and the valuation is a pass.
  • $HOOD (Robinhood). Per Milk Road, the "Amazon Prime of finance" thesis rests on 28M funded accounts, an ARPU near $160 against rivals at $600 to $1,400, and prediction-market volume that spiked to $15 to $20B a day on the World Cup. Watch net deposit growth as the tell.
  • $SKHY (SK Hynix). The Nasdaq debut lands Friday July 10, the largest ADR listing on record by proceeds, and per Ticker Symbol YOU, its HBM is sold out into 2027 with the chairman warning the shortage could stretch to 2030.

Psychology. Sit with how strange today was. The chips had their worst day of the year, $SNDK fell 14%, and the fear gauge did not move. Per the Market list, that is a market so certain of the dip-buy that it will not pay up for protection even while a whole sector bleeds. The rotation crowd is calling it opportunity, Trump spent the day mocking Michael Burry by name, and the temptation is to feel smart for buying the semis dip because everyone around you is calm. Calm on a red day is not safety. It is the absence of the fear that usually marks the actual bottom.

Now hold the mirror up to Bitcoin. Saylor sold $216M into the low, the single scariest headline a holder could get, and the coin held while the short-term money quietly left and the long-term money accumulated. Per Lark Davis, the ETFs bought more than Saylor sold. One tape is complacent into a selloff, the other is fearful into strength, and your job is to not confuse the two. Do not revenge-buy the semis dip because the VIX told you it was safe, and do not stay scared out of the asset that just ate its worst news and refused to break. Per Scott Redler on the Financial Markets list, measure your commitment at the trickier moments, because that is the only place the size of your conviction actually gets tested.

item.title: The Dow printed 53,000, crash insurance has never been cheaper, and the memory trade cracked overnight on the one thing nobody was watching
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Samsung posted a 19x profit and still missed, dragging the whole memory complex down, while the S&P put/call skew hit a record low that says every hedge has been sold. The surface is a melt-up. The plumbing is getting nervous. Here is the open.
item.content:encoded:
Samsung posted a 19x profit and still missed, dragging the whole memory complex down, while the S&P put/call skew hit a record low that says every hedge has been sold. The surface is a melt-up. The plumbing is getting nervous. Here is the open.

The tape looks invincible and the positioning looks insane, and those two facts are the whole morning. The Dow closed above 53,000 for the first time ever, and at the same moment the cost of protecting against a crash fell to the lowest reading anyone has on record. One layer under the melt-up, the memory trade just took its first real hit. Here is the open, then the five things that matter.

The open. Watch the memory complex at the bell. Per the Stocks list, Samsung posted second-quarter operating profit up roughly nineteen times year over year to about $58B and still missed on revenue, sending the stock down 7.8% and dragging $MU, $SNDK, and $WDC lower after the close, with the Kospi down 5.7% and program selling halted per the Kalshi list. $SOXL fell more than 20 points overnight and $NVDA is sitting lifeless on its 200-day average.

Against that, $SPCX enters the Nasdaq 100 at the open, forcing index buying at roughly a 0.60% weight. Bitcoin reclaimed $64,000 after the White House confirmed a US strategic Bitcoin reserve, per the Polymarket list. Gold is still negative on the year, Iran reportedly fired missiles at ships in the Strait of Hormuz overnight, and the FOMC minutes land tomorrow.

1. The memory trade cracked on a profit beat, and the reaction is the warning. Samsung made a fortune and the stock fell anyway. Per the Stocks list, the revenue miss underneath the 19x profit surge is what spooked the tape, and the sell-side split in real time, with JPMorgan telling clients to buy the semiconductor dip while Morgan Stanley called it a momentum unwind and said rotate into the hyperscalers, $MSFT, $AMZN, and $META, over the chips.

The bear case got a specific spine overnight. Per one detailed teardown, the next memory node, HBM4, is a manufacturing nightmare, with 14 to 16 stacked layers warping under 260-degree heat and pushing yields below the 65% break-even, so real volume production likely slips to 2027 or 2028 rather than late this year, and only Samsung has cleared the qualification bar. The kicker: Micron may get zero initial HBM4 allocation from Nvidia, even as Micron itself says it has no line of sight on supply catching demand before 2028.

2. The hedges are gone at the exact top of the range. This is the number that should stop you. Per Thierry von Arvy, the S&P put/call skew just collapsed to 0.71, the lowest reading on record, against a ten-year average of 12 and a 2020-panic peak of 34. Crash protection is essentially free because nobody wants it.

Stack the context and it gets louder. Per the Stocks list, the market sits at the 98th percentile of valuations since 1950 while the real economy sits at the 24th, a gap only crossed once before, in June 2001. Per Prof G Markets, Scott Galloway is calling real deja vu of 1999, when the front-end demand story collapsed and even Cisco lost 92% of its value, and now the entire AI capex bill leans on demand from just two players, Anthropic and OpenAI. Per one retail thread, ordinary investors are already quietly moving to CDs and booking profits.

3. SpaceX joins the Nasdaq 100 today, and it is both a bid and a trap. Mechanical buying is a real catalyst and a real setup. Per the People-to-follow list, $SPCX enters the index at the open as the 42nd-largest holding, and rebalance-arbitrage desks have already made a reported $3.7B positioning for it, with Cathie Wood adding $5.1M. Per Stocks with Josh, this is the textbook buy-the-rumor, sell-the-news event, and he only nibbles under $115 and accumulates from there toward $60 to $65, not into the inclusion pop. His broader call sets the clock: safe in July, and by around August 11 the downside story of midterms, inflation, and a SpaceX share unlock starts to bite.

4. Bitcoin took the worst headline it could and went up anyway. The most bullish thing a market can do is refuse to fall on bad news. Per the Crypto list, Michael Saylor sold 3,588 Bitcoin for $216M to fund his preferred dividends, and Bitcoin still ran from $58,000 to above $64,000 on the week, helped by the White House confirming a US strategic reserve. The forced-seller fear is now an open debate, with Lawrence Lepard arguing you cannot break Strategy unless Bitcoin itself breaks, and Novogratz saying Saylor has the cash and is not a forced seller even as his preferreds trade badly.

The skeptics are not gone. Per the Kalshi list, traders still put only a 15% chance on $100,000 this year and are actively pricing a crash toward $50,000, and per the habits letter, the line every desk is watching is $60,000 to $61,000, with the bull case invalid below $59,000 and the first upside target near $66,000.

5. Ethereum is quietly building the institutional case Bitcoin already has. The under-covered rotation is into ETH. Per Milk Road, Ethereum co-founder Joe Lubin and former BlackRock digital-assets lead Joseph Chalom laid out an institutional supercycle, with Lubin's Sharplink $SBET now the second-largest corporate ETH holder near $1.5B, its institutional ownership jumping from 6% to 48%, and a new $125M Galaxy on-chain yield fund built on overcollateralized ETH lending. Per the Financial Markets list, CredibleCrypto is already floating a $20,000 target, and per the Crypto list, Tron is out-earning every layer-one on fees. This is early, but it is the first time the ETH pitch sounds like the BTC one did two years ago.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $SKHY (SK Hynix). Per the Stocks list, the US listing prices Friday July 10 raising about $28B, with Aschenbrenner's fund, Coatue, and Baillie Gifford eyeing up to $7B, roughly a quarter of the deal. A cornerstone-backed memory play landing the same week the memory tape wobbled.
  • $MU (Micron). Per the Stocks list, the take-or-pay book covers 18 deals through 2030 with $22B of cash deposits and a BofA target of $1,550, but the chart just gapped and the HBM4 timing risk is now live. The gap-fill sits near $780, with near-term support flagged at 906 to 922.
  • $SPCX (SpaceX). Index inclusion today, but treat the pop as a place to trim, not chase, per the sell-the-news read above.
  • $RIVN (Rivian). Per the Stocks list, a 75-million-share offering, roughly $1.4B, to fund the equity match for a $4.5B Department of Energy loan, which erased the day's gains. Dilution now for a cheaper cost of capital later.
  • $VRTX / $CRNX (Vertex, Crinetics). Per the Stocks list, Vertex is acquiring Crinetics for about $10B in cash, a rare large biotech deal and a premium worth studying if you hold either.

Psychology. Sit with the contradiction the tape is handing you this morning. The Dow just printed 53,000, valuations are at the 98th percentile, and per Thierry von Arvy the price of crash insurance has fallen to the lowest reading in the history of the data. Everyone has sold their hedges at once. The bullish read, that melt-ups happen because people are underexposed and have to chase, is true. It is also exactly how the top of a range feels from the inside, right before it is not the top anymore. When protection is this cheap and this unwanted, that is usually the moment to quietly buy a little, not to add size naked into the rip.

Now look at Bitcoin for the opposite lesson. Saylor sold $216M, the exact headline every holder feared, and per the Crypto list the price went up. When an asset refuses to fall on its worst news, the fear is doing your position sizing for you and it is sizing you too small. The discipline today is to hold both truths at once. Do not chase the melt-up unhedged just because it feels safe, and do not stay scared out of the thing that just proved it can eat bad news. The tape is testing whether you react to how things feel or to what they actually do.

item.title: The tape was green every hour of the session, and underneath it the smartest money and the loudest bear were both quietly selling the leaders
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Stocks ran a clean trend day up on the first session back, but Aschenbrenner's fund is short the chips and long the power, Michael Burry says the end is near, and the money is rotating from the design layer into the stuff that keeps the lights on. AMD led, Micron faded, and Saylor sold Bitcoin into a shrug.
item.content:encoded:
Stocks ran a clean trend day up on the first session back, but Aschenbrenner's fund is short the chips and long the power, Michael Burry says the end is near, and the money is rotating from the design layer into the stuff that keeps the lights on. AMD led, Micron faded, and Saylor sold Bitcoin into a shrug.

Two tapes again, and today they sat right on top of each other. On the surface it was a picture-perfect melt-up, orderly and green into the close. One layer down, the people with the best track records spent the day rotating out of the exact names that led. Here is the recap, then the rotation nobody on the ticker was talking about.

The close. The first session back from the long weekend was a clean trend day up. Per the Financial Markets list, Linda Raschke called it a pure trend day out of a three-bar triangle with no reversal, the S&P held near 7520 into the bell, and the running joke was that the market has been green every day of July. Per the People-to-follow list, $AMD was the standout up more than 10% as shorts got liquidated and Lisa Su appeared at a Trump event, while ISM Services printed 54 with a firm 51.2 employment read, and the Fear and Greed index sits at a neutral 45.

$DELL ran 8% after Trump told people to go buy one and Michael Dell pledged billions to child accounts. SpaceX joins the Nasdaq 100 tonight under $SPCX, forcing more than $4B of passive buying at the close. The one date that matters this week is still the FOMC minutes on Wednesday July 8.

1. The smartest money spent the day rotating out of the chips and into the power. This is the story under the melt-up. Per Invest with Henry, Leopold Aschenbrenner's roughly $20B fund is running about $8.5B of downside against the crowded design layer, with put exposure to the $SMH semi ETF, $NVDA, $AVGO, $AMD, $MU, $TSM, and $ASML (the sole supplier of the EUV lithography every advanced node needs, per Rick Orford), while going long the deployment layer instead: Bloom Energy $BE, SanDisk $SNDK, CoreWeave $CRWV, and $IREN. It is not an anti-AI bet, it is a bet that the market overpays the chip designers and underpays the power, memory, and data-center slots that actually bind.

The institutional read agrees. Per Adam Parker, the former Morgan Stanley strategist, we are in the seventh inning on stock prices but only the third on fundamentals, and the tell he is watching is correlation: Eaton, GE Vernova, Vistra, Constellation, and even gold now track the AI-semis basket more tightly than Nvidia does. His advice is to pair semis with energy and healthcare, the two things that do not move as one when the AI trade wobbles.

2. Michael Burry stopped hedging his words and said the end is near. The loudest bear got louder. Per the Polymarket list and the Kalshi list, Michael Burry followed yesterday's $MU short by declaring the end is near for AI stocks and an imminent major collapse, and Morgan Stanley is now openly describing the AI trade as rotating away from the leaders with semis momentum fading. You do not have to trade on Burry, whose timing is famously early, but when the man who called the last blowup and the biggest sell-side desk are pointing at the same rotation the tape just ran, it is worth knowing which side of it you are on.

3. Memory gapped up at the open and then faded all day, and the calendar is why. The morning's melt-up did not hold in the one name it was built on. Per the Stocks list, $MU gapped up more than 100 points on the Sunday-night open and then lagged the rest of semis for the entire session, unable to reclaim its near-term moving average even as $AMD ripped. The overhang has a date on it. Per the People-to-follow list, SK Hynix filed to list in the US under $SKHY by the end of this week, raising around $28B in the largest US IPO marketing in memory, and Aschenbrenner's own fund is a cornerstone buyer, so the memory bid is rotating into the fresh paper. The offset is real demand: Micron signed a strategic supply agreement to put its memory in Ford's next-generation vehicles, and $MU is now the third-largest weight in the Nasdaq 100.

4. The money that left the chips showed up in power and the neoclouds. If you want to see where the rotation landed, follow the electricity. Per a Goldman map relayed by Milk Road, of the $7.6T headed into AI infrastructure through 2031, power is a small slice but the single gate that can stall everything downstream, which puts scarce-asset owners in the path of the capital. Per Rick Orford, GE Vernova $GEV is pricing new gas turbines 10 to 20% above last quarter into a book that is sold out to 2030, throwing off $4.8B of free cash in a single quarter against a $163B backlog.

And per Barchart and Yahoo Finance, Oklo $OKLO just won DOE safety approval for its test reactor and is building a 1.2 gigawatt Ohio campus to power Meta data centers, even after the stock fell 22% in June when the DOE floated a $17.5B loan program aimed at big traditional reactors instead of small modular ones.

The neoclouds, the high-beta version of the same trade, ripped today. Per the People-to-follow list, $IREN jumped 11% on a reported Anthropic data-center deal in Australia, $CRWV added 5%, and $WULF is carrying a roughly $19B twenty-year Anthropic lease. Own the picks and shovels if you want the theme with less blow-up risk than the levered cloud rentiers.

5. Broadcom is quietly building the chip that pressures Nvidia's whole story. One more crack in the design-layer thesis. Per Rick Orford, Broadcom $AVGO co-designed OpenAI's first custom inference chip, taped out in nine months and targeting gigawatt-scale deployments starting this year. That is a direct shot at Nvidia's pricing power on general-purpose GPUs. The catch is valuation. $AVGO trades north of 53 times earnings against Nvidia near 23, so the custom-silicon future is already partly in the price, and its revenue leans on a handful of giant buyers.

6. Saylor sold Bitcoin for the first time in four years, and the market shrugged it off. The overhang everyone feared arrived, and the tape absorbed it. Per Milk Road, Michael Saylor sold 3,588 Bitcoin for about $216M at an average near $60,197 to fund his preferred dividends, still holds around 850,000 coins, and has flagged room to sell up to $800M more, a known seller sitting above any rally. Yet per the Crypto list, the reaction was positive, with $MSTR and the $STRC preferred both firming and Bitcoin holding $61,000 to $62,000 into the close.

The bottom-callers are getting louder underneath the fear. Per Lark Davis, spot Bitcoin ETFs just flipped back to a $200M inflow, Bernstein notes the 54% drawdown from the $125K peak is far milder than the 75 to 90% declines of past cycles, and altcoin sell pressure has fallen to a multi-year low that usually marks capitulation. Per Jordi Visser, the two forces that crushed crypto, a Fed repricing from cuts to hikes and capital fleeing into AI infrastructure, are both close to exhaustion, and the level to watch is clean: $64,000 reclaimed turns the tape, $60,000 lost brings the $55,000 bid into play. Per 0xResearch, Bitcoin actually closed the day slightly ahead of both the Nasdaq and gold, and the money inside crypto is rotating toward the things that print cash, the exchange tokens and the prediction-market venues, rather than the high-beta majors.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $GEV (GE Vernova). Per Rick Orford, turbines sold out to 2030 with 10 to 20% pricing power and a backlog heading from $163B toward $200B. The risk is conversion, since a chunk of the pipeline is slot reservations on data centers that still need permits, fuel, and financing. A buy-on-dips name, not a chase after a double.
  • $OKLO (Oklo). Per Barchart, DOE safety approval in hand and a 1.2 gigawatt Meta campus in the plan, with a consensus target near $84 against a low-$50s price. Pre-revenue and policy-whipsawed, so size it as a call option on nuclear, not a core hold.
  • $CCXI (Agility Robotics). Per Milk Road, the first pure-play humanoid stock, a SPAC merging with Agility at a $2.5B valuation, whose Digit robot has logged 65,000 operating hours and $300M in binding orders with Amazon, Toyota, and Schaeffler backing it. It burned $100M in cash last year, so this is a multi-year venture bet in a public wrapper.
  • $PFE (Pfizer). Per Stocks with Josh, the same $23.50 entry from this morning, trading below its pre-COVID price at a 6 to 7% yield with the revenue-cliff fear priced in. Accumulate in a range, do not lump in.
  • $QBTS (D-Wave). Per Rick Orford, fifteen analysts rate it a strong buy with targets implying a possible double, helped by $100M of proposed government funding, though the valuation rests entirely on quantum becoming a real layer rather than on today's financials.

Psychology. Notice how good today felt. Green every hour, $AMD up double digits with the shorts carried out, SpaceX forcing four billion dollars of buying into the bell, the Fear and Greed needle sitting at a comfortable neutral. Everything about the tape invited you to relax and add. That is precisely the moment to look at who was selling into it. Per Invest with Henry, the sharpest young manager in the game is short the chips you want to chase, and per the Polymarket list, Michael Burry is telling anyone who will listen that the end is near. A green trend day does not make them right, but it is the exact backdrop where you are most tempted to oversize into the names they are fading.

Then look at Bitcoin for the mirror image. Saylor sold for the first time in four years, the single most bearish headline a Bitcoin holder could imagine, and per the Crypto list the market simply shrugged and held $61,000. When the worst-case news lands and the price refuses to break, that tells you something the fear does not. The discipline tonight is symmetrical. Do not let a flawless up day talk you into full size at the top of a rotation, and do not let a scary headline talk you out of a position that is quietly refusing to fall. The tape rewards the person who feels both pulls and sizes off the plan anyway.

item.title: Micron gapped up 7% before the bell and Michael Burry is short it, and the space between the invoice and the skeptic is the entire tape this morning
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The memory melt-up ran straight into a famous bear on the first session back from the holiday, the crypto side is bleeding out in silence, and three fresh names (Microsoft, AeroVironment, Veeva) showed up cheap. Here is what to watch into the open.
item.content:encoded:
The memory melt-up ran straight into a famous bear on the first session back from the holiday, the crypto side is bleeding out in silence, and three fresh names (Microsoft, AeroVironment, Veeva) showed up cheap. Here is what to watch into the open.

Two moods are fighting for the tape and they could not be further apart. Semis and memory are gapping up like the supercycle is a settled fact, while crypto is quietly down 50 to 87% from its highs and nobody is talking about it. The morning's job is to hold both in your head without letting either one size your book for you. Here is the open, then the six things that matter.

The open. Futures are green and the memory complex is doing the heavy lifting. Per the Market list, $MU is up about 7% overnight after breaking ground on a $9.3B HBM plant in Hiroshima (high-bandwidth memory, the stacked chips that feed AI accelerators) with $3.1B of Japanese government support, and $SOXX, $SMH, and $SNDK are all set to open firm. Keep yesterday's $SPY map: 755 is the breakout, 740 is the breakdown, per Stocks with Josh.

Three dates own the week. The FOMC minutes land Wednesday July 8 and get read for whether a hike is really coming, the SK Hynix US listing debuts Friday July 10 under $SKHY as a second pure memory play, and $AVAV holds an Investor Day Wednesday July 8. Gold is stalling just above $4,200 as traders scale back Fed hike bets, per the Financial Markets list, and oil is lower on an OPEC+ output increase.

1. The memory melt-up got an invoice and a short-seller in the same session. The bull case keeps getting more concrete. Per the Stocks list, Gavin Baker is now framing memory as the cheapest thing in the AI stack at 3 to 5x earnings, with only three HBM makers on earth and Micron's contracts covering about half its revenue at floor prices above the last cycle's peak margins. And per Lark Davis, Micron's own CEO Sanjay Mehrotra is stretching the runway further, arguing a humanoid robot carries ten times the memory of a self-driving car and that the real multi-decade demand cycle has not even started.

Then the other shoe: Michael Burry has disclosed a short on $MU, and separately, per the Polymarket list, he is calling the whole AI semiconductor complex a bubble about to pop. One live risk to file under the bull case, per the Financial Markets list: China's CXMT is testing next-generation bonded DRAM and closing the gap with Korea faster than expected. That is the one thing that could crack the three-supplier scarcity story.

2. Nvidia's Kyber slipped a year, and that repriced the whole optics supply chain. The under-owned story this morning is a rack delay. Per Melvin, Nvidia's Kyber NVL144 (the 144-chip successor to the current Blackwell rack that Jensen demoed at GTC three months ago) is now reportedly pushed more than 12 months to 2028 on co-packaged optics yield problems, and the NVL72x2 bridge rack was cancelled outright. The read-through: $AMD, $MRVL, and $AVGO all benefit as hyperscalers accelerate their own custom silicon when Nvidia's roadmap slips. The sharper single-name call is $AAOI, which the market treats as a co-packaged optics play but which actually makes most of its money on pluggable transceivers, and a Kyber delay keeps those pluggables bid for two more years.

3. The neoclouds that got crushed on Friday caught a bid overnight. The complex that had its worst two days since COVID is trying to bounce. Per the Stocks list, $NBIS is up about 4% overnight, and $IREN is being called the 2026 bottom on the back of a reported 5-gigawatt, 500,000-GPU Nvidia partnership that puts a $500 target in the conversation, with $CRWV trading near 2.85x forward sales. This is the same high-beta, balance-sheet-sensitive end of the AI trade that broke first on the Meta headline, and a two-day bounce does not fix the leverage. Own it on the flush, never with size into the rip.

4. Microsoft is the cheapest it has been in five years, and the drawdown is almost entirely multiple. A fresh name worth real attention. Per one detailed Reddit breakdown, $MSFT sits near $373 to $390 at a 19.3x forward multiple, roughly 40% below its three-year average, with the entire 33% drawdown coming from multiple compression rather than any business crack. The fundamentals are still elite: Azure grew 40%, the AI business is at a $37B annualized run rate up 123% year over year, operating margin is 46%, and return on equity is 34%.

Per a second write-up, a base-case DCF lands fair value around $580 to $600, though the honest risk is the roughly $190B of 2026 capex crushing free cash flow, plus a restructured OpenAI deal that ended Azure's model exclusivity. Earnings hit July 29.

5. Bitcoin's bottom call split cleanly in two overnight. You have two credible reads pointing opposite ways, and that split is the whole decision. Per Benjamin Cowen, the four-year cycle is replaying 2018 at exactly 10x the price, the local low of $57,000 already printed on July 1, and a July window of strength should carry Bitcoin back toward its 200-day average before it rolls over into a Q4 low. Against that, per Stocks with Josh, the bottom is not in, $69,000 is the level that invalidates the downtrend, and until price closes above it he wants to short the rejection there.

Under both calls the damage is already brutal. Per Wajahat Mughal, Bitcoin is down 50% from its high, Ether 64%, and the alts are a graveyard with $SOL down 72% and $AAVE down 87%. The long-term case has not changed, and per Jeff Park Bitcoin has still existed for only about 7% of the time the United States has, but that is cold comfort to anyone marked to market this morning.

6. Saylor sold Bitcoin for the first time in four years, and the preferred is where the stress shows. The MicroStrategy story turned genuinely two-sided. Per Lark Davis, $MSTR has fallen to an $82 two-year low even though it still holds 847,000 Bitcoin worth roughly $55 to $60B, and Saylor sold a token 32 coins, likely for compliance reasons, while authorizing a larger monetization program. The real pressure is in the $STRC preferred, which trades at $71 against a promised $100 as its dividend obligations quadrupled from $300M to $1.2B in six months and coverage runway collapsed from seven years to fourteen months.

The bull answer is Saylor's own math that those dividends survive 40 years even at zero Bitcoin appreciation, but Peter Schiff is calling it finished and JP Morgan now labels him a risk to Bitcoin itself.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $AVAV (AeroVironment). Per this earnings breakdown, Q4 revenue came in at $641.6M, up 133% year over year and well past consensus, adjusted EPS was $1.84 versus $1.47 expected, and the stock ripped about 35% in three sessions before landing a $500M firm Army contract for the Titan counter-drone system. Investor Day is Wednesday July 8, the first look at strategy after the BlueHalo merger. Consensus target sits near $258 against a roughly $191 price.
  • $SKHY (SK Hynix). The US listing debuts Friday July 10, a second pure memory play beside Micron into a tape that is already gapping the sector up. If you want memory exposure without single-name Micron risk, this is the new instrument to size.
  • $VEEV (Veeva Systems). Per this write-up, the life-sciences software vendor owns more than 80% of pharma CRM seats, carries zero debt and over $5B net cash, and has compounded free cash flow per share above 35% a year while the market has left it unloved. No catalyst named, a watch-and-wait, but a quality name going cheap.
  • $PFE (Pfizer). Per Stocks with Josh, a fresh swing idea with a stated entry near $23.50, trading below its pre-COVID price at a 6 to 7% dividend with the revenue-cliff fear largely priced in. A slow accumulate, not a lump-sum chase.
  • $SOFI. Per the People-to-follow list, the loan-platform take rate is running near 4.6% on about $12B annualized originations at roughly 15x 2028 earnings with 30%-plus member growth. Reports July 29.

Psychology. Look at what the tape is asking you to feel this morning. Memory and semis are gapping up, the timeline is full of people posting $1,800 and $3,000 targets on $MU and $SNDK, and the pull is to chase the green candle before the bell so you do not miss it. That is exactly the moment Michael Burry chose to disclose a short and call the whole AI-semi complex a bubble, and per the Crypto list even Pentosh1 admitted he did not buy the memory dip on Friday. When the crowd is euphoric and one of the few people who called the last blowup is fading it, the risk is not being wrong on the thesis. The risk is oversizing into strength because the strength feels safe.

Now look at the other screen. Crypto is down 50 to 87% across the board, Saylor is selling for the first time in four years, and per the Crypto list Novogratz is saying nobody cares about crypto anymore. That silence is the opposite emotion, the flat apathy that shows up near lows and makes you want to close the tab and stop looking.

The discipline today is to run both feelings through the same rule. Per Steve Burns, risk a fixed unit per trade and count your outcomes in R, not dollars, so a hot memory name and a bombed-out alt are the same size of bet and neither the euphoria nor the despair gets to move your hand. The people who blow up in a tape like this are not the ones who pick wrong. They are the ones who felt certain and let the feeling set the size.

item.title: Micron was the whole week, and by Sunday the trade had a famous bear, a structural bull, and a number under $1,000 to explain
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The AI memory trade ran through all eight editions this week: record fundamentals and a supplier now invoicing 20% price hikes on one side, Michael Burry's chart short and a crowded-buy flush on the other. Here is the full case, and what to actually do with it.
item.content:encoded:
The AI memory trade ran through all eight editions this week: record fundamentals and a supplier now invoicing 20% price hikes on one side, Michael Burry's chart short and a crowded-buy flush on the other. Here is the full case, and what to actually do with it.

Every trading day this week had memory at the center of it, and no other story came close. Micron opened the week getting flushed under $1,000, spent the middle of it absorbing a Michael Burry short, and closed it with a supplier literally invoicing a 20% price hike and a decade-scoreboard showing memory as the best-performing corner of the entire market. This is the one position the whole book keeps circling, so it earns the long form. The bull case and the bear case are both real, they are arguing about different things, and knowing which is which is the difference between owning this well and getting run over in it.

Why Micron is the week's subject and not just another semi in the AI basket. The tell is that the same name led the tape from two opposite directions. On Monday the memory air pocket showed up and $MU gave back 6.7% in a session. By Wednesday Micron had closed under $1,000 and every desk that bought the dip looked early. Then on Thursday Michael Burry opened a short and the entire complex wobbled, and by Friday the bulls had answered with a record print and Asia had voted with a green tape. A stock does not do that unless it is the fulcrum of the market's biggest live debate. It is.

The bull case, in full. Strip out the noise and the long side rests on four legs, and they are stronger than the price action this week suggested.

  • The supply is structurally short, and management keeps saying so on the record. CEO Sanjay Mehrotra told Bloomberg that Micron can meet only about 50% to two-thirds of key-customer demand right now, that the shortage runs well beyond 2026 with no meaningful new supply until 2028, and that the take-or-pay book, the contracts where the buyer pays whether or not it takes the chips, is up to 16 agreements with $GM freshly added and 14 of them carrying a $100B minimum-revenue floor. He is committing $200B to a US buildout on the back of it.
  • The margins are the kind you see once a cycle. The most recent quarter ran an 84.9% gross margin, and Wolfe Research pegs Micron's DRAM gross margins above 90%. The reason it is durable is physical: high-bandwidth memory eats four times the wafer capacity of standard DDR5, so adding wafers does not add bits, and the clean-room shortage caps supply well into 2027.
  • The valuation still prices a cyclical, not a monopoly. Fiscal Q3 revenue was $41.5B, up 346% year over year, with adjusted EPS of $25.11 against a $20.49 estimate, and the Q4 guide is $50B in revenue and $31 EPS. Against a CEO target of $4,000 the stock has traded near 6x forward earnings. That is a value multiple stapled to a hypergrowth print.
  • The forecast became an invoice. The single most important development of the week was that the thesis stopped being a thesis. Samsung began issuing verbal notices to raise DRAM prices roughly 20% quarter over quarter for Q3. A price hike you model is a hope. A price hike your supplier sends in writing is a fact, and it flows straight to Micron's line.

The smart-money vote backs the fundamentals. Martin Shkreli made the cleanest structural argument, that DRAM is not a commodity because it needs cleanroom fabs full of $ASML and Lam Research machines nobody can stand up on demand, so rational entrants match the incumbent price instead of starting a war, and memory behaves like a disciplined oligopoly. Leopold Aschenbrenner is voting with real weights, 14.9% of his book in $SNDK and more in the memory-and-power complex. And the 2026 scoreboard says they have been right all year: $SNDK up 635%, $MU up 242%, $WDC up 213%, $MRVL up 189%. Memory is not participating in this market, it is leading it.

The bear case, in full, and it is not what most people think. The mistake is treating the Burry short as a fundamental call, when it is nothing of the kind. Per the Market list, Burry's Substack short is pure technical analysis, Micron overextended from its 200-day average targeting roughly a 30% drawdown, with nothing about HBM or supply under it. That is a bet on gravity and positioning rather than on the business.

And on that narrow point he might be right, because the real bear case this week was crowding. This was, in the words of Tuesday's edition, the most crowded buy on the board, the rare moment when every independent desk piled onto the same trade at the same time, and crowds like that either front-run a move or mark a local top. The stock closing under $1,000 mid-week, then the concentrated flush that took $SNDK down 14.8% and $MRVL down 11.2% in a session, is what an overowned trade does when it exhales.

The two legitimate fundamental worries are about timing rather than thesis. The first is the 2028 air pocket, the point where the new supply Mehrotra is building finally lands and the shortage eases, a real risk to the out-years even if it does not bite in 2026. The second is macro. The Fed turned hawkish enough this week that a July hike went from unthinkable to a coin flip under Kevin Warsh, and Tom Lee mapped a September-to-October correction driven by the Anthropic and OpenAI IPO supply shock. A high-beta, heavily-owned name like Micron is exactly what gets sold first in that kind of drawdown, regardless of how good the invoice looks.

The verdict, and what to do with it. My conviction is that the bull case is the stronger one and the bear case is mostly about the entry, not the asset. When the supplier is invoicing the price hike, the CEO is turning away a third of his demand, and the stock trades at 6x forward, you are being paid to own the structural short in memory. But Burry is shorting the crowd, and a tape this one-sided can hand him his 30% on positioning alone before the fundamentals win. So the play is to own the core position through the noise and treat any Fed-driven or IPO-supply-driven flush as the place to add rather than the reason to exit.

  • Levels that matter. Stocks with Josh put a measured-move target of $1,500 on Micron, activating on a reclaim of $1,250, with a hard bounce expected first off the $895 fibonacci level. That $895-to-$1,250 zone is the accumulation range if the autumn correction Tom Lee flagged actually arrives.
  • The watchlist around it. The bottleneck arc runs compute to memory to connectivity, so the same trade lives in $MRVL on the connectivity leg, $SNDK and $WDC on the storage leg, and the neoclouds that rent the compute, $NBIS above all, which kept signing capacity all week. $INTC is the wild card, up 226% on the year as the only leading-edge US foundry with its 18A yield reportedly fixed.
  • The risk control. Size it like the high-beta position it is and never like a core holding you can forget about. The thing that breaks this trade is not a bad Micron quarter, it is a Warsh hike or the IPO air pocket dragging every crowded AI name down together. Keep dry powder for that, because that is when you want to be buying the invoice, not selling the shakeout.

item.title: The memory trade got its invoices this weekend, and on the very same tape the companies that rent out the compute had their worst two days since COVID
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: UBS put hard numbers on the DRAM price hikes and Micron locked five-year take-or-pay contracts through 2030, while the neoclouds fell 20 to 25% in two sessions on one Meta headline. The AI trade is splitting into the part you get paid to own and the part that just got dangerous.
item.content:encoded:
UBS put hard numbers on the DRAM price hikes and Micron locked five-year take-or-pay contracts through 2030, while the neoclouds fell 20 to 25% in two sessions on one Meta headline. The AI trade is splitting into the part you get paid to own and the part that just got dangerous.

Two things happened at once and they point in opposite directions. The memory supercycle stopped being a forecast and became a set of signed contracts and quoted price hikes. At the same moment, the equities built on renting AI compute went through the fastest drawdown in years on a single Meta headline. Owning the AI trade now means knowing which half you are in. Here is the week ahead and the split underneath it.

The week ahead. Markets reopen Monday July 6 after the long weekend. Three dates matter. The FOMC minutes land Wednesday July 8 and get read for whether a July hike is real.
